data.title: BBC knew about Scott Mills allegations almost a year ago
data.description:
The BBC has admitted it knew of allegations against Scott Mills almost a year ago but failed to investigate, The Telegraph can reveal.

The Radio 2 presenter’s BBC contract was terminated on Monday following allegations about his “personal conduct”.

It was reported that the sacking related to allegations of serious sexual offences committed by Mills against a teenage boy who was under 16 between 1997 and 2000.

But The Telegraph can reveal that a former BBC presenter contacted the corporation in May 2025 to say she had received information about alleged “inappropriate communications” involving Mills and asked whether the broadcaster was “ever aware of or involved in any related matters”.

Anna Brees, a freelance journalist, also asked the BBC whether it had ever received any “formal or informal complaints” about Mills “relating to safeguarding, inappropriate conduct or harassment” and whether it had ever conducted an internal investigation into him.

She did not receive a response. On Tuesday, the BBC admitted her information “should have been followed up and we should have asked further questions”.

In a statement to The Telegraph, the broadcaster added: “We apologise for this and will look into why this did not happen.”

There is no suggestion that the inquiries by Ms Brees related to the same alleged victim whose complaint to the police led to Mills being questioned under caution in 2018.

But the BBC faces questions about why it did nothing in response to a claim of wrongful behaviour by a star presenter who later had to be dismissed.

‘Inappropriate messages’

The emergence of the email sent by Ms Brees to the BBC press office shows that the corporation was made aware of unspecified allegations about Mills’s conduct more than 10 months ago.

Ms Brees, who presented news programmes for both the BBC and ITV from 2003-2011, said: “Someone who knew I used to work for the BBC volunteered some information about Scott Mills messaging someone inappropriately.

“It was a credible source and I was concerned so I decided to contact the BBC to make them aware of it and see if they came back with anything. I didn’t get a response.”

The BBC said in a statement: “We received a press query in 2025 which included limited information. This should have been followed up and we should have asked further questions. We apologise for this and will look into why this did not happen.

“More broadly, we would always urge anyone who has concerns or information to raise it with us.”

The BBC has been accused of responding too slowly – or not at all – to claims of inappropriate behaviour by stars including Jimmy Savile, Huw Edwards and Gregg Wallace.

It was reported on Tuesday night that the BBC knew about the allegation which led to Mills’s sacking, first reported by The Mirror.

The Sun on Sunday revealed that an investigation was being carried out into an unnamed radio presenter in March of that year, but BBC bosses chose not to take Mills off air.

A source told The Sun that Mills told bosses he had been questioned by police but they allowed him to carry on working while the investigation continued.

The BBC launched its investigation after the complaint was raised with the broadcaster again, The Sun reported.

Allegation made in 2016

The allegation which led to Mills’s sacking was made to Hampshire and Isle of Wight Constabulary in December 2016 and then passed to the Metropolitan Police.

Mills was interviewed under caution in July 2018 and a full file of evidence was passed to the Crown Prosecution Service (CPS) but the case was closed in May 2019 because of insufficient evidence.

The Telegraph understands that the alleged victim was unhappy with the CPS decision not to bring charges.

He requested a review of the decision, but it also concluded there was insufficient evidence.

On Tuesday the charity Neuroblastoma UK announced it had dropped Mills as a patron.

Mills – who had worked for the BBC since 1998 – will no longer host the Radio 2 breakfast show, which he took over from Zoe Ball last year.

The 53-year-old was last heard on air on March 25 and had been scheduled to return the next day, telling listeners that he would be “back tomorrow”.

In an internal email seen by The Sun, Rhodri Talfan Davies, the acting BBC director-general, told staff: “I hope you all understand that we are not able to share any more information.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Revolut vs Monzo vs Starling vs Chase: which is the best online bank?
data.description:
If you never intend to set foot in a bank branch and are well used to running your finances from your phone or tablet, you might consider one of the popular online-only banks next time you switch current accounts.

As many as 88pc of UK adults now use online or remote banking services, according to research from Finder. In the face of a rapidly disappearing presence on the high street, it’s hardly surprising that more of us are turning our backs on traditional banks and switching to new digital current account providers instead.

In fact, at the start of this year, an estimated 49pc of adults had opened an account with a digital bank, up from 40pc in 2025.

Here, Telegraph Money sizes up the big four – Revolut, Monzo, Starling and Chase – if you’re not sure where to start.

  • Revolut
  • Monzo
  • Starling
  • Chase
  • Is Monzo, Revolut, Starling or Chase best for travelling?
  • Best for saving
  • Best for everyday spending
  • The verdict

Revolut

This global money app currently boasts over 70 million customers globally, with more than 13 million in the UK, and offers a range of banking, saving and investment services.

Although Revolut launched in 2015, it only got its UK banking licence in July 2024, and received full approval in March 2026 after a protracted wait. It is now a fully fledged UK bank, with regulatory approval.

Its range of personal current accounts goes beyond the basics, and includes a freelancer (or side-hustle) Pro account and an under-18s account, in addition to the expected individual and joint accounts. It also offers banking services to businesses.

Personal account holders can choose between five plans, some of which you have to pay for monthly. They include: Standard (free), Plus (£3.99), Premium (£7.99), Metal (£14.99) and Ultra (£55 a month). The more expensive plans come with statement metal and platinum cards.

Monzo

Monzo is a fully fledged bank, having obtained its authorisation from the FCA back in 2017.

Its current account is available on four different price plans. They include: Monzo (fee-free), Extra (£3 a month), Perks (£7 a month) and Max (from £17 a month).

It offers children’s accounts for those aged between 6 and 15, as long as parents hold a Monzo account, where you can see their spending and customise controls to work for your child.

Starling

Starling Bank is another pretty well-established challenger bank, obtaining its banking licence in 2016.

It was also one of the first digital banks to sign up to the Current Account Switch Service, an industry-wide initiative that guarantees account switches in seven working days.

Unlike its rivals, Starling keeps it simple with one fee-free account – there are no paid plans to compare.

Chase

Another notable digital bank is Chase, which is owned by JP Morgan. Although it has branches across America, it’s only available via a mobile app in the UK.

The fee-free account boasts a numberless debit card for the security conscious, and pays 1pc cashback on everyday expenses – such as fuel and groceries – for the first 12 months of holding the account.

It also pays interest on the “spare change” from your spending, and has a linked savings account that pays 4.5pc AER for a limited time with its boosted rate.

Is Revolut, Monzo, Starling or Chase best for travelling?

If you regularly travel overseas or plan to use your card on holiday, you’ll want to be sure you don’t rack up additional fees for the privilege.

When it comes to overseas debit card payments, you can use a Monzo, Starling Bank or Chase card, wherever you like, free of charge and using the Mastercard exchange rate.

It’s a bit more complicated with Revolut. Between Monday and Friday you can spend, withdraw and exchange money in foreign currencies free of charge, but only up to a certain limit, which varies according to the plan you’re on. At the weekend, however, fees will be charged for those on the Standard or Plus plans.

Overseas cash withdrawals are also free with Starling Bank, wherever you are. You can get free withdrawals from Monzo as well, but they can be limited in the EEA if Monzo is not your main bank. If you’re travelling outside the EEA, the limits are £200 every 30 days if you have the standard account; £400 if you have Monzo Plus; and £600 for the pricier plans.

Again, the position with Revolut isn’t straightforward. Fees will be charged for withdrawing local currency from ATMs. However, you can avoid unexpected fees by exchanging money into local currency first.

Best for saving

The best available savings rate is from Revolut, and currently stands at up to 4pc. The catch is that to get it, you’ll need to sign up for its priciest Ultra plan, which will set you back £55 a month.

The best linked savings rate from a free account is 4.5pc from Chase, but this “boosted” rate only lasts for the first year, after which it reduces to 2.25pc. The best rate without a bonus period is 3.75pc from Starling Bank.

However, it’s important to note that wherever you bank, your current account is unlikely to offer you access to the most competitive savings rates. While it’s easy and convenient to save with your current account provider – especially if you are using features like round-ups that automatically pass money into your account – it makes sense to shop around for larger sums.

Telegraph Money’s best buy savings tables can help you find a competitive product.

Best for everyday spending

The best option for your everyday spending will depend on how you’re likely to use the account.

If you pay for everyday essentials via debit card, you could earn both cashback (for the first year) and get interest paid on your round-ups with Chase.

If you use overdrafts, Starling and Monzo are currently the only options for this – and Starling can beat Monzo’s rates.

You’ll need to watch out for fees and limits when it comes to depositing or withdrawing cash. Chase and Revolut don’t accept cash deposits at all, while Monzo charges a £1 fee. Starling has a £1,000 fee-free annual cash deposit limit, with a 0.7pc charge thereafter.

For withdrawing cash, Monzo, Revolut have cash deposit limits, which vary depending on which plan you’re on. Starling and Chase do not charge withdrawal fees.

All four offer an impressive range of tools to give you an insight into your spending and to help with budgeting. However, which is best for you will very much depend on your personal preferences and what you’ll find most useful. Some people might find a bill splitter tool handy, others won’t be bothered.

The verdict

All of these banks have their fans, but for all-around usability, our favourite is Starling Bank. It pays a decent rate on savings, and its overdraft charges are lower than Monzo. It also offers a raft of tools and features that leave lots of high street banks in the shade.

But perhaps most of all, we like Starling Bank’s simplicity – it offers one strong, fee-free account. You don’t need to stump extra cash for a better deal, and therefore won’t end up inadvertently paying for perks or benefits you might not need.

data.title: Marmite sold to the Americans in £50bn deal
data.description:
Marmite will fall into American hands after its owner agreed to a $66bn (£50bn) deal with a US food giant.

The brand, known for its love-it-or-hate-it slogan, is one of several supermarket staples being sold by Unilever to Baltimore-based spice and sauce maker McCormick.

Other brands changing hands under the deal include Hellmann’s mayonnaise, Knorr stock cubes, Bovril, Colman’s mustard and Pot Noodle. They will join McCormick’s stable, which includes French’s mustard and Schwartz herbs.

Fernando Fernández, the boss of Unilever, said the deal would create a “global flavour powerhouse”.

The combined company will be 65pc-owned by Unilever but will operate under McCormick’s name and leadership, with its headquarters in the US. The new company will also be listed in New York.

Unilever and McCormick said they hoped to find $600m of cost savings from the deal, a forecast sure to raise concerns about potential job losses.

Marmite was founded in Burton-on-Trent in 1902, utilising leftover yeast from a local brewery, and is still made in the Staffordshire town today. Colman’s started in Norwich in 1814 and continues to be made from mustard seeds grown in the area.

Brendan Foley, McCormick’s chairman, said a “big portion” of savings would come from the power to negotiate better prices with suppliers, given the bigger orders that will be placed.

However, he refused to rule out factory closures in the UK. Asked about the potential to move manufacturing out of Britain, he said the company may see “synergies play out” in manufacturing, distribution and media.

“When we think about getting more detail about those areas, it is probably detail yet to be shared,” he added.

News of the potential sale of iconic British brands Marmite and Colman’s sparked a backlash earlier this month. John Farrand, the managing director of the Guild of Fine Food, said the companies would “become a number on a spreadsheet.”

It follows other US takeovers of historic British brands such as Cadbury and Lea & Perrins.

The sale leaves Unilever focused on home and beauty brands such as Dove, Vaseline and Lynx.

Unilever recently demerged its ice cream business, spinning it out as The Magnum Ice Cream Company. The new entity, which was the result of an almost two-year-long process, is now home to Ben & Jerry’s and Cornetto, as well as the eponymous ice cream brand.

As well as a majority shareholding, Unilever will get $15.7bn in cash under the terms of the deal.

Mr Fernández said the McCormick deal was “another decisive step in sharpening our portfolio and accelerating our strategy towards high-growth categories”.

He said: “We are unlocking trapped value through a growth-led separation of foods, creating a scaled, global flavour powerhouse.

“By combining Unilever Foods’ iconic leading brands and global reach with McCormick’s exceptional portfolio, category expertise and capabilities, we are establishing a focused, high-quality business with significant top-line growth and value creation potential.”

However, shares in Unilever fell more than 7pc on news of the transaction.

Chris Beckett, an analyst at wealth manager Quilter Cheviot, said: “The market, so far, has not reacted well to the news.

“Completion is not expected until mid-2027, which is a long time and introduces risks that mean it could be stopped.”

Unilever has been on a major drive to trim down its sprawling portfolio of food brands.

It sold its spreads business in 2017, which included I Can’t Believe It’s Not Butter and Flora, and in 2022, it offloaded most of its tea business, including Lipton and PG Tips.

Brendan Foley, McCormick chairman, said: “The Unilever Foods business is one we have long admired, with a portfolio that complements our existing business, capabilities and long-term vision.

“This combination will create a diversified flavour leader with a robust growth profile that remains differentiated by its focus on flavouring calories while others compete for them.”

As well as French’s, a sweet mustard typically associated with hot dogs, McCormick also makes Frank’s Red Hot and Cholula hot sauces and various spice blends, including Old Bay seasoning.

data.title: British AI startup faces backlash over Microsoft data centre
data.description:
One of Britain’s largest artificial intelligence (AI) startups is facing a backlash over plans to build a data centre for Microsoft after local officials raised fears it would drive up energy costs for residents.

Nscale, which counts Sir Nick Clegg as a board member, has been hit with objections in Essex over concerns the power-hungry energy demands of the site will strain the local electricity grid.

The planned data centre, built on a former scaffolding warehouse in Loughton, has been billed as “the UK’s largest AI supercomputer” and will be backed by 50 megawatts of AI processors. 

One of its key customers will be Microsoft after the two companies signed a deal to develop AI power. 

However, Loughton Town council officers have now demanded fresh evidence from Nscale to prove the development’s “substantial energy demand” will not “strain local grid capacity or increase costs for residents”.

The objection was sent to officials at Epping Forest District Council who are overseeing the application. It notes that the site is 50pc higher than originally proposed and has 50pc more internal capacity.

“The proposal would require more cooling and increased energy to facilitate this application,” the objection warns.

The town planning officer has requested that Nscale submit a full new planning application to proceed with the plans. The timeline to get the project up and running has also been pushed back from this year to early 2027.

It is understood that the delay is to allow time to secure access to the most up-to-date AI processors from US chip giant Nvidia, which were only announced this month.

Nscale has been cited by the Government as a key driver of Britain’s AI infrastructure revolution.

Earlier this month, the company, led by Australian entrepreneur Josh Payne and headquartered in the UK, confirmed it had raised $2bn to expand across the US.

The business has secured investment from backers including Nvidia, Dell and Norwegian investor Aker. Its site in Loughton is expected to house thousands of Nvidia graphics processors and will count Microsoft as a key customer.

AI systems such as OpenAI’s ChatGPT and Anthropic’s Claude require large amounts of computing power and have fuelled hundreds of billions in investment around the world in data centre capacity.

Labour has been courting AI data centre developers as it seeks to engineer an AI infrastructure building spree in Britain and has sought to ease planning constraints.

Last week, The Telegraph disclosed that Steve Reed, the secretary for housing, had bypassed objections from councillors in Buckinghamshire to give ministers the final say over a £2bn data project.

Meanwhile, the global building blitz of AI data centres has prompted concerns over their ballooning energy demands and water use, clashing with Labour’s stated net zero agenda.

Last month, a report from the energy regulator Ofgem admitted that the total pipeline of UK data centre projects included power demands of more than 50 gigawatts (GW).

If all these projects were built and operated at full capacity, they would require more power than Britain’s peak daily electricity.

An Nscale spokesman said: “Site investigation and permit work is under way on the Loughton site, and we expect construction work to begin in the second quarter of 2026.

“While the schedule was recently updated to accommodate the installation of the latest Vera Rubin 200 technology, we expect the site to be operational in the second quarter of 2027.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Housebuilder facing cash squeeze handed £300m by government
data.description:
Labour is under pressure to explain why the Government has handed a troubled housebuilder more than £300m in taxpayer-backed loans and contracts.

Paul Holmes, the shadow housing minister, said there were “questions about Labour’s judgement” after it partnered with Vistry to deliver affordable housing.

The London-listed building firm has been handed around £315m in grants and loans, according to Matthew Pennycook, the housing minister.

Homes England, the housing quango responsible for allocating the funds, does not directly monitor the financial viability of the companies it works with, he added.

Shares in Vistry have crashed by more than 50pc over the past month following the surprise departure of Greg Fitzgerald, chief executive, and amid concerns about a cash squeeze at the business.

Vistry has announced plans to offer deep discounts on some of its homes to offload them and raise capital. Reports suggest it has also demanded delays on paying subcontractors and asked for discounts as it battles to preserve cash.

It follows a string of profit warnings after bosses underestimated the cost of building homes in the south of England.

Paul Holmes, the Conservative shadow Housing minister, submitted an official parliamentary question to Mr Pennycook last week on the taxpayer’s “financial commitment to Vistry”.

“Vistry’s plight raises serious doubts about Labour’s ability to deliver their so-called Affordable Homes Programme,” Mr Holmes told The Telegraph.

“And it also raises questions about Labour’s judgement. If the Government isn’t properly scrutinising the companies it partners with, how can it say whether they will succeed or fail?

“Labour must come clean about how and why ministers concluded Vistry was the best choice – and tell us how much taxpayers’ money could be lost.”

In November, ministers cleared Vistry to bid for as much as £700m in taxpayer subsidies as part of the Government’s £39bn affordable homes programme. It has the same status under the existing programme, begun by the Conservatives in 2021.

Anthony Codling of RBC Capital Markets said there were “amber flags” about Vistry’s financial position.

“Vistry needs to slow down, shrink the business, stabilise the cash situation before it sees growth,” he said.

Mr Codling added that the going concern note in Vistry’s latest financial accounts had grown from around 350 words to more than 1,700 words, three times the size that it was this time last year, which “paints a darker downside” than before.

The wider economic backdrop also offers little comfort, with housebuilders warning of inflationary uncertainty stemming from the conflict in Iran.

“If the market really turns down, it’ll get a lot trickier for Vistry,” said Aynsley Lammin of Investec.

Mr Pennycook said that the Affordable Homes Programme does not carry hard credit risk exposure on companies it works with, as it is a grant programme, so a “purposely high-level review” is undertaken instead.

The ministers added that there are “ no checks undertaken on the off-balance sheet liabilities” as it “is not the role of Homes England to undertake liquidity tests on strategic partners”.

A Vistry spokesman said that taxpayer money was not at risk and said it “has been deployed on building affordable homes that this country desperately needs”.

They added that Vistry “continues to maintain a comfortable level of headroom against its banking facilities”.

“We work closely with Homes England on the delivery of new homes, and, like all of its partners, our programme is closely monitored and audited,” they said.

“The grant funding has all been committed, and the homes for which the funds were allocated are now contracted and owned by regulated, registered providers.”

In March, Mr Fitzgerald vowed to shrink Vistry’s debt by the end of this year.

A spokesman for Homes England said: “Homes England operates within robust, transparent frameworks when working with delivery partners. We don’t comment on the commercial or financial position of individual partners.”

The Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government declined to comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: The green-belt village about to fall under the shadow of Europe’s largest data centre
data.description:
“The peace, quiet and openness of the country.” That’s why Danny Leach and his family first moved to North Ockendon, on the north-east side of Greater London, back in 2005.

Despite being barely outside the M25 and a part of the London Borough of Havering, the village and surrounding countryside are remarkably quiet.

The fenland surrounding the village is undeveloped and, considering its proximity to the capital, surprisingly biodiverse.

“I’ve got nearly 100 species of birds that visit my garden, including kingfishers, barn owls and tawny owls,” says Leach. “There are also great crested newts and foxes. On a summer evening, I can hear the frogs croaking.”

Part of the reason such an environment can exist so close to a city of 10 million is the Metropolitan Green Belt, which, since its creation in 1938, has protected the countryside near London and prevented urban sprawl.

Now, a proposed Green Belt-busting development could change the area’s character forever.

Havering council recently launched a consultation on “East Havering Data Centre”, a hyperscale AI facility that would make North Ockendon home to the largest data centre in Europe.

The facility would use 540 acres of existing farmland and require 600 megawatts of electricity to operate – the same amount of energy needed to run one million homes.

Developer Digital Reef says the site will include around 84 acres of data centre buildings, along with a 280-acre ecological park.

Residents were outraged to learn that the consultation period for the 2,700-page proposal would last only 30 days, clashing with the May elections, and that Havering council were processing the project via a “Local Development Order”, a special type of planning tool that allows conventional planning permission steps to be skipped.

Labour and Conservative members of the council’s strategic planning committee advocated a longer, 12-week consultation period, but were outvoted by the Havering Residents Association, a local party that runs the council.

Data centres are essentially large warehouses filled with specialist computing equipment that processes AI requests submitted by users on their phones or computers. East Havering Data Centre is one of hundreds of these projects proposed near Britain’s major cities to power the growing demand for AI, many of which will be built on green-belt land.

Data centres require large amounts of energy and water to operate, raising concerns that construction plans could put pressure on electricity and water infrastructure and drive up household bills.

Leach is one of many North Ockendon residents who are unhappy with the proposal. His property, which backs on to the site, will be particularly affected.

‘People move to the country for a reason’

“It’s a big shock,” he says. “People move to the countryside for a reason, and then suddenly you have something the size of a battleship going up at the end of your garden.”

Local businesses are also worried. Daniel Anthony has run Top Meadow Golf Club his entire working life, taking over from his father.

Since launching a nine-hole golf club in 1985, the family has grown the business into an 18-hole club and 20-room hotel that employs 50 people.

“Everything, everything, everything has been reinvested from day one,” says Anthony. He is worried the project is putting a lifetime of work at risk.

Today, Anthony says the club makes most of its money from functions hosted at the hotel and restaurant: “We do anything from birthdays and anniversaries to funerals, which are a big one for us because it’s only two and a half miles to the nearest crematorium.”

East Havering Data Centre will be built directly across the road from Top Meadow. Anthony is worried that the facility will be a blight on the club’s prized views of the countryside. “It’s the view really; that’s what people come for,” he says.

He is also concerned about industrial traffic and possible traffic diversions during the construction. A traffic diversion could extend the journey from Top Meadow to the nearby crematorium from two and a half to 10 miles, threatening the club’s funeral and wake business.

The rise of data centres

Developer Digital Reef estimates the facility will take four years to construct – longer than most data centre projects.

Ed Griffiths, of construction consultancy Barbour ABI, says construction is relatively fast for most data centres, with build programmes averaging 19 to 30 months, depending on scale and power requirements.

Sites near large cities are ideal for AI data centres, as user requests can be processed more quickly.

“Urban fringe locations are common for data centres, particularly where they provide proximity to users while still offering enough space and infrastructure to support large facilities,” says Griffiths.

This makes the green-belt land that surrounds many of Britain’s cities tempting for developers.

Data provided to The Telegraph by Barbour ABI indicate that 13 of the 100 largest data centres planned in the UK would fall within the green belt.

These green-belt data centres have a combined value of just over £10bn, representing an outsized share of the 100 largest planned projects at 19pc of the £52bn total.

Data centre construction is a priority for the Government, which views the facilities as essential for turning Britain into an “AI superpower” and for growing the economy.

In a January 2025 speech, Sir Keir Starmer said AI will be “mainlined into the veins of the nation” and has classed data centres as “critical national infrastructure”.

‘Destroying a rich ecosystem’

Many developers include environmental concessions, such as the East Havering Data Centre’s “ecology park” in London, in their planning applications as a sweetener for nearby communities.

However, Ian Pirie, a Havering Friends of the Earth co-ordinator, is sceptical of their benefits.

“Developers think that they can just tear up all that land and then dig somewhere else, plant some trees, and they’ve restored the ecosystem. It’s complete nonsense,” says Pirie.

“They are destroying an already existing, rich ecosystem. It would take another 100 years to have what we’ve got there now.”

Another local concern is the data centre’s potential water usage. The facilities often use water cooling to prevent computers from overheating.

Digital Reef has proposed that excess heat generated by the data centre will be redirected to heat local homes and a novel 12-acre indoor avocado farm.

But residents are unconvinced, telling The Telegraph that the local water supply is already temperamental and that industrial usage could push it to breaking point.

A spokesman for North Ockendon Residents’ Association says: “The water supply here is very old, under-maintained pipe work. If they were to then start taking thousands of gallons a day and something happened, we would be without water.”

Griffiths, who works at Barbour ABI, says access to grid power is the most important factor in selecting a site for a data centre, followed by water availability.

Data centres’ use of energy and water resources could have a national impact. Ireland, which is a hub for trans-Atlantic data cables and hosts the European headquarters of American tech giants such as Amazon and Google, has prioritised its data centre infrastructure.

In 2024, Ireland’s data centres used 22pc of its total electricity supply, more than the 18pc used by the country’s urban homes, according to the Central Statistics Office. There are concerns that a similar build-out in the UK would drive up energy costs, squeezing consumers.

A spokesman for Havering Local Planning Authority says that a full evidence base for East Havering Data Centre would be considered as part of the application, including assessments on biodiversity, ecology, economic impact, transport, water usage and use of the green belt. 

Digital Reef could not be reached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Times have changed, but Ed Miliband is stranded in another age
data.description:
When the Climate Change Act sailed through Parliament in 2008, it did so with overwhelming political support and barely a murmur of opposition. Only five Tory MPs voted against it on the second reading.

Such was the political consensus around the so-called “climate change emergency” that no vote at all was held when the Act was later beefed up further to make “net zero” by 2050 a legally binding commitment.

The costs were barely considered; it was 2019, and we were at the height of Greta Thunberg mania, a collective, mass panic about climate change, fuelled by the protests of a 16-year-old Swedish schoolgirl.

The then prime minister, Theresa May, wanted to be remembered for something other than her bungled handling of Brexit, and this was to be her legacy – making the UK the first major country to pass a law requiring complete decarbonisation of the economy by a defined future date.

Seven years and two energy crises later, and what was thought of as a masterstroke of global leadership back then is proving a debilitating poisoned chalice that acts as a warning to all who might follow.

Everyone can see that it is not working as hoped, and yet we remain lashed to the mast as if no longer in control of our own destiny.

Sir Keir Starmer was at his legalistic and process-driven worst last week when confronted by these issues at Prime Minister’s Questions.

Decisions on whether the UK should further develop its North Sea oil and gas reserves were not his to make, he in essence said. Rather, they were a “quasi-judicial” matter belonging to the Energy Secretary, Ed Miliband.

Here was proof positive that Miliband, already credited with having forced Sir Keir to distance himself from Trump’s war with Iran, is the real power behind the throne. Seemingly, Sir Keir cannot even make up his own mind on energy policy.

Some suggest a devious cunning in Sir Keir’s apparent impotence; that he is making Miliband wholly own a policy that is looking less and less defensible by the day.

When the moment comes, Miliband – still a popular figure among Labour members and therefore a potent threat to Sir Keir’s leadership – can be made to take the blame.

But I doubt that fully explains the Prime Minister’s refusal to take responsibility. As we know, Sir Keir is a stickler for legal process, and net zero is positively crawling with legislative and judicial determinations that collectively amount to an almost insurmountable barrier to change.

Even if outlawing further North Sea oil and gas development were not a Labour Party manifesto commitment, what can and cannot be done is closely guarded by quangos with semi-independent authority – in particular, the North Sea Transition Authority (NSTA) and the Offshore Petroleum Regulator for Environment and Decommissioning (OPRED).

Ultimate responsibility lies with the Secretary of State, but he’s hardly likely to go against judgments with which he wholeheartedly agrees. They provide useful cover.

As does the legislation, which requires the Government to set regular carbon budgets based on mandatory advice from another independent quango, the Climate Change Committee. Running down the North Sea is very much part of the plan for getting to net zero.

This objective is further buttressed by a number of judicial decisions which have required environmental impact assessments to take into account the supposed harms of actually burning the oil and gas alongside the carbon costs of extracting it.

The real game-changer was a Supreme Court judgment regarding a tiny little onshore oil prospect at Horse Hill in Surrey. Planning permission was ruled unlawful because the promoters had failed to consider the greenhouse gas emissions that would occur when the oil was ultimately burned – so-called Scope 3 emissions.

Last year, Scotland’s Court of Session used the same logic to block approval for the Jackdaw gas field and Rosebank oil prospect in the North Sea.

Fossil fuel development has become a legal quagmire worthy of Bleak House, the Charles Dickens novel that satirised the paralysis of England’s early 19th century courts system.

Indeed, it might be seen as a metaphor for the wider problem of getting anything done in today’s Britain.

The whole economy is seemingly sinking beneath a sea of lawyers, taxmen, computer-says-no government bureaucrats and environmental do-gooders.

When the boss of a company as synonymous with high corporate standards as Marks & Spencer starts yelling blue murder at rocketing energy costs, it is surely time to finally sit up and take notice.

The situation is obviously being greatly worsened by renewed conflict in the Middle East, but as M&S’s Stuart Machin points out, that’s not the root of the problem. Rather it is the costs that are loaded on top by government taxes, levies and instruction.

The Chancellor, Rachel Reeves, removed some of these levies from household bills in her Spring Statement and absorbed them onto general taxation instead.

But she did not extend the same courtesy to the business community, which she continues to treat like a milch cow.

In May 2024, just before the last general election, 120 business leaders signed an open letter backing Labour’s economic plans, saying it was “time for a change”. Not many of them will hold those sentiments today. Surprise! Labour’s pro-business, pro-growth affectations have turned out to be no more than skin-deep.

As is often said, allowing a more expansive North Sea won’t in itself make much difference to underlying oil and gas prices, which are mainly international in determination.

But it ought to make a sizeable difference to energy security, the balance of payments, tax revenues, investment and jobs.

If those arguments are still not enough to sway the green energy zealots of the Labour leadership, then the following certainly ought to.

According to modelling by the lobby group Offshore Energies, UK developed reserves of North Sea gas are running out at such a rate that within 10 years, Britain will be reliant on imported liquefied natural gas from the Middle East and America for more than half its total gas consumption.

We’ll still be burning some gas even as we stagger across the net zero finishing line 24 years hence – if indeed we ever do, which looks increasingly unlikely, despite the forced march imposed on Britain’s already enfeebled economy.

Sorry to keep harping on about tiny little Norway, but across the North Sea they see the UK as very much a cautionary tale. Norway, too, has some pretty challenging net zero targets, but they leave the Scope 3 emissions of their oil and gas industries outside the calculation, knowing that the country’s comparative prosperity would plummet without them.

Renewed conflict in the Middle East has again highlighted the perils of globalised supply chains and re-emphasised the importance of economic self-sufficiency.

When the facts change, said the economist John Maynard Keynes, I change my mind. What do you do, sir? Carry on regardless, comes the answer, as the ship sails blindly on towards the rocks.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Jim Ratcliffe’s chemical plant suffers £515m loss
data.description:
Sir Jim Ratcliffe’s chemicals empire racked up €593m (£515m) of losses last year, as it warned of further pain from the war in Iran.

Ineos blamed soaring energy costs and Donald Trump’s tariffs as losses jumped from €71.1m the year prior, highlighting mounting economic strain on the billionaire’s sprawling business operations.

As well as a lack of profit, Ineos’s debt pile also rose from €10.6bn to €11.7bn at the end of December 2025.

It comes after S&P Global and Fitch, two of the world’s largest rating agencies, downgraded chunks of Ineos debt in recent months.

The latest results predate the outbreak of war in Iran, with bosses warning that the conflict risks piling more pressure on the debt-laden business.

An Ineos spokesman said: “While we do not have operations in the Middle East and revenue generated in this region is not material to the [Ineos] Group, the length, impact and outcome of the ongoing military conflict in the Middle East is highly unpredictable and there can be no assurances that further unforeseen events related to this conflict will not have a further material adverse affect on our operations in the future.”

The latest results emerged as Ineos warned investors of possible delays to its €4bn (£3.5bn) flagship petrochemicals plant because of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz.

Ineos Group told investors it was facing potential problems completing its Project One plant in Antwerp, Belgium, after two of its components were stranded in the Gulf.

The plant, which will make materials for plastic products and was set to be finished by the end of this year, was already suffering from delays after its environmental permit was suspended in 2023 by the Belgian government.

Ineos has described the €4bn Project One development as the largest investment in the European chemical sector in a generation. It said last June that the plant was 70pc completed.

Project One is made up of heavy modules which have been built across Thailand, the United Arab Emirates and the Philippines before being shipped to Antwerp.

Two of those shipments are stuck in a port in the Gulf.

Iran has effectively closed the Strait of Hormuz since Israel and the US first launched strikes on the country in late February.

Ineos said that rerouting tankers around the Cape of Good Hope, away from escalating tensions in the Middle East, had already cost the business more than €30m over the last two years.

S&P revealed its latest downgrade for Ineos’s debt last month.

It warned that Ineos was facing turmoil from “challenging trading conditions” around the world for the petrochemical sector. At one point, the company was reportedly considering asset sales as it looked to refinance some of its debt pile.

Sir Jim has blamed mounting losses at his Grangemouth chemical works on government carbon taxes and Britain’s sky-high energy costs.

However, he has also triggered controversy over his comments surrounding immigration.

The Manchester United co-owner, one of Britain’s richest men with a £17bn fortune, said earlier this year that Britain was being “colonised by immigrants”. He later apologised for “offending some people” with his language.

Ineos declined to comment.

data.title: West Midlands Police makes £1m by ‘harassing’ lorry drivers
data.description:
West Midlands Police has come under fire for making more than £1m by “harassing” lorry drivers and forcing them to pay for escorts.

The force has increased its income from road haulage companies 70-fold over the past five years from £15,000 to more than £1m, according to data obtained by the Construction Plant-hire Association (CPA).

Police have powers to require an escort for unusually large or heavy lorries which pass through their area while making deliveries, including those made to HS2 sites.

Lord Attlee, who went undercover to investigate the force, compared the tactics used by West Midlands Police to “ones that you would expect to see used by a corrupt police force in a slowly developing country”.

A qualified lorry driver himself from his days in the Royal Electrical and Mechanical Engineers, the Tory peer travelled in the cab of a lorry on a route known to be targeted regularly by West Midlands Police while posing as a driving assessor from the haulage firm.

He told Parliament that he witnessed officers “harassing” drivers by “deliberately misquoting or misapplying regulations, and prohibiting vehicle movements when there is no power to do so”.

“Such excessive and overzealous checks are interfering with the smooth running of the heavy haulage industry, which is racking up significant extra costs in all the wasted time spent responding to these demands,” he added.

If officers decide the load needs to be escorted by them, there is nothing to prevent them refusing permission until the lorry company agrees to pay the force sums measured in thousands of pounds.

Lord Attlee also told The Telegraph that he believed West Midlands Police had been charging haulage companies its standard six-hour overtime rate several times in one day.

This means the same group of police officers may have billed those companies several times over for the same amount of work, he said.

‘Disruption and stress across the industry’

The boss of one CPA member company, who spoke on condition of anonymity for fear of police retaliation, said: “Operators are also being pushed to request police escorts when they are not needed. If an escort is requested, the cost is around £2,500 just to travel through the West Midlands area.

“This is creating a huge administrative burden and will cause serious disruption and stress across the industry,” added the executive, who explained that some deliveries were only worth £2,000 – meaning police demands for money more than doubled the price paid by the customer.

Steven Mulholland, chief executive of the CPA, said: “This isn’t about safety – it’s about misinterpretation and incentives that are putting HS2 and major infrastructure projects at risk.

“You simply cannot deliver HS2 or major rail and construction projects if the machinery needed to build them is held up by regional policy drift.

“We need an urgent return to a single, nationally consistent interpretation of the rules, so compliant movements are treated the same way in every part of the country.”

A West Midlands Police spokesman said that the force tries to “foster positive relationships with haulage operators”, doing so “without unnecessary penalties”.

“Demand for abnormal loads across the region is increasing, fuelled by a thriving industry and major infrastructure projects such as HS2 and developments around M42 Junction 5A,” the spokesman said.

“Last year, we assessed 171,565 notifications. Of these, 13,354 notifications were reviewed as they potentially required police support due to the route, size or nature of the load.

“A total of 706 were escorted by our officers, which equates to 0.41 per cent of movement notifications.

“We only ever seek details that are relevant and outlined in legislation, and vehicle movements are only ever prohibited when they are assessed as unlawful.”

data.title: We could start fracking in the UK within months, says developer
data.description:
Gas could be fracked from Britain within months if Labour overturns a ban on the practice, a leading energy company has said.

Francis Egan, the chief executive of Australian-owned Cuadrilla Resources, said it could take as little as three months to get a gas well drilled. Production could start shortly thereafter.

The Iran war has revived the shale gas industry’s hopes of creating an “inland North Sea” in Lancashire and the East Midlands, as Britain scrambles to counter a looming energy crunch.

Cuadrilla began fracking at Preston New Road, Lancashire, in 2017. But the project was eventually scuppered when Rishi Sunak, the then prime minister, banned the practice in 2022.

“This is the second wake-up call after the Ukraine invasion, which was not that long ago. I don’t know how many more of these the politicians need,” said Mr Egan.

Cuadrilla has plugged its wells and is maintaining the site, but Mr Egan said he was ready to go back in.

“We’re keeping the lights on at Preston New Road. We’re trying to hang on to the licences,” he said.

“You could drill a well within three months in Preston New Road. It’s not a big task to get a rig, go out in the field and drill a well.”

‘The size of the prize’

Cuadrilla is not alone. Europa Oil & Gas is hoping that North Yorkshire County Council will approve next month an application to drill an appraisal well just north of Scarborough.

Planning officers have recommended that councillors approve the project, but local activists are fighting the proposal.

Ed Miliband, the Energy Secretary, praised community activists last year who had “fought back” against shale gas exploration. He promised to pass a law that would “ban fracking and send the frackers packing”.

But Mr Egan said that “nothing is forever in politics”. If the Iran war had put Britain’s North Sea oil and gas reserves back up for debate, fracking could be too.

“Shale is not like drilling a well in the North Sea – it’s like the whole North Sea. That’s the size of the prize,” he said.

Cuadrilla’s operation repeatedly breached tough limits on seismic tremors, prompting frequent mandatory shutdowns. The company also faced court challenges from anti-fracking campaigners, and on-again-off-again moratoriums from successive Conservative prime ministers.

Nigel Farage, the Reform UK leader, has supported a resumption of fracking, but there is no sign of any Labour about-turn.

Mr Egan said: “The Prime Minister seems to have outsourced everything, energy-wise, to Ed Miliband. And Ed Miliband has pretty much outsourced it to Extinction Rebellion. And it’s more extinction than rebellion, I’m afraid.”

Cuadrilla’s owner, Australian-listed mining services company AJ Lucas, has written off the value of its UK investment on its balance sheet and reduced the company to a skeleton operation.

“But they have still valued the option. And of course, given the events of the last two or three weeks, that option value has probably increased,” Mr Egan said.

Some energy analysts are sceptical that Britain’s Bowland Shale, which stretches from Lancashire across to Yorkshire and the East Midlands, is as viable for fracking as the Permian Basin in the US.

Matt Drinkwater, from analysis firm Energy Aspects, said: “Because the UK is so much more densely populated than the US, fracking is much higher cost. There’s a lot of expenditure required for mitigation of impacts on the local community, and also for the social licence to operate.”

While shale gas might be viable when gas prices are high in the coming few years, by 2028 it might be uncompetitive again and the industry would not even be up and running by then.

Mr Egan acknowledged that there was still technical work to be done to ensure that fracking the Bowland Shale would produce the right gas flows over the right time frame.

However, he said developing a domestic shale industry would give Britain lower-cost gas and an economic boost. “We’re talking tens of thousands of jobs and billions of tax revenue.”

data.title: Next boss warns Government against profiteering from energy crisis
data.description:
The boss of Next has warned the Government against “profiting” from the Iran crisis as soaring oil and gas prices are expected to trigger higher tax revenues.

Lord Wolfson of Aspley Guise said it was a “reasonable ask” for the Treasury not to retain any unexpected profits from disruption in the Middle East at a time when businesses are grappling with inflated costs.

His comments mark an escalation in tensions between the Government and the private sector after Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, blamed businesses for higher prices in the wake of the war.

The chief executive of Next said: “I think a reasonable ask from our industry – and in fact all industry – is that the Government doesn’t end up profiting from it.”

He singled out the prospect of higher revenues from fuel duty owing to increased petrol and diesel prices.

Lord Wolfson said it would be fair for the Treasury to take “what it was expecting from fuel but not necessarily more as a result of the price changes”.

“I think that would be a very reasonable ask for the industry to say that to the Government: ‘Don’t actually make more money out of this than you were expecting,’” he said.

Petrol forecourt bosses have hit back at claims they were profiteering from higher prices, arguing instead that the Treasury takes a bigger share of fuel sales than they did.

According to figures from the RAC, the retailer margin on a litre of petrol currently stands at 6pc.

Meanwhile, fuel duty comes in at 52.95p per litre. On top of that is VAT, charged at 20pc.

Petrol prices have risen almost 17p per litre since the start of the Iran conflict while diesel prices have risen around 33p per litre.

The Government is facing mounting pressure to help bring down prices for motorists.

Robert Jenrick, Reform’s economic spokesman, called for an emergency halving of VAT on road fuel on Thursday. He accused the Chancellor of “raking it in while motorists suffer”.

The Conservatives have urged the Government to scrap the upcoming fuel duty increase.

Previous analysis has suggested that the Treasury could also bank as much as £3bn more in tax revenues if the war in the Middle East results in higher oil and gas prices for longer.

Granville Park Partners said last week that it expected soaring energy costs to increase the corporation tax paid by North Sea firms by £1.5bn alone, while the windfall tax on energy companies’ profits would boost the Treasury’s coffers by a further £1.3bn if current oil and gas prices are sustained.

While rising prices at the pump won’t increase the fixed pence amount of fuel duty HMRC collects with every litre sold, the amount owed in VAT will.

While levied at 20pc, in line with most other goods and services, crucially, it is applied after fuel duty, constituting what many have called a “tax on a tax”. Higher petrol and diesel prices could deliver a windfall of £2.5bn to the Treasury.

Lord Wolfson said businesses should not be asking for tax cuts or reductions in levies to help them deal with extra costs, saying this would be “problematic”.

He weighed in on the debate after Next unveiled a £15m hit from the Middle East conflict, which could force it to push up prices as early as this summer.

The fashion retailer said the war in Iran was likely to have “knock-on effects” across its business, as it battles higher air freight fees and inflated fuel costs.

If the disruption drags on longer than expected, Next said it would have to start increasing prices in its stores as soon as June. Further price increases would come in the autumn.

Lord Wolfson said these would be “no more than 10pc”. However, it said this was currently a “contingency, not a plan”.

In its latest trading update on Thursday, the company said: “Much will depend on how long the conflict persists, and how much permanent damage is done to the world’s energy infrastructure.”

Next said if the war continued, extra costs would be reflected in “higher prices to consumers and disruption to our supply chain, both of which are likely to suppress sales”.

There are mounting fears that the war in Iran will start piling fresh pressure on shoppers as global trade chaos and higher energy costs threaten price rises.

Goldman Sachs has warned that inflation in Britain could hit 5pc this year “in a severely adverse scenario”. This compares to inflation of around 3pc last month, with households facing higher energy bills and grocery costs.

The Institute of Grocery Distribution has said food inflation could hit 8pc by June 2026 if the oil shock persisted.

However, Next’s warning suggests shoppers should brace for price rises not just in supermarkets, but across the high street.

Air cargo prices have already soared following strikes across the Middle East, as companies battle to avoid turmoil in key shipping lanes through the region. Rates rose 10pc this week, according to WorldACD Market Data, following an 8pc increase a week earlier. It also reflects sharp increases in fuel costs since the outbreak of war in Iran.

The price of Brent crude is up by more than a third since the conflict began, as Tehran halts oil flows out of the Gulf. Oil prices rose 2.5pc to nearly $105 in early trading on Thursday.

Next issued its sales warning as it said profits jumped 14.5pc to £1.2bn in the year to January. This was ahead of its earlier guidance, driven by better sales in January.

The retailer said sales had been “encouraging in the UK” since the end of its financial year, and had also been “strong overseas up to the point the conflict began in the Middle East”.

However, it said: “We have not yet reached the period of unusually strong UK trading we experienced last year and, perhaps more importantly, instability in the Middle East – which represents around 6pc of our total turnover – may continue to restrain growth in that region.”

data.title: Beware the private credit cockroaches lurking in the UK
data.description:
There is another global crisis rumbling that has almost nothing to do with Iran, but might end up having a similar-sized impact

This is the private credit crisis, which has been simmering for a year now. It has prompted dark warnings from the likes of JPMorgan’s Jamie Dimon, who warned there were “cockroaches” lurking in the underbelly of the US financial engineering industry.

There are fears that a credit crunch could become much more systemic and undermine not only the big banks but also some insurers.

Private credit generally describes loans made directly by non-bank lenders, typically private funds, which are not traded such as bonds, stocks or other types of loans.

The first inklings of the crisis emerged last year as the era of low-cost, cheap money came to an end.

This emerged first of all in the US stock market, where there are funds called business development corporations (BDCs) that invest in private credit.

A few funds started reporting that some of their loans had gone bad.

Unsurprisingly, the share prices of these BDCs sank. An index that tracks these BDCs is down a ghastly 25pc over the last year.

Then in September, car parts supplier First Brands Group and subprime car lender Tricolour both failed, spooking bond investors even more, although it’s worth saying that in both cases, private credit was not at the core of the problem.

Investors were also ruminating on the huge wave of debt being rolled out to tech companies to fund their AI data centres, with Meta in particular drawing attention for its innovative debt structures to fund expansion.

More generally, default rates across the private credit spectrum have been rising, which is peculiar given the healthy state of the US economy.

According to one industry report by ratings agency Fitch, default rates across 1,200 US private debt borrowers ticked up to 5.6pc in the fourth quarter from 5.4pc in the prior quarter, while for 300 privately issued loans, the default rate jumped to 9.2pc from 8.4pc.

And remember, these defaults are happening with the US economy supposedly on fire.

Industry insiders push back, pointing to other industry measures such as the Proskauer Private Credit Default Index, which show defaults are much lower. They also say that net yields after losses remain strong, with most funds returning well into the double digits in income.

Unfortunately, many wealthy US investors didn’t buy this keep calm message, triggering a wave of redemptions across the industry, a trend exacerbated by some fund managers.

One leading player, called Blue Owl Capital, announced a merger of its public and private funds, which didn’t go down well with some investors, prompting the firm to halt redemptions and sell assets.

There are ways this crisis could become a bigger problem over here. The first is that UK funds might start to take a hit on their loans.

There’s a reasonably large number of UK funds listed on the London stock market that invest in everything from private equity and infrastructure debt (Sequoia Infrastructure) through to leveraged loans (CVC Income and Growth) and Collateralised Loan Obligation structures (Fair Oak Income fund).

The good news is that, according to Shonil Chande, a funds analyst at Panmure Liberum, the UK-listed credit space looks relatively resilient right now.

The cleanest strategies, he argues, are those focused predominantly on European leveraged loans – with lower software exposure and more vanilla structures.

He says yields of 8pc to 10pc remain attractive, and with loan prices drifting lower, the entry point might be improving for patient investors.

The bigger issue with private credit is systemic and focused on banks and insurers.

It’s thought that overall bank exposure to private credit is probably around $300bn (£225bn) as of last year, which is a substantial, though not huge, number compared to other loans.

British banks do have private credit exposure, with Barclays’ total exposure at about £20bn, while HSBC’s direct exposure to private credit appears to be small.

Pam Kaur, the HSBC finance chief, said it was in the “single-digit billion” range.

The numbers look manageable, and some European bank bosses are very relaxed.

The boss of Deutsche Bank says they haven’t lost a cent on their now roughly $30bn private credit portfolio in more than a decade.

Insurers, especially in the US, have also been bingeing on private credit, with the Wall Street Journal reporting that the sector’s exposure had increased 21pc or $83bn last year alone, with a total exposure of $482bn at the end of December, or 8pc of total insurer balance sheets.

European insurers have also been active, though to a much smaller extent.

Axa, for instance, warned about industry-wide exposure to AI data centres and said that private credit accounts for about 14pc of the insurer’s total deployment, though a large share of that is in mortgage-related debt structures.

More broadly, at the end of 2024, ratings agency Moody’s estimated that private credit accounted for approximately 13pc of insurers’ investment portfolios in Europe. In the US, those numbers are thought to be much higher.

The core problem here is whether the private equity model that has emerged over the last few decades is as robust as its proponents argue.

Private credit supplies a large chunk of the funding behind these deals, and if this pool of liquidity starts to dry up, if only because investors start to panic, that might force private equity fund managers to liquidate portfolio holdings, maybe at fire-sale prices, crystallising losses.

And before we get too complacent, we should remember that private equity owns huge swathes of UK industry and has been the key buyer of stock-market-listed UK assets.

A faltering funding engine for private equity will mean problems for us in the UK.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Labour has no plan for the worst energy shock of our lives
data.description:
Keep calm and carry on – or something to that effect.

It’s week five of Donald Trump’s Iran excursion, and just when you think the president couldn’t sound any more deranged, he’s now talking about taking Kharg Island by force. 

This is an idea military strategists have compared with anything from a “suicide mission” to a “self-imposed hostage crisis”.

Either that, or Trump could decide to blow the offshore terminal to smithereens instead; such is the clear and rational thinking that pervades the White House these days.

The price of crude oil responded with another 3pc jump on Monday, taking it to $116 (£87.94) at one point – a roughly 50pc increase since hostilities began. Expect it to go much higher if the US puts boots on the ground in Iran.

Meanwhile, there is talk that repairs to damaged energy infrastructure could take between two and five years, prolonging the disruption to global supplies and keeping prices ruinously high.

Indeed, many experts already believe the Middle East conflict is shaping up to be the worst energy shock the world has ever faced.

As Fatih Birol, the head of the International Energy Agency, has warned, the Iran war has knocked out roughly 11 million barrels of oil a day compared with the loss of five million barrels during each of the two crises in 1973 and 1979.

Gas shortages have been similarly devastating, with about 140 billion cubic metres a day lost – double what was removed from the market after Russia’s invasion of Ukraine.

So what, might you ask, is our Government’s plan to address what could prove to be an even bigger blow to our livelihoods than both the pandemic and Vladimir Putin’s imperialist expansion on Europe’s doorstep?

The answer, it seems, is that there isn’t one. On the contrary, ministers are still – publicly, at least – persisting with the absurd stance that it is pretty much “situation normal”.

Yes, contingency plans are being drawn up. “You would expect any government to plan for eventualities where events happen in the world,” Bridget Phillipson, the Education Secretary, said breezily at the weekend.

However, “people should be reassured, ahead of the Easter holidays, just to carry on as things are”, she added, seemingly oblivious to the irony of how anxiety-inducing such words are.

While ministers attempt their desperate “nothing to see here” act for fear of “scaring people”, as Phillipson patronisingly puts it, those same people can turn on the news and see that two things are happening.

The first is the supply squeeze becoming more acute worldwide. Bangladesh may have only a few weeks of oil left, swathes of Southeast Asia have only a few months’ worth of supplies at best, parts of Africa are experiencing shortages, and so too are Australia and New Zealand.

True, many of the countries facing a critical supply squeeze are more reliant on Middle Eastern oil, while we, on the other hand, get most of our crude from the US and Norway. 

However, much more of our refined fuels are imported, which is why the price of diesel has gone up 26pc versus 15pc for unleaded petrol, so it’s only a matter of time before Europe is next.

Indeed, industry figures think the crunch is just around the corner. Wael Sawan, Shell’s chief executive, has warned of energy and fuel shortages by mid-April. That’s a fortnight away. 

Traders and fuel suppliers estimate that diesel stockpiles could halve by mid-May.

I know who I’m inclined to listen to, and it’s not the Education Secretary, nor any Cabinet member, for that matter. The Government is being far too complacent.

The second thing that people in this country can see is that while ministers dilly-dally, a growing number of countries are acting quickly and decisively, starting with sweeping tax cuts to soften the hit to household incomes.

Spain’s socialist government was among the first to move, cutting VAT on fuel, electricity and gas by more than half to 10pc as part of a €5bn (£3.8bn) emergency support package. 

Ireland cut fuel duty by a fifth, while the Polish government slashed VAT on fuels from 23pc to 8pc, and reduced excise taxes to the lowest level permitted by Brussels.

At the weekend, Anthony Albanese, the Australian prime minister, joined in by halving fuel duty – a move expected to save motorists 26 cents a litre when they fill up their tanks – as well as unveiling a new four-stage fuel security plan to manage supply.

Other than support for those reliant on heating oil, there has been no such relief here.

Steps to bring down both energy and fuel consumption are also being rapidly introduced, though again not in the UK. 

India has shut down factories, Australia has introduced free public transport to discourage people from driving, civil servants in the Philippines are working a four-day week, and Slovenia has become the first European country to introduce rationing.

None of these represents a silver bullet, but surely any action is better than none at all? Why not start here by designating filling stations for emergency vehicles and prioritising diesel for commercial vehicles?

It is ludicrous for our Government to pretend we are somehow immune to the pressures being felt everywhere else, and it is even sillier to think we can’t handle a few small sacrifices for the greater good of the country. 

Reducing unnecessary journeys, turning off the lights, turning down the heating and going on fewer holidays would be nothing compared to the restrictions everyone endured during Covid.

Our thoroughly forgettable Prime Minister would much rather have more pointless meetings than take tough decisions – the latest with representatives from the shipping, insurance and energy industries to discuss the “importance of working closely with the private sector in tackling the crisis” – whatever that means.

For Rachel Reeves, it has reached the humiliating point where bosses are avoiding her altogether. 

First, the petrol retailers gave the Chancellor the cold shoulder because of accusations of “profiteering”, then several supermarket bosses were too busy to discuss rising food prices with her. Devoid of ideas, this Government has been reduced to populist show trials.

At the very least, it can scrap the planned fuel duty rise, yet Phillipson insists there is “no need” to. It is a farcical position to take, and it sets the scene for yet another screeching Labour policy reversal in the coming weeks. 

No wonder business bosses have stopped listening. Then again, hasn’t everyone?
