data.title: M&S seeks to crack America with clothing launch
data.description:
Marks & Spencer is preparing to launch its clothes in US stores for the first time in its 142-year history as it aims to crack America.

The retailer’s womenswear range will be available in 30 Nordstrom stores across America, including New York and Los Angeles, from Friday, April 3.

The collection will feature a curated selection of more than 60 of M&S’s bestselling items. The launch of the range comes after M&S struck a partnership deal with Nordstrom, a department store that already has agreements with other UK brands including Next and Topshop.

M&S sells food in the US through a partnership with American supermarket Target, as well as a fashion range in Australia.

However, the latest tie-up marks a landmark moment for M&S in that it will see its clothes sold in American shops for the first time since the British high street stalwart was founded in 1884.

M&S has been seeking to boost sales by expanding overseas. Archie Norman, the M&S chairman, has said the company needed to become “a global brand” to survive another century.

The clothing launch will be viewed as a sign of M&S’s growing confidence in the US. It follows a previous disastrous attempt by the British retailer to crack the American fashion market by buying up a local brand.

M&S spent $750m (£565m) on Brooks Brothers in 1988, the tailor that pioneered the off-the-rack suit, in an attempt to gain a foothold in the US.

However, it was forced to retreat from the market after struggling to make the deal work, selling the American chain in 2001 for $225m – less than a third of what it paid.

Speaking in late 2023, Mr Norman said M&S would not make the mistake of “flag planting” again, adding: “You have to choose where you compete and be strong in individual markets.”

One in 10 US customers are aware of M&S as a fashion brand, according to the retailer. It said the highest awareness was among women aged between 25 and 34.

There is strong demand for M&S’s food range in the US. Under the Target tie-up, it sells more than 30,000 bags of Percy Pigs in the US every week.

American customers can buy M&S pieces online, although it has only around 51,000 US visitors to its website each year, suggesting there is significant room to grow. 

Nordstrom previously claimed it had around 33 million customers, although recent shopper numbers are not available as the department chain has since been taken private.

Mark Lemming, the managing director of M&S’s international business, said: “With strong brand momentum as the UK’s most trusted retailer and now leading in style, quality and value perceptions in our womenswear range, now is the time to build our brand awareness in the US fashion market and establish ourselves as a globally trusted brand.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: These drones can take on Putin but can’t beat British bureaucrats
data.description:
It had taken months of hard work. But the British start-up ZeroUSV was finally getting a chance to prove its mettle to the Royal Navy.

In a Nato exercise off the coast of Portugal, the company’s Oceanus12 speedboat drone successfully deployed and recovered special acoustic sensors without any human involvement.

The demonstration was a triumph of British engineering, showing the role autonomous platforms could soon play in hunting Russian submarines in the High North.

Given this success, why is the company now considering permanently moving its equipment overseas?

“How long have you got?” jokes Matthew Ratsey, the managing director.

ZeroUSV is just one of several innovative defence start-ups that have emerged in recent years, buoyed by advances in drone technology that have revolutionised the battlefield in Ukraine.

By air, sea and land, many of these firms are developing kits that could one day help to modernise the Armed Forces.

Yet talk to the entrepreneurs behind them, and a familiar complaint crops up repeatedly: they can build their products in Britain, but it’s nigh on impossible to test them here.

For those who try, the result is often a labyrinthine regulatory process that drags on for months or confinement to small spaces that are not big enough for truly useful experiments.

As a result, many are packing their bags and heading to countries such as Ukraine, America, Spain, Estonia and Lithuania to fly, sail and blow things up, says Toby McCrindle, a partner at law firm Mishcon de Reya, who advises several of the drone start-ups.

“Some incredible British businesses have come out of the traps in the last few years,” he says. “And they’ve hired some of the top engineering minds from our best universities, raised money and built world-leading capabilities.

“But we’ve ended up in a perverse situation where they are having to send their equipment out of the UK to test it.”

The Government is making progress in some areas, for example, by opening an enormous, hangar-like facility in Swindon, Wiltshire, for indoor tests that will be the largest of its kind in Europe.

Yet what many companies want and need is permission to test their inventions outdoors, “beyond visual line of sight”, which is seen as the true test of autonomy.

Securing permission to do this in Britain is often fiendishly difficult and requires wading through thickets of red tape.

In ZeroUSV’s case, this was why September’s Nato exercise was somewhat bittersweet for Ratsey.

For a month, the start-up was allowed to roam freely in some 250 square nautical miles of ocean.

However, at home, the company has permission to sail around less than one square mile of open water in Plymouth Sound.

“It’s like having a new car and not being able to take it out of the driveway,” Ratsey says.

“We can learn almost nothing about our fully autonomous boat because we’re only allowed to do a few laps around the bay.”

And even getting permission to do this was extremely arduous.

To satisfy the Maritime and Coastguard Agency’s workboat code, a team of people at ZeroUSV had to spend six months working on more than 400 pages of documentation.

The code requires companies to provide answers to more than 300 line items, with each one requiring supporting evidence.

“It’s quite possibly the most long-winded and complicated set of rules ever produced,” complains Ratsey.

Against this backdrop, getting a free run off the coast of Portugal felt like a breath of fresh air.

“We learned more in a few days than we’d learned in four months of testing in the UK,” he adds.

“Afterwards, I said, ‘Right, we’ve got to fix this. Otherwise, we’re better off keeping a boat in Portugal permanently.’”

This is just one of many examples across the industry.

Default to foreign testing

MGI Engineering previously said it went to Spain to test its one-way SkyShark “kamikaze” drones, stating that the paperwork could be completed in “a couple of weeks”.

In the UK, the process with the Civil Aviation Authority (CAA) would have taken months.

Meanwhile, start-ups working on interceptors designed to take out Shahed-style drones – of the kind being fired by Iran en masse in the Middle East today – confess it is just easier for them to go to war-torn Ukraine to test their wares.

Or take Hypersonica, the Anglo-German company started by two Oxford University PhDs, which is attempting to make a low-cost hypersonic missile.

It was hailed in February for successfully conducting the first test of its navigation systems, developed in just nine months, during a launch that saw the missile accelerate to more than Mach 6, or around 4,600 miles per hour.

But instead of Britain, where suitable Ministry of Defence (MoD) facilities are available, the test was carried out in Norway.

“As a UK defence startup, our intention is to test in our home country in the near future,” says Jonathan Adams, Hypersonica’s managing director.

The reason regulators are so cautious is understandable in one sense.

With the UK being one of the most densely populated countries in Europe, there is a higher chance of something – or someone – being hit if a drone malfunctions and drops out of the sky during testing.

Yet critics argue that although safety should be paramount, the current processes are too risk-averse – particularly at a time when war has returned to Europe.

The CAA has recently tried to improve things with a new approach to the Sora (specific operations risk assessment) process, in which companies and the regulator are supposed to have an ongoing back-and-forth.

But as one industry source says: “It is meant to be much quicker, but for several reasons it’s not. If anything, it’s more infuriating, and it is having a disproportionately bigger impact on smaller businesses that don’t have vast resources to throw at it.”

‘General capability problem’

Many also argue that parts of Scotland and Wales, which are more sparsely populated than England, should be easier to test in than they are now.

“We’re not against regulation,” says ZeroUSV’s Ratsey. “But we do think the regulation should be fit for purpose. It needs to support companies like ours that are innovating.”

But McCrindle, at Mishcon de Reya, says the UK also has “a general capability problem” in providing places for start-ups to try out their wares.

For example, there are only two privately run facilities with large areas of controlled airspace where drone companies can currently test: West Wales Airport, near Cardigan and the Snowdonia Aerospace Centre.

Companies can theoretically also apply to use some military facilities, such as the MoD’s Hebrides Range, but to do so, they must go through QinetiQ, the defence giant that manages the sites, and tests by the Armed Forces themselves must always take priority.

“We don’t have adequate testing facilities; we don’t have the ranges we need to test anything other than relatively contained drones or systems,” explains McCrindle.

“If you want to test a counter-UAV interceptor system that has to fly at a particular speed and height, and you need to test it kinetically, you can’t do that in the UK. You just can’t.

“There just isn’t really the capacity, the regulation, or the processes built around the regulation, and where they do exist, they are too cumbersome.”

‘Not easy to export this stuff’

Going abroad is a hassle too, he adds, but ironically less of a hassle than trying to test in Britain.

“It’s not easy to export this stuff; typically, young companies don’t have the international trade expertise they will need to do this,” he says. “So we have to get really involved in that for them.

“That adds a huge time burden, which increases cost and slows down innovation.”

At West Wales Airport, the new owner, drone manufacturer Tekever, which bought the airport last summer, says it is eager to get more drone companies out for testing.

Despite being under new management, the site continues to operate as a commercial airport.

During a visit by The Telegraph, one of the workers who has been training staff from the Royal Air Force shows how they build their autonomous drones to fly over the surrounding landscape, avoiding populated areas.

The machines are also programmed to circle or return to base automatically if something goes wrong.

“The key selling point of West Wales is that it’s not that congested out here, so this is an ideal place to test,” says Stewart Pearce, the head of training and regulation at Tekever.

“Ultimately, we all want to fly our drones in unsegregated airspace with freedom. We can’t do that yet, because safety is really important. 

“But we’re working on a detect-and-avoid capability, which is software that will appease the CAA and prove that we can mitigate any [risks].

“In our experience, the CAA is an innovative public body, and they recognise they need to change – and we are at the forefront of those conversations with them.”

The CAA says it is seeking to make beyond-visual-line-of-sight testing easier and hopes to approve more sites, with two currently in the approvals process.

A spokesman for the Maritime and Coastguard Agency said it was also “committed to accelerating support for innovators and unlocking growth and skills in maritime”.

The Government adds: “We want the UK to be a global leader in drone technology and are working with industry alongside the regulator to grow the sector while maintaining the safety of our airspace.”

But ZeroUSV’s Ratsey remains more circumspect for now. “We’ve got a real edge in Britain at the moment,” he says.

“But we’re going to lose that if we’re not allowed to keep developing and moving forward.

“At the moment, we’re shooting ourselves in the foot.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Majority of Labour voters back drilling in the North Sea
data.description:
A majority of Labour voters support drilling for oil in the North Sea, a new poll has found.

In a blow to Ed Miliband, a survey has revealed that supporters of drilling outnumber the opponents by more than two to one amongst his party’s voters.

The Energy Secretary has repeatedly dismissed suggestions that Britain should ramp up domestic oil and gas production, even as the crisis in the Middle East has piled pressure on global supplies.

But a poll by campaign group Looking for Growth suggests Labour’s supporters – and a majority of voters in the country – disagree with him.

The poll found that support for drilling for oil in the North Sea stood at about 57pc amongst all voters, compared to only 15pc who were against.

Within this, support amongst Labour voters stood at 51pc while 21pc were opposed. Another 22pc said they neither agreed nor disagreed, with 5pc saying they didn’t know.

Even amongst backers of the eco-focused Green Party, supporters for North Sea drilling narrowly outnumbered opponents by 38pc to 33pc.

Support amongst Conservative and Reform voters topped 70pc.

Some 49pc of Labour voters also indicated they supported onshore fracking of oil and gas – although just 40pc were happy for it to take place in their area.

Lawrence Newport, the chief executive of Looking for Growth, said: “There has been no government plan to reduce bills, have abundant energy and stable long-term power.

“Instead, politicians have adopted policies that have left the lives of millions across the country at the mercy of international events.

“When successive governments give in to the consultants, lawyers and lobbyists, the energy bills of the British people pay the price.

“This new polling shows that voters of all parties want a broken system fixed and to be prioritised again.”

Looking for Growth’s poll also asked voters about nuclear power and found that 61pc supported it, compared to 14pc who opposed it.

The debate over North Sea drilling has intensified in recent weeks following the US-Iran conflict, which sent energy prices soaring.

The RAC said on Tuesday the crisis had pushed the cost of filling up a typical family car with diesel beyond £100 for the first time since 2022.

The cost of filling up a petrol car has similarly increased by around £11, bringing the total to £85.

Most supporters of increasing domestic oil and gas production say it is unlikely to directly lower costs, as both commodities are priced on international markets, but that it could improve energy security, generate higher tax revenues and protect jobs.

Mr Miliband has introduced a ban on new drilling in the North Sea and has so far resisted pressure to rethink his position.

Speaking to Labour MPs this month, he reportedly said: “Gas is bought and sold on the international market, so whether it comes from the North Sea or is imported, it is charged at the same price.”

He went on to praise the amount of money raised by a renewable energy auction earlier this year, saying technology such as wind turbines and solar panels was “homegrown power that we can rely on”.

The Conservatives accused the Energy Secretary of dogma and called on the Government to cut taxes on domestic energy production.

Claire Coutinho, the shadow energy secretary, said earlier this week: “It is unforgivable that Labour are continuing with Ed Miliband’s mad plan to shut down our own energy supplies in the North Sea in the middle of an energy crisis.

“Keir Starmer is too weak to stand up to Ed Miliband, who has allowed radical activists to make us weaker, poorer and more dependent on foreign imports with higher emissions through endless lawfare.”

A Government spokesman said: “Regardless of where it comes from, oil and gas is sold on international markets, which set the price for British billpayers – making us a price taker.

“The only way to truly protect ourselves from these price spikes is to get off the rollercoaster of fossil fuel markets.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Britain heading for recession, Morgan Stanley warns
data.description:
The UK is heading for a recession if the energy price shock continues, according to a Wall Street bank.

Morgan Stanley said the Bank of England could raise interest rates if energy prices fail to come down, which would be likely to cause an economic downturn by the end of 2026.

Bruna Skarica, chief UK economist at Morgan Stanley, said Britain would face “a pronounced UK recession at the turn of the year” if energy prices stay at recent highs in the coming months and borrowing costs rise.

The war in Iran has caused oil and gas prices to soar in recent weeks, sparking fears over inflationary pressures and the impact on the UK economy.

The fallout from the Middle East conflict led to the Institute of Grocery Distribution warning on Wednesday that food inflation could hit 8pc by June 2026 if the oil shock persists. 

That is more than double the current rate of 3.6pc.

Huw Pill, the chief economist at the Bank of England, said on Tuesday that “there are limits to what the MPC [Monetary Policy Committee] can do” to protect households from the energy price shock.

However, he stressed that Threadneedle Street “stands ready” to raise interest rates if the conflict in the Middle East drags on.

“The fog of uncertainty in which we always operate ​cannot be an excuse for inaction,” he said in a speech on Tuesday.

Mr Pill’s intervention comes as a growing number of economists warn that the energy crisis threatens to push the UK into a downturn.

Simon French, the chief economist at City stockbroker Panmure Liberum, said a recession in the final six months of the year was now “a real possibility”.

He added: “Events in the Middle East can, of course, reverse, but the UK economy’s soft underbelly has been revealed. It cannot be unseen.”

Thomas Pugh, the chief economist at RSM UK, a tax and consulting firm, said: “Everything depends on how energy prices move going forward, but we now expect growth of around 0.5pc this year with a decent chance of a recession.”

The war in Iran triggered the sharpest rise in factory costs since Black Wednesday in 1992, when Britain withdrew the pound from the European Exchange Rate Mechanism, triggering a huge sell-off of sterling that led to soaring interest rates.

The jump in factory input prices recorded by S&P Global in March underlines the effect of the Middle East conflict on manufacturers, which are heavily reliant on fuel and energy-intensive materials.

Both oil and gas prices have surged since the war broke out late last month, as the closure of the Strait of Hormuz has strangled fuel supplies.

Echoes of Black Wednesday

Chris Williamson, of S&P Global, said: “Inflationary pressures have surged higher on the back of rising energy prices and fractured supply chains.

“The acceleration in cost growth in the manufacturing sector was especially severe, being the sharpest since the depreciation of sterling following Black Wednesday.”

The comparison with Black Wednesday will worry investors. At the time, then-chancellor Norman Lamont withdrew the currency from the European Exchange Rate Mechanism after failing to maintain the value of the pound.

The move led to sterling plunging by 25pc against the US dollar and 15pc against the German mark, with market panic prompting the Bank of England to raise interest rates from 10pc to 15pc.

Growth stalls as inflation rises

Alongside a rapid increase in manufacturing costs, Tuesday’s closely watched S&P survey also revealed that private sector activity in Britain fell to a six-month low with a score of 51, just above the 50-point mark indicating no growth.

Mr Williamson said the conflict in the Middle East had “hit the UK economy in March, stalling growth while driving inflation sharply higher”.

He added: “The full impact on inflation and economic growth depends not just on the duration of the war but also on the length of disruptions to energy markets and shipping.”

Meanwhile, in the US, business output slumped to an 11-month low in March, according to the S&P Global flash US purchasing managers’ index (PMI).

Companies raised their sale prices at the fastest pace since August 2022 and began cutting staff as they grappled with supply-chain delays and higher energy costs.

Mr Williamson said the data indicated that consumer price inflation will surge to 4pc, up from the current 2.8pc.

He said: “Companies are reporting a hit to demand from the additional uncertainty and cost of living impact generated by the conflict.

“Travel, transport and tourism related issues are compounded by financial market jitters and affordability constraints, notably including concern over the impact of higher interest rates, surging energy prices and supply-chain delays.”

The war has sent US borrowing costs soaring, with both two-year and ten-year treasury yields rising at their sharpest rate since October 2024 this month. 

Separate figures on Tuesday revealed that, in Britain, business optimism has tumbled to its lowest level since last June.

Jake Finney, a senior economist at PwC, said: “Private sector growth slowed considerably in March as events in the Middle East clouded the economic outlook. Demand has weakened, cost pressures have picked up, and the pace of job cuts has accelerated.”

Around a quarter of manufacturers said they had experienced longer delivery times from suppliers, causing some companies to build up stock in case of supply chain disruption.

Alongside cost pressures, businesses have warned that poor economic conditions weighed on retail sales in March.

Figures from the Confederation of British Industry showed that retail sales volumes fell at their fastest pace since the pandemic for the month so far, as cost of living fears caused shoppers to rein in spending.

It marks the fastest decline since April 2020, when many stores were closed during the first pandemic lockdown.

data.title: Honda and Sony scrap £70,000 electric car in last-minute about-turn
data.description:
Honda and Sony have abruptly cancelled plans for a £70,000 electric vehicle (EV) amid a slowdown in demand.

The two Japanese giants said on Wednesday they would scrap the launch of Afeela, an electric sedan that had been scheduled to go on sale later this year.

The duo had established a joint venture, known as Sony Honda Mobility, four years ago in an effort to tap into rising demand for battery-powered cars. The Afeela was scheduled to be built at a plant in Ohio, with production trials starting last year.

However, the cancellation is a surprise because the carmakers had been so close to launching the vehicle.

Afeela showrooms had been open for customer demonstrations and sales were expected to begin in the US in the coming months.

At the Consumer Electronics Show in Las Vegas, just weeks ago, they also revealed plans for a new SUV and claimed its new vehicles would “evolve” modern cars from “driver-centric machines into intelligent partners”.

‘Challenging earnings situation’

Honda disclosed a $15.7bn (£11.7bn) write-down on its EV division this month, blaming a slowdown in demand for battery-powered vehicles in the US.

Honda said it had invested heavily in electric cars as “striving for carbon neutrality is a responsibility Honda, as a manufacturer of mobility products, must fulfil for the future”.

But it admitted its cars business division had “fallen into an extremely challenging earnings situation”.

The Japanese business said Donald Trump’s tariffs had hit the profitability of its vehicle sales in America, while a decision by the president to scrap EV incentives had dampened consumer demand.

It also warned of rising competition from cheap Chinese rivals that offer greater value to consumers.

It announced it had cancelled three of its own EVs that had been planned for the US market, and this week confirmed the end of its Afeela project with Sony.

Sony Honda Mobility said on Wednesday that Honda’s “reassessment of its automobile electrification strategy” had “fundamentally altered” plans to develop and launch the Afeela.

It said the venture’s owners had agreed to launch a review of the plans for Sony Honda Mobility.

Honda, Japan’s second-biggest carmaker, has repeatedly lowered its ambitions for EVs as it battles waning demand and competition from China, switching instead to launching new hybrid models to appeal to more drivers.

It also cut its EV sales target last May from 30pc of vehicles to 20pc. Toshihiro Mibe, Honda’s chief executive, said at the time it would also reduce investment in electrification by about three trillion Japanese yen (£14bn) by 2031.

data.title: Making bombs won’t save Volkswagen
data.description:
It summons up dark memories. It may prove ethically troubling, both for its workforce and for its local community.

As reports emerge that Volkswagen may start making parts for the Israeli Iron Dome, lots of difficult questions are being asked about the pivot from making cars to manufacturing military equipment.

However, there is a more serious issue, both for VW and for the rest of Europe’s struggling auto industry.

What makes them think they will be any good at it?

Defence is a high-tech, fiercely competitive industry and not one that anyone can easily conquer.

VW certainly needs to try something. What was once the biggest auto company in the world and the emblem of Germany’s post-war economic miracle is in deep trouble.

Soaring energy costs in Germany have been a huge burden alongside the European Union’s blundering determination to impose a ban on new petrol and diesel cars in the 2030s.

Over the last five years, VW’s share price has fallen by more than 60pc and it has started to cut its workforce. Its net profits fell by 44pc last year. The company had planned to become a major manufacturer of electric vehicles but the new Chinese players appear to have that market sewn up with cheaper – and often better – models. The outlook is grim.

Its solution appears to be a pivot to the defence industry.

While VW has publicly ruled out making weapons, reports emerged this week that its Osnabrück plant, currently threatened with closure, may start making parts for the Iron Dome anti-missile system produced by Israel’s Rafael Advanced Defence Systems.

There were reports last year that Rheinmetall, Germany’s largest defence company, could take over the plant instead. We will see in the next few weeks what happens.

On one level, switching from cars to tanks and guns might make a lot of sense. Defence is booming, especially in Germany where the government is spending tens of billions of euros on re-equipping its armed forces. With the war in Iran, demand is only going to grow in the next few years.

Every government in the world will be looking at installing anti-missile shields – apart from the UK, where we will probably spend the money on extra welfare instead – as well as re-stocking arsenals that are already looking badly depleted.

Who knows, VW might set a template for the entire industry with the likes of BMW, Stellantis (the owner of Fiat and Citroën) and Renault all moving into defence manufacturing as well.

Even Britain’s Jaguar Land Rover could opt to go back to its roots – at least for its Land Rover unit – and start making military off-roaders instead of plush SUVs for the school run in the world’s more prosperous suburbs. It could be the saviour of an industry that looks as if it is heading into terminal trouble.

Here’s the problem, however. It will be a lot harder than it looks.

There may be some superficial similarities. An auto plant needs to bash a lot of metal, just as a defence factory does. It has to keep costs down and the lines rolling, and it needs to be able to put together a lot of complex parts very efficiently.

In a modest way, the auto manufacturers might be able to make a success of it, at least as subcontractors of the major established giants.

Even so, let’s be honest here. This is not the Second World War, when every available scrap of industrial capacity had to be converted to armaments manufacturing to keep the front lines supplied and every production site from bicycle factories to food plants suddenly found themselves making bullets and bombs.

Modern defence manufacturing involves a very different skill-set. It is high-precision and high-tech, and it involves vast quantities of research and development, as well as massive investments in a skilled workforce.

Just take a look at the world’s top defence manufacturers.

The top three are all American, led by Lockheed Martin, RTX and Northrop Grumman, with Britain’s BAE Systems coming in at number four and Italy’s Leonardo at number 11 in the global market.

These are all companies with long and deep histories in the industry, with established contracts and with relationships with the military that stretch back for generations. They spend billions of dollars developing the best kit that can be made and testing it in the most extreme conditions.

The idea that VW or any of the other traditional auto manufacturers can suddenly break into that list is laughable. It is not going to happen. They don’t have the skills or the expertise, nor are the production lines set up in the right way.

Even if they were, it is a fiercely competitive industry, with their customers largely national governments who are going to spend their budgets on domestic manufacturers instead of in other countries.

If there is space for new players, they are far more likely to be from Ukraine – where local manufacturers have been forced to become world leaders in cheap, effective drones – or from Israel – where they have been forced to become experts in missile protection – than from Germany, France or indeed Britain. It isn’t going to happen.

Europe’s traditional auto giants certainly need to do something. With each month that passes, it becomes increasingly clear that attempts to replace petrol cars with battery-powered ones has been catastrophically mismanaged.

Bureaucrats and politicians appear to care more about grandstanding to the green lobby than preserving vital jobs and manufacturing capacity. It is hard to see how any of them are going to survive the Chinese onslaught on the industry.

Even so, these companies are kidding themselves if they think that switching to making bullets and bombs will save them.

It won’t. All it will do is guarantee terminal decline.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Dithering Labour is crippling Britain’s defence renaissance
data.description:
For want of a nail, the saying goes, the battle was lost.

When a tiny defence firm goes bust, Britain falls further off the map of global power.

MPs were recently given a vivid example of how Government dithering was causing lasting damage.

MTE in Slaithwaite, West Yorkshire, was just one of thousands of engineering companies that are invisible to policymakers in Westminster. It performed specialised hardening and treatment of turbine blades but was forced to close its doors.

As Andrew Kinniburgh, the director general of members’ association Make UK Defence explained, the blades journey several times between Italy, the UK, Ireland and the US before returning here for finishing.

Small companies perform much of this work. Each closure makes the nation less attractive for business.

“The inevitable consequence of that little 30-person company going bust is that the company procuring those blades will say, ‘Actually, it is a bit easier just to do it in the States because we can get the machining done there and the British company – which is fantastic and a world leader in what it does – is just a bit of an inconvenience,’” he says.

A ripple has a “tsunami wave of impact on the supply chain”, he adds.

One reason is Ed Miliband’s green ideology. High energy costs are “slowly deindustrialising” our manufacturing capacity, says Kinniburgh. But this is not why MPs wanted to hear from industry.

The Government has created a fresh crisis by delaying crucial spending details. The defence investment plan, originally scheduled for last autumn, is eight months overdue.

“Defence is a strange market in which the core customer is [the] Government – so how [the] Government behaves has a huge impact on the sector,” says Emma Baker, an adviser for ADS Group.

Samira Braund, the company’s defence director, told MPs that the ecosystem was paralysed: “With us delaying and prevaricating, we are basically telling those companies: perhaps you should invest [in] someone else.”

This is a completely unforced error and it’s snuffing out growing optimism in the defence engineering supply chains.

With national security now a higher priority than it has been for decades, Britain has the talent and capability to take advantage of it and grow.

There are good reasons why this optimism was well-founded.

Firstly, the engineer-entrepreneur is perhaps the most underrated figure in Britain’s economic history and hasn’t gone away.

“The brainpower in our engineering is phenomenal,” says Marcus Gibson, the founder of a research agency which monitors Britain’s small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). “We have a quality that the French and Germans can’t match.”

This is not an idle boast.

The privately held family firm of Martin-Baker, which makes ejector seats, is a global leader with 70pc of the military market.

It has achieved the seemingly impossible: beating out American defence contractors on their home turf. The US uses “military standards” to tilt the playing field in favour of domestic suppliers.

Another world-beater is Accuracy International, which has built its rifles for demanding combat conditions. It’s one of very few firms that manufacture their components in-house and even “shave their own barrels”.

With fewer than a hundred employees, these are not headline-grabbing or heavyweight firms – although with more favourable capital conditions, we could have many more of them.

The major manufacturers such as BAE and Rolls-Royce have thousands of small precision engineering shops in their supply chains.

It’s such SMEs that kept the British economy ticking along in the 1930s, says Gibson, stopping a recession from becoming a deeper depression as they found markets for products such as new electrical motors.

British engineers have other advantages too. People like doing business with British firms, I am regularly told, as they leave some margin on the table. Fair play is not a myth.

They can also improvise brilliantly, something needed more than ever in a fast-moving field such as drones and uncrewed vehicles.

Five drone firms have set up in Swindon, Wiltshire, bringing know-how from the front in the Donbas. So the dithering over spending could not have come at a worse time for industry.

“The very cautious approach to procurement that we see coming out of the Government now is really not helpful,” says Trevor Taylor, a research fellow at the Royal United Services Institute, a think tank.

“Longer term contracts can encourage investment by SMEs: six years rather than one plus one, with an invitation to recompete.”

The Ministry of Defence knows what can be fixed, but there’s a long way to go.

“If you’re an innovative company do you want to be subjected to going into a competition? There’s a risk your expertise will leak out, it’s costly and it’s time-consuming,” says Taylor.

And if you’re anointed as a sole supplier, there’s a dedicated quango set up to pore over the contracts.

Small British engineering firms such as Cheshire’s Didsbury Engineering are being snapped up by Swedish firms, a largely unreported trend. Under the “Swedish model” of capital investment, they will be in a good place: benefitting from patient long-term planning.

Although Sweden only joined Nato two years ago, it has doubled its defence budget in three years to 2.8pc of GDP and its engineering sector will reap the rewards.

Our British firms, who must sell up simply to grow the business, must look on such commitment to their craft with envy.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Sir David Kirch, reclusive collector of rare banknotes who gave £100 million to the elderly of Jersey
data.description:
​Sir David Kirch, who has died 89, was a London property speculator who assembled what was thought to be Britain’s largest collection of provincial banknotes and the world’s most extensive personal collection of airship memorabilia; but he sold it all in the process of becoming one of the nation’s most generous philanthropists.

In the 1960s the reclusive Kirch had amassed a property empire that at one point included swathes of Kensington and Earl’s Court, as well as the Shaftesbury Theatre in the West End – which he threatened to demolish. Tall and elegant, he was viewed as an enigma. “Often at weekends, he takes his mother down to his cottage at Shoreham-on-Sea, otherwise he sits alone in his 15-room mansion in Hyde Park Gate and watches colour TV after eating his solitary dinner served by his Spanish butler,” reported the Evening Standard. In 1973 he settled as a tax exile in Jersey.

On reaching “three score and ten” in 2006, he decided to mark the milestone by distributing £100 Co-op vouchers at Christmas to every islander aged 70 and over – a commitment he honoured even on discovering that there would be 9,000 recipients. “I am fond of old people because I’m old myself,” he explained. He was delighted to learn that one pensioner had been able to buy a microwave oven, and called it “Mr Kirch”, so that “every time it pinged they thought of me”.

He repeated the scheme in subsequent years – later upping the voucher to £110 – and established a charitable trust to provide homes for pensioners on Jersey. In 2012, on being diagnosed with cancer, he announced that he would leave £100 million, the bulk of his fortune, to the elderly of Jersey.

Observing that his two happiest encounters with money were, first, in the making of it, and second, in the giving away, he proceeded to liquidate his assets to fund his trust. He sold his Rolls-Royce in favour of a Mini, and auctioned his collection of nearly 4,000 English provincial banknotes issued by defunct 19th-century institutions such as Scarborough Old Bank, Glastonbury & Shepton Mallet Bank and many others, their £5 and £10 denominations often fetching four-figure prices. He also sold a more unusual collection of “skit notes” – historic joke banknotes with political or satirical themes, issued by entities such as “the Bank of Love”.

His 15-ton collection of airship relics, sold in 2012, included medals awarded to the crew of the R101 which crashed in France in 1930 and a singed beer bottle salvaged from the wreckage of the Hindenburg in 1937.

David Roderick Kirch was born in Wimbledon on July 23 1936, the son of Leonard Kirch and his wife Margaret. His father had done well as a wholesale meat trader, but chose to keep travelling by train at six o’clock to Smithfield because, if the other traders saw his chauffeured Rolls-Royce, “he’d never get the price he wanted”.

At Tonbridge School, David demonstrated a flair for selling stamps. His commanding officer on National Service agreed that he would make a better businessman than a soldier, and since “I wasn’t clever enough to go to university,” as Kirch recalled, he found a job in the Lloyd’s insurance market.

On the untimely death of his father, aged 59, Kirch inherited £5,000 and launched himself in the meat trade. When his firm was taken over by a Dutch company, who wanted his Kingston cold store rather than his business, he realised that property was a better bet than meat, and bought a stake in a Hampshire estate agency.

He joined forces with his brother Peter in 1962, and they borrowed heavily to buy up chunks of west London, specialising in properties with sitting tenants that could be converted to more lucrative short-term lets. One flat in Courtfield Road was rented to the Rolling Stones; the police stripped off the oak panelling in the drawing room during a drugs bust.

Their first big gamble, as Kirch told an interviewer, was to buy 23 houses in Earl’s Court for more than £250,000 – 90 per cent of it borrowed – though they were producing rental income of only £1,000 a year. “Once you had dealt with sums of a quarter of a million,” he observed, “things do become much easier.”

In the early 1970s the Kirch brothers bought into a quoted company, Peureula (Sumatra) Rubber Estates, which they renamed Peureula Investments and through which they amassed unpayable debts of some £35 million – chiefly to Keyser Ullman, one of the fringe banks heaviest hit by the property crash of 1973. “They can only ever hope to get their money back,” Kirch said in 1976, “which they won’t.”

Having lost nearly everything, David Kirch later rebuilt his portfolio to a value at one point estimated at £250 million.

His enthusiasms as a collector ranged from glass eyebaths to memorabilia from the Crystal Palace (which he dreamed of rebuilding) and a bench that once belonged to Lord Lucan. One particular indulgence was cars; another was gambling. At the Casino de la Fôret at Le Touquet, Ian Fleming challenged him to a race back to their hotel in their sports cars.

Being a covert homosexual, Kirch recalled, was another form of gambling: “Sex was that much more exciting when it was illegal.” He regretted never having children, and observed that his life had spanned an era when his friend, Lord Montagu, could be imprisoned for homosexuality, to the legalisation of gay marriage and adoption by same-sex couples.

In 2013 David Kirch was appointed KBE.

His long-term partner Serge Cuhat died in 2009 in an Alpine ballooning accident.

Sir David Kirch, born July 23 1936, died February 28 2026​

data.title: ‘Rogue builders ran off with £200k and left me homeless’
data.description:
Have you lost money to rogue builders? Email money@telegraph.co.uk

Steve Higgs never envisioned that six years after buying his forever home in Surrey, he would find himself living in a plasterboard shack and £200,000 out of pocket.

But when the building company he hired to redevelop his £625,000 property claimed it had gone bust and abruptly ceased all communication with him, he was left with nothing but foundations and a few half-finished walls despite handing over £170,000 for the work.

Mr Higgs, 39, had set out to demolish and rebuild his five-bedroom property in Horley, after he was advised that it had serious asbestos issues.

Since early 2022, he has been forced to live in a makeshift cabin on the grounds of his three-acre estate as his property became uninhabitable.

He last had contact with the building company responsible in January 2025 when they told him they were experiencing “financial troubles” after his home had been demolished.

Mr Higgs, who was paralysed from the chest down in a motorcycle accident when he was 26, said the ordeal had left him emotionally drained, homeless and facing a big hole in his finances.

He said: “It has played havoc on my mental health. They’ve already wasted four years of my life and £170,000. I can’t sit on my hands in this little box any more. I’ve got no windows. It’s just plasterboard walls. I’m literally just living in a cell of my own making.”

It comes as Britain faces a plague of rogue builders that is costing homeowners and the economy billions of pounds a year.

In the past five years alone, homeowners lost an estimated £14.3bn to cowboy builders, while a fear of being ripped off has cost the economy a further £10bn in lost jobs, according to the Federation of Master Builders (FMB) trade body.

Jeremy Gray, of the FMB, said the cost of living crisis had created the “perfect environment” for sham building companies to proliferate.

He said: “There are no barriers to entry so it’s easy to come in and scam people. The rogue builder will come along, undercut a decent builder and then, because people are short of cash, they will go for the cheap option. And it’s only getting worse.”

Mr Higgs paid the building company £113,000 to demolish and rebuild his five-bedroom house in Horley, Surrey, after he was advised that issues with asbestos and rotten timber frames made it more cost-effective than his original plan to renovate and extend.

He paid an additional £55,000 for roof truss installation and spent £30,000 on buying materials directly from a roofing supplier.

He said the building company had a strong online presence, including a professional website, Facebook reviews and a range of photos displaying previous work, as well as a branded fleet of vans. Up to four labourers a day would appear on site.

However, when Mr Higgs raised concerns about the quality of bricklaying and incorrect proportions for the new foundations, he was told, in correspondence seen by The Telegraph, that the company had “run into financial troubles”.

Despite numerous calls and emails the company stopped responding to queries about when the remedial work would be carried out.

After a few months, he hired a lawyer to draft a letter before action for £7,000, only to be advised that it could cost up to £120,000 pursuing the claim in a civil court.

He was told that even if he won there was no guarantee he would be able to recoup what he paid out as the company was claiming financial distress.

‘Hopeless’ legal process

Currently all builders carrying out work on private homes are regulated by the Consumer Rights Act 2015. It sets out the standards that consumers can expect when a trader supplies building works, and statutory remedies if those rights are breached.

However, if the company is declared insolvent, options for reclaiming money can be extremely limited and outcomes are typically poor.

Mark Garnier, shadow economic secretary, who has campaigned for more stringent controls on building firms, has previously called the legal process for bringing rogue traders to justice “hopeless”.

If the work was paid for using a credit card, homeowners can claim back up to £30,000 under Section 75. But many rogue traders encourage cash-in-hand payments at a discount to avoid creating a paper trail.

Paula Higgins, at the Homeowners’ Alliance trade body, said “phoenix companies” – whereby the owner of an insolvent business simply rebrands under a different name – was an ongoing problem in the construction sector.

She said: “If you investigate certain directors, they will be setting companies up and closing them down all the time – [the regulation] around it is very weak.”

Despite calls from industry leaders for tighter regulation such as a mandatory national licensing scheme ensuring minimum competency for all builders, the Government has dragged its heels on introducing any new legislation.

Mr Gray said the Government didn’t appear to have any plans to implement controls in the construction sector despite “zero protections” for consumers against rogue builders.

He said: “Anyone can call themselves a builder because they’re not regulated. In certain circumstances you can go through the court, but ultimately if the company folds there’s nothing you can do. You can’t claim anything back. You just have to go to the drawing board.”

To avoid falling prey to rogue builders, Ms Higgins said the most important steps were to get a contract in place, avoid paying for projects upfront and in cash, and to carry out extensive research into the company beyond its own website and social media channels.

She also warned against any company that is available at short notice. “The best builders are the ones you have to wait for,” she said, adding that everyone should look on Companies House to see for how long a business has been operating.

For word of mouth recommendations, ensure their previous work is similar to what you are asking for and check previous projects yourself, she added.

Mr Higgs said he had been quoted £23,000 for remedial work, including widening the existing foundation to the correct size. The rest of the project is expected to cost another £90,000, plus VAT.

“It’s pretty much looking at the same figures again,” he said.

Mr Higgs receives an income from a personal injury trust, but said the financial impact would be long-lasting. “It’s definitely thrown a spanner in the works,” he said. “It’s turned what was meant to be a five-year build plan into a 10-year plan.”

A spokesman for the Department for Business and Trade said: “Rogue builders are a scourge for hard-working families. Anyone who is affected can contact their local authority who have new powers to hand out tougher fines against businesses who rip off consumers.”

data.title: Five changes every landlord must prepare for in 2026
data.description:
Britain’s landlords must brace for a year of new rules, as the Government plans to enforce a raft of regulations and additional tax requirements on a sector already burdened by rising costs and tighter rules.

While the Government insists the changes are about raising standards and strengthening protection for tenants, landlords warn that it could lead to worse outcomes for renters if enough landlords decide to leave the market entirely.

Here, Telegraph Money sets out what’s coming for landlords this year and what you need to do to get prepared.

Making Tax Digital

When? From April 6.

What does it mean for landlords?

Making Tax Digital (MTD) will require certain taxpayers to report their income and expenses to HMRC four times a year, in addition to annual tax returns. This doesn’t only affect landlords – other taxpayers, such as the self-employed, are also affected.

For landlords specifically, MTD applies to those whose qualifying income exceeds £50,000, but this income doesn’t have to be from property. For example, if you have income from property and self-employment that is more than £50,000 combined, then you would need to register.

Those required to take action should have already received a letter from HMRC, but, as always, the onus is on you to check whether you should be signed up.

Heather Powell, of accountancy firm Blick Rothenberg, said: “The first return, reporting rental income and expenses for the three months from April 6, 2026 to July 5, 2026 has to be filed by Aug 7, 2026, with filing from ‘compliant software’ or bridging software that picks up the financial information from an Excel spreadsheet.”

As a landlord, you need to be on your game, as there is an initial £200 fine after two late filings.

While HMRC says this new filing regime will “increase the accuracy of filings”, the shift will undoubtedly feel daunting for the many landlords juggling multiple properties and income streams.

What’s more, the submissions must be made using MTD-compatible software. There is a long list of companies offering options, many of which require payment. Whether landlords try to grapple with this on their own or with the help of an accountant, they’re likely to incur additional costs.

The key right now is to find out whether you’re affected and, if you are, get your accounting records in order and start moving towards digital record-keeping.

Renters’ Rights Act reforms come into force

When? May 1.

What does it mean for landlords?

After becoming law in November, the first phase of the Renters’ Rights Act (RRA) 2025 reforms will come into effect from the start of May – and there’s a lot to get your head around.

Michael Dear, landlord insurance specialist at insurer Hiscox, said: “The RRA brings changes such as limits on rent increases and the end of fixed-term tenancies, which aim to make renting fairer.”

In addition, there are new rules on “no-fault” evictions and tenant requests to keep pets.

With only a few months to go, awareness is believed to be relatively high among landlords, but concerns remain that actual understanding is patchy.

Sián Hemming-Metcalfe, operations director at Inventory Base, a supplier, said: “Most landlords know the RRA is coming, but far fewer have grasped what it means in practice. This is not a cosmetic change; it rewrites how possession works, how rent is handled, how tenancies are structured and what landlords will need to evidence if something goes wrong.”

As such, there are a few things you should prepare now, if you haven’t already.

  • Before the abolition of Section 21: Review the new rules in detail, update your tenancy documentation, and potentially seek legal advice if you are likely to need to evict tenants.
  • Prepare for the end of fixed-term tenancies: All existing and new fixed-term assured shorthold tenancies must convert into rolling tenancies, so think now about how you need to change your existing processes and update your contract paperwork.
  • Prepare for rent increases being restricted to once per year: Now is a good time to assess how much rent you’re charging. Aneisha Beveridge, head of research at Hamptons, said: “With rent increases due to be open to tribunal challenge from May, many landlords are using the months ahead to ensure their rents are aligned as closely as possible to market levels.”
  • Plan for new pet rules: Requests to keep pets cannot be turned down “unreasonably” starting in May. Before this, it’s prudent to consider any practical adjustments that might better protect your property from pet damage, update your inventories and perhaps your tenancy agreement paperwork and insurance policy to make sure you’re covered.

For more information on what the new law means for landlords, see our full guide to the RRA.

Provide information to existing tenants

When? By May 31 to avoid penalties.

What does it mean for landlords?

This is part of the RRA, but has its own separate deadline, so we thought it was worth pointing out separately.

The requirement is for landlords to issue tenants with key information in writing, setting out the details of the tenancy, including – but not restricted to:

  • The property address
  • The tenancy start date
  • The rent amount and when it is due
  • A statement to confirm that any proposed rent rises will be in accordance with section 13 of the Housing Act 1988
  • Details of the tenancy deposit
  • Requirements should apply if either party wishes to end the tenancy.

New tenancies made on or after May 1 must include this information as standard. If you have existing tenancies – ones already in place on May 1 – you will need to issue this information to them if you haven’t already.

If it’s already part of their tenancy agreement, you must instead give your tenants a government-produced information sheet telling them how changes being made by the RRA might affect the tenancy. This is expected to be published this month and can be sent as a hard or digital copy.

Don’t ignore this, as failure to comply will be considered a breach of the act and can mean hefty penalties, starting from £4,000.

You can read the full government guidance for landlords here.

Further ahead: New Energy Performance Certificate metrics

When? Had been planned for the end of 2026, but recently delayed to 2027.

What does it mean for landlords?

Under current plans, landlords must ensure that all rental properties have an Energy Performance Certificate (EPC) rating of “C” or above by 2030 or spend £10,000 to try.

However, the existing EPC system is also due for reform, with the single metric to be replaced by four new metrics, which had been expected in the latter half of this year. The Government has since announced the new system will not be introduced until 2027. 

Once introduced, landlords will have to achieve a rating of “C” in the property’s “fabric performance” (which considers insulation) and least two of the other three new green metrics.

The aim is to help landlords (as well as other homeowners and renters) understand certain headline measures in their properties. The assessments will also cover the heating system (encouraging the adoption of heat pumps), the building’s “smart-readiness” (the potential to integrate smart technology) and practical steps to improve homes and reduce energy costs.

The idea is that this will provide a broader picture of a property’s energy profile.

The Government estimates that the average landlord will need to spend £5,400 to meet the standard, so if you know your rental property or properties have a poor EPC rating, it’s worth planning now.

You may need to start setting money aside to fund improvements and start looking into grants you can apply for (such as the Boiler Upgrade Scheme if you are considering a heat pump).

Jason Harris-Cohen, managing director at LandlordBuyer, said: “This represents a significant retrofit challenge for landlords, many of whom face difficult decisions between absorbing costly upgrade expenses, raising rents, or exiting the market altogether.”

Minimum qualifications for letting agents

When? TBC.

What does it mean for landlords?

Last year, the Government set out proposals to introduce mandatory qualifications for letting agents to raise professional standards across the sector.

The plans include a new code of practice establishing minimum requirements for all residential property agents, including estate agents, managing agents and letting agents.

As yet, no date has been confirmed. However, with many landlords reliant on agents to meet increasingly complex obligations, there is growing pressure for clarity on when the reforms will take effect.

Until this happens, any landlords using letting agents to manage their properties should ensure that the agents are trained and prepared for the forthcoming law changes. For instance, is there a named compliance specialist available to answer questions about the new rules? Are there facilities for staff to be trained on the changes and any updates? Are they updating their services and terms in accordance with the new rules?

If not, you might want to consider moving to a different company, as a letting agent’s failure to follow the new law will embroil you as the landlord.

data.title: Heathrow blocked from raising passenger charges to pay for £10bn upgrade
data.description:
Heathrow’s proposal to raise passenger fees to pay for a £10bn upgrade has been rejected by the industry regulator amid growing divisions about funding for the airport.

The Civil Aviation Authority (CAA) said on Tuesday that average charges per passenger should rise from £28.40 to just £28.80 during the next five years. Heathrow had proposed a 17pc increase in fees to £33.26, which was rejected.

It means Heathrow will only be permitted to spend £5.8bn upgrading the site under the CAA plan, including capital investment in the airport’s electrical network.

Heathrow had proposed a £10bn upgrade but the watchdog urged the airport to focus instead on improving its resilience and quality of service for passengers.

The airport is allowed to recoup the money it spends on airport improvements through the landing fees it charges airlines, which are then often passed on to customers in the form of higher ticket prices.

Heathrow’s proposed higher fees had led to pushback from airlines including IAG, the owner of British Airways, and Virgin Atlantic, which warned such a move would lead to higher ticket prices.

The airport had asked for permission from the regulator to spend £10bn on upgrades to manage an extra 10 million passengers by 2031.

Heathrow was forced to close temporarily and cancel more than 1,300 flights last year after a fire at a nearby electricity substation caused a power cut at the airport.

After the announcement, Thomas Woldbye, Heathrow’s chief executive, said: “We will now review the CAA’s initial proposal in detail to fully understand the implications for delivering the innovation, progress and improvements customers expect.

“On the face of it, the CAA’s proposal may force choices that create trade-offs for service and delay delivery.”

Airlines have opposed previous attempts to increase landing fees. Heathrow was forced to cut its fees for 2024 after demand recovered quicker than expected from the pandemic.

A Virgin Atlantic spokesman said: “These initial proposals for the next five years are a more reasonable starting point than Heathrow’s business plan but the final settlement must prevent passengers paying for an unjustified level of shareholder returns.”

The five-year plan for fees does not include a third runway. The CAA will publish its final proposals in November, with a final decision expected in April 2027.

Selina Chadha, a director at the CAA, said: “Our primary duty is to protect consumers and at the heart of today’s proposals is doing the right thing for passengers using Heathrow airport while supporting sustainable growth, investment and efficiency.

“Our proposals for the airport charges levied by Heathrow on airlines strike the right balance between keeping passenger prices fair while enabling the airport to make the investment needed to improve services for the future.”

data.title: £50bn construction giant abandons London stock market
data.description:
Building materials giant CRH is to quit the London Stock Exchange (LSE) in a further blow to Britain’s standing as a global financial hub.

CRH, which is worth nearly £54bn, said it had decided to exit the UK market because it was hamstrung by high regulatory costs and low levels of trading activity.

The firm’s shares will now be traded only in New York, where they had been listed in addition to London since September 2023.

The move serves as yet another setback for London’s struggling stock market, with CRH joining a string of high-profile companies that have defected to New York in search of higher valuations.

Online payments company Wise, worth £12bn, last year transferred its main share listing to the US, saying the move would allow better access to the world’s most liquid stock market.

Meanwhile, AstraZeneca said in September that it would upgrade its presence on the New York exchange in what bosses described as “a global listing for global investors in a global company”.

While the company said it would maintain its London listing and remain based in the UK, the announcement has stoked fears that Britain’s biggest drugmaker and most valuable business could ultimately use the move as a stepping stone to quitting the LSE.

CRH had already declared New York as its primary listing and had been removed from the FTSE 100 index.

The company, which is based in Dublin, said a review launched last month had concluded that quitting London completely was “in the best interests of CRH and its shareholders”.

It said: “CRH carefully considered, among other factors, the level of trading activity for its ordinary shares on the LSE as well as the additional cost, regulatory and administrative obligations arising from retaining the LSE listings.”

The firm said it had asked the Financial Conduct Authority to cancel its London listing, with the move expected to be completed by Apr 20.

CRH said on switching to the dual listing three years ago that the decision had also been partly driven by a US push for onshoring manufacturing activity, something that has gathered pace under Donald Trump.

CRH, which was founded in 1970 via a merger of two Irish cement and quarrying companies, already counts North America as its biggest market.

data.title: Seven common home improvements that won’t pay off when you sell
data.description:
If you’re considering selling your home this year, you might be tempted to fix everything you can before putting it on the market.

Whether it’s replacing your outdated kitchen or getting new carpets, many sellers think a bit of investment will help secure a higher price or a quicker sale. But some fixes simply won’t pay off.

Certain improvements will waste both your time and money, and may well get replaced by the new owner as soon as they move in.

Angela Kerr, from the HomeOwners Alliance, said: “Home buyers realise a home has been loved and lived in, so will have some wear and tear. While in an ideal world, your property would look perfect, you need to prioritise spending money on essential repairs and minor redecoration that will help your home sell faster.”

To maximise value and minimise stress, knowing where to hold back on improvements may be just as useful as knowing where to take action.

Here, Telegraph Money has asked the experts which common property fixes sellers shouldn’t bother spending money on.

1. A kitchen replacement

Think twice before going all out and installing a shiny new kitchen, especially if your current one is functional.

There’s a risk you could spend thousands on this outlay shortly before selling, only to discover it does little to increase your home’s value.

Tom Evans, sales director at estate agency Purplebricks, said: “Most buyers prefer to personalise this area for themselves. As long as it works – and isn’t too shabby or flimsy – I would advise against the time and expense of upgrading.”

If you’re looking for a quick fix in the kitchen that won’t break the budget, consider replacing just the cabinet doors, handles or worktops to give your existing room a bit of a revamp.

2. Adding an extension

Another common mistake sellers make before putting their property on the market is going all out on a loft conversion or extension.

Marc von Grundherr, director of estate agency Benham and Reeves, said: “What sellers should avoid is embarking on expensive projects purely to try to increase the sale price in the short term. Major extensions, loft conversions or structural layout changes rarely make financial sense if the only goal is to sell the property immediately afterwards.”

While projects like this can add value, they can also be very pricey – not to mention time-consuming.

Mr Evans said: “An expensive conversion is unlikely to ever correspond with a particularly elevated property price. There is a risk that if you, say, spend £50,000 on the extension, it might only add £40,000 to the property.”

Another big no-no is “over-improving” the area.

Mr Evans added: “If your home is already at the top price range for your street, there is little point in putting more time and money into the property pre-sale.”

3. Redesigning the garden

If you’re considering giving your outside area an overhaul, think again.

Mr Evans said: “Another area people tend to really push the boat out on is garden design. There’s a dizzying array of things you could do to your garden, and an eye-watering sum you could plough into making it look great. But from experience, this rarely makes a difference to the asking price – or the speed or amount the property sells for.”

Of course, you shouldn’t neglect your garden, either. But rather than spending money on a redesign that isn’t worth the time or money, carrying out smaller, more manageable tasks, such as tidying lawns, weeding flower beds and repairing fences.

It’s all about “kerb appeal”, according to Dominick Brown, director of buying agency Prime Purchase.

He said: “Think about that all-important first impression or kerb appeal. Is the driveway clear? Does it need additional gravel to fill in bumps? Does the house present as well as it possibly can at first glance? See if there are any slipped tiles which need to be corrected, bins hidden, and flower beds planted or tidied.”

4. New white goods

While you might like the idea of potential buyers setting eyes on a top-of-the-range fridge, freezer or dishwasher when they come to view your home, buying these appliances could be a waste of money.

Ms Kerr said: “You’re unlikely to recoup what you’ve spent when you sell. If you need to replace an appliance because it’s broken, consider a second-hand [one] to keep costs down.”

5. A bathroom upgrade

As with your kitchen, there’s a risk that if you pour lots of cash into bathroom renovations, it could be money down the drain.

Mr Evans said: “There’s no point replacing a perfectly usable bathroom suite with an expensive one. If you do, there’s every chance the potential buyer will have different views on what their dream bathroom might be.”

As long as the taps and fixtures are working properly – and you don’t have any leaks – you are better off focusing your efforts on tasks such as deep cleaning, regrouting tiles and replacing worn flooring.

6. Expensive home improvements

While it’s not a bad idea to give your home a fresh lick of paint if needed, be sparing, as doing too much could end up putting buyers off.

Your choice of decor is important, too. Give bold colours a wide berth, and focus instead on using just a fresh coat of neutral paint to make your home feel lighter and brighter.

Mr Evans said: “Any home improvement that involves personal taste – or which is a trend – is not worth adjusting. If the imperfections are just cosmetic, and something that the buyer can live with, we would recommend leaving well alone.”

7. New carpets

If your existing carpets feel old and worn, you may be dying to refresh your home with brand new ones. But once again, this is not a sensible use of your cash.

Ms Kerr said: “Instead, for a much cheaper fix, get your carpets deep cleaned. You’ll be amazed at the difference it makes.”

You can either pay a professional to do the job for you. Or, to keep the costs down further still, you could hire the equipment and do the job yourself.

What is worth spending money on?

When preparing a home for sale, it’s important to spend time and effort on the things that genuinely reassure a buyer, rather than shelling out on upgrades that they may not like or will not be reflected in the sale price.

Mr von Grundherr said: “The priority should always be the fundamentals of the property. Issues such as damp, visible structural problems, electrical faults, heating issues or missing roof tiles are all immediate red flags because they raise concerns about the wider condition of the home.

“Even relatively minor problems, such as cracked plaster, can make house-hunters question whether there are deeper issues beneath the surface.”

Addressing these types of repairs before listing can make a big difference to buyer confidence.

Amy Reynolds, from estate agency Antony Roberts, said: “The key is to focus on issues that could derail a sale – rather than chasing perfection. If the rest of the property is in good condition, it is usually worth honing in on issues that regularly trigger down-valuations, renegotiations or even collapsed sales.”

This includes any problems that may get flagged up in a survey.

Mr Brown said: “For example, any lingering damp is likely to raise concerns for both buyers and their surveyors. Failing to remedy it will almost certainly become an issue during price negotiations or following a survey.”

Roof repairs are another no-brainer; you need to get these issues fixed as they will be visible to a buyer before they even step in the door, whether they get a building survey or not.

Remember the small ‘fixes’

If you are trying to move quickly, there are plenty of smaller tasks you can do yourself, which can improve the appeal of your home – without significant expense. Decluttering and deep cleaning are a great starting point.

Ms Reynolds said: “Focus on presentation – fresh paint, good lighting and declutter.”

Get rid of limescale, clean and repair tile grout, and wax wooden floors.

It’s not all about what a property looks like; make sure it smells good too. Ms Kerr said: “Try different products to get your bathroom to shine, get rid of odours, hang up fresh towels. This will make the place more appealing and allow potential buyers to imagine living there.”

Mr Evans added: “Whether it’s touching up scuffed paintwork, filling in small cracks in the plastering, replacing broken handles or light fittings, re-sealing bathrooms and kitchens, or deep cleaning carpets – a neutral, clean and well-maintained property will sell better than a stylish but tired one.”

If you’re not sure which jobs to prioritise before putting your home on the market, speak to an estate agent about which fixes are likely to make your home more likely to sell.

Ms Kerr said: “If you want to know if anything in your home is a potential deal breaker, then ask a good local estate agent. They will know what prospective buyers in your area are looking for.”
