data.title: ‘What does the RSC really need? A better train line to Stratford’
data.description:
I meet Daniel Evans and Tamara Harvey on the morning they announce their biggest coup to date – bringing Game of Thrones to the Stratford main stage this summer. The Mad King, a prequel play to the epic fantasy saga, has been written with the blessing and input of author George RR Martin. The news coincides, oddly, with the dramatic arrest of Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor (“Very Westeros,” Evans quips).

Have the two artistic directors of the Royal Shakespeare Company hit on the holy grail – the next Stranger Things: First Shadow, perhaps or Harry Potter and the Cursed Child? “That thinking is dangerous,” Evans, 52, counters. “The minute you think like that, you can jinx it.

“The reason we’re passionate about this is because it reads like a Shakespeare history play. It concerns leadership, succession, power, internecine fighting. George RR Martin is obsessed with Shakespeare.”

The globally successful HBO series (2011-19) was notorious for its scenes of sex and violence – it carried an 18 certificate in Britain. The prequel centres on the fabled Great Tourney [jousting tournament] at Harrenhal castle, and the tyrant king Aerys II Targaryen. “It’s going to be total theatre,” promises Evans of the production, which will be directed by Dominic Cooke (Hello, Dolly!). “There will be a content advisory, but we’re expecting it to be something the whole family can enjoy.”

The pair also tell me about the next wave of RSC programming for the autumn and winter which is announced today. This includes an all-male production of As You Like It (directed by Evans), Harriet Walter reprising her star turn as Brutus in a 90-minute version of Phyllida Lloyd’s acclaimed all-female Julius Caesar, plus a two-part adaptation of Middlemarch from Nina Raine. The year looks set to end on a swashbuckling high with The Three Musketeers, in which Dumas’s musketeers will be – gasp! – female.

Evans and Harvey, are a seamless and courteous double act. Having both enjoyed success running regional theatres (him: Sheffield and Chichester; her: Theatr Clwyd in Wales), they bonded during the stresses of the pandemic, and applied for one of the biggest jobs in British theatre on a joint ticket in 2022, succeeding Erica Whyman, who was briefly promoted from deputy after 10 years of Gregory Doran at the helm. Their tenure began properly in 2024. It’s full-on stuff, with Harvey, 48, talking about days that run from dawn to midnight, with her family commitments as a mother of two interspersed: “We often wish we could clone ourselves.”

In a departure from longstanding RSC man Doran’s methodical march through the canon, they have modified the approach so that it’s now more about bringing in diverse artistic talents with fresh, exciting ideas about what to stage, and how to stage it. As Harvey explains: “For us, there’s no point in putting on King John just because it hasn’t been done for a few years.”

The new rule is that there is no rule – anything could, conceivably, be an RSC show. While that approach might alarm traditionalists, it has reaped dividends in terms of ticket sales. More than 352,000 people attended productions in Stratford in their first year and The BFG last Christmas was the highest-grossing play in the history of the RSC. Rupert Goold’s Hamlet also played to 90 per cent capacity, while Kenneth Branagh’s imminent return to the RSC for the first time in more than 30 years in The Tempest has already sold out, just as Macbeth, starring Outlander’s Sam Heughan, did. Cyrano de Bergerac, starring Adrian Lester, is transferring to the West End this summer on the back of rave reviews.

A recent hit, Measure for Measure at Stratford, played fast and loose with the text – what do they think is acceptable in terms of tweaking texts many regard as sacrosanct? Evans tells me: “It’s OK to rewrite Shakespeare, but it’s not a free-for-all. Give us your rationale. The rule is that we’re not opposed to it. We’re not purists.” Harvey chips in with thoughts fresh from her rehearsals on Henry V, which she is about to bring to the Stratford main stage, with Alfred Enoch in the lead. “You can pick up any Arden edition and the footnotes read like a food fight between different editors,” she says. In her staging, too, she’s feminising some parts: “I didn’t want it to be a world where we assumed that almost everyone is male other than Katharine and Queen Isabel.”

One could easily spend an hour talking about Henry V (Harvey is, shockingly, the first woman to direct the play in the RSC’s history), but there are other serious matters to discuss.

Earlier this month, Trevor Nunn, the former RSC chief, stated that the RSC needed a London home; its purpose-built abode, the Barbican, is now one of a number of venues taking RSC work. “We are working out what London means for us,” says Harvey.

The more pressing task is transport to Stratford, which can seem Elizabethan in its remoteness. “To be frank,” Evans says, “a direct and better train service from London would be transformational. There used to be a direct train to Stratford [until 2023]. There no longer is. And we suffer because of it. When you look at theatre programmes from the 1960s, they used to have the last train time.” Could transport failure scupper their mission? “It might, because if we can’t get people to Stratford, how on earth are we meant to put on plays?”

That sounds quite existential, and behind the upbeat news there are clearly pressing challenges. Dividends reaped from ticket sales have not been substantial enough to mitigate the financial strain on the organisation, which is looking to reduce its workforce to achieve annual savings of £2.8m. In the autumn, the RSC announced that 420 of its 835 employees were eligible for voluntary redundancy – that consultation period is almost complete. The accounts for 2024-25 highlight the deficit problem: total income was £82m, substantially down on the previous year, with total expenditure running over £84m. Since then, annual repayments of the £20m Culture Recovery Fund loan taken out during Covid have kicked in, too. The drop in income partly reflects previous bumper takings at the box office – the 2022 Matilda film helped boost audiences for the stage show. But a cluster of factors are making life tougher.

“If you talk to most theatre leaders up and down the country, this is the case for them too,” Evans says. “Audiences have less money to spend because of the cost of living crisis. Costs have gone up exponentially. And there has long been a stand-still in Arts Council funding [currently approx £15m], which means a real-terms cut.”

There are no magic wands. Shows such as Les Misérables – which bankrolled the RSC for decades – are, he says, “like gold-dust – you’d be a fool to rely on them and part of the work we’re doing [in cutting back expenditure] is insulating the RSC from that reliance”. The desirability of any given corporate sponsor aside (and the RSC withdrew from a BP sponsorship deal in 2019 following protests), those options have dwindled, too: “There’s less money. Wherever the money comes from, there’s a deeper issue about relying on corporate sponsorship now.”

One option would be to scale back on productions, but that isn’t in their game plan, Harvey argues. “We have to find different ways of working but a sure-fire way to oblivion would be to reduce our artistic ambition and do less because then there’d be no point in our existing. You can cut back or you can be ever more ambitious and find different ways of making work and we’re going that route.”

There have been rumblings of discontent, particularly over plans to close the music department, with its in-house musicians. An online petition – attracting almost 7,000 signatures – has called for a rethink. If enough people complain, will they change their position? Harvey is swift to justify the proposed changes: “It’s about ensuring that music is more fully integrated into how we make work and giving composers the opportunity to work with freelance musicians. Some music will be created electronically, some will be recorded, some of it will be live.”

Upheavals notwithstanding, they’re looking to the future, with Evans (also an actor who left drama school early in order to join the RSC in the 1990s), hinting at repertory ensemble models. “We want to keep surprising audiences,” says Harvey.

As the actors bound back into the spacious Ashcroft room to resume rehearsals for Henry V, she leaves me with a final thought. Whenever the weight of responsibility presses hard, she confides, “I love to remember that this place exists because in the 1870s a local brewer and his son thought there should be a theatre in Stratford. The town and the theatre industry told them that they were mad but they understood that these plays survive because they’re performed. I love to remember that right at the root of this is a local brewer who had a radical idea, and the passion to see it through.”

Priority booking for all newly announced productions opens March 2 at 10am, with public booking opening March 18 at 10am. Priority booking for Game of Thrones: The Mad King opens April 14 with public booking from April 24. Info: rsc.org.uk

data.title: Supermarket petrol pumps run dry as drivers rush to fill up
data.description:
Supermarket petrol pumps have temporarily run out of fuel as motorists rush to fill up before prices surge higher.

The boss of Asda said the grocery giant had seen “significantly” higher demand for petrol, and as a result “the odd pump” at some stations had run dry.

Supermarkets are understood to have experienced higher than usual demand for fuel in recent weeks as nervous drivers fill their tanks in anticipation of further price rises.

The RAC said the price of unleaded petrol hit 150p per litre for the first time in almost two years on Friday, taking the cost of filling up a family car to about £80. The price of diesel is fast approaching 180p per litre.

Separate reports said industry insiders had warned ministers of a diesel shortage coming in mid-April. The Telegraph has not been able to verify the claim, which government insiders insisted they “did not recognise.”

Fuel industry sources also said they remained confident in supplies for at least another four weeks.

In a joint statement on Friday, Fuels Industry UK and the Petrol Retailers Association said: “We’re aware of reports circulating about fuel availability at a small number of forecourts for one retailer.

“Supply across the UK is flowing normally and there is no need for any change in usual buying habits.”

Motorists in India, South Africa, Australia and New Zealand have been panic-buying petrol in recent days, causing pump shortages even as national fuel supplies remain stable.

Russia ordered a ban on petrol exports on Friday following shortages that have been caused by Ukrainian attacks on Moscow’s oil refineries. The largest buyers of Russian gasoline are Turkey, China and Brazil.

Asda, which is the UK’s second-largest fuel retailer, admitted on Friday that it had run out of petrol at a small number of petrol pumps because of unusually high levels of demand in recent weeks.

Allan Leighton, Asda’s executive chairman, said: “Our fuel volumes are up quite significantly and clearly demand has been outstripping supply.

“Supply is tight and we are all trying hard on that. The issue is a temporary one, and some could see issues when we are waiting for delivery, and we can expect to see that continue.

“The spikiness at the moment makes this tricky for us, as spikes can lead to temporary shortages.

“These are temporary and are addressed very quickly.”

Petrol hits 150p per litre

Fuel Industry UK, a trade body, added: “Companies are monitoring the fuel supply situation closely and reporting regularly into the energy department on the status now and for the weeks ahead.

“Supply remains stable in the UK and our members are working hard to ensure that remains the case.”

Gordon Balmer, the executive director of the Petrol Retailers Association, said: “Industry and government remain in regular contact to monitor deliveries and stock levels in the UK as well as the situation internationally.”

A spokesman for the Department for Energy Security and Net Zero said: “The UK has a diverse and resilient energy supply.

“We continue to work with partners on the international situation.”

The price of petrol has now surged by 17.3p to 150.1p per litre since the US first attacked Iran at the end of February, according to the RAC. The price of diesel has also leapt by 35.3p to 177.7p per litre.

Average prices at motorway service stations are even higher, at 166p and 182p for unleaded and diesel respectively.

Simon Williams, the head of policy at the RAC, said: “With the long-awaited four-day Easter weekend almost within touching distance, the cost of getting away by car is going to be noticeably higher this year.

“Drivers on long journeys will need to plan very carefully where they refuel.

“The best advice remains to shop around for fuel and make use of free apps such as myRAC to never pay a penny more for fuel than is absolutely necessary.”

data.title: Boss of Qatari fund sues Deutsche Bank for £440m in lost wages claim
data.description:
The head of a Qatari sheikh’s family office is seeking almost £440m from Deutsche Bank after accusing the lender of blaming him for an £11bn accounting fiasco.

Michele Faissola, a former senior Deutsche banker in London, is one of four former employees who have accused the bank of making them scapegoats after the lender was forced to restate its financial accounts in 2013.

He was among the most senior figures at Deutsche, heading the bank’s asset and wealth management business.

In a High Court filing, the Italian banker claimed the German bank owed him lost wages of nearly £25m a year between 2016 and 2034, or £439.5m in total.

The 58-year-old is currently the chief executive of Dilmon, the London-based family office of Sheikh Hamad bin Khalifa Al Thani, who ruled Qatar between 1995 and 2013.

He is also a director of Maybourne Hotels, which owns five-star hotels in London including The Connaught and Claridge’s.

Collectively, Mr Faissola and three other traders are suing the bank for more than £600m over claims Deutsche created a “false narrative” over a 2013 trading incident.

After Mr Faissola left the bank in 2016, he and a number of other traders were embroiled in a criminal court case in Milan because of their conduct at the bank, which relied in part on an internal audit report overseen by Christian Sewing, Deutsche’s current boss.

The bankers were involved in controversial “enhanced repo deals” that concealed the size of loans Deutsche was making to other banks.

Through these trades, the bank was able to omit roughly £11bn from its balance sheet, the claimants allege, which made it appear that the bank was taking on less leverage than it actually was.

The four bankers allege that Mr Sewing was well aware of the transactions but he started an investigation into them anyway on a false premise.

It was subsequently forced to restate its accounts after it changed the accounting treatment of the trades.

An internal investigation soon followed, overseen by Mr Sewing, who was then head of the bank’s internal audit unit.

Deutsche has said in its annual report that it considers all such claims to be without merit and it will defend itself against them robustly. In the past, the bank has said it stands by the core findings of the audit report.

The findings of the investigation contributed to an Italian criminal trial of six former Deutsche traders, including the four currently suing the bank.

The group was sentenced to jail terms of up to four years and eight months for abetting false accounting and market manipulation. However, they were later acquitted by an appeals court in Milan.

The four traders now allege that the bank invented details about the incident in an effort to deflect scrutiny from senior executives.

They added that the report produced by Mr Sewing was “false and misleading” and that the bank carried out a “flawed disciplinary procedure” which sought to emphasise the purported wrongdoing of the traders while deflecting from the real reasons for the accounting restatement.

Deutsche Bank declined to comment.

data.title: A gritty new Lovejoy? Give us a break from these ludicrous remakes
data.description:
I’m afraid my first reaction to the news that the Eighties Sunday-night cosy drama Lovejoy might be returning to our TV screens was to roll my eyes up to the sky. When I read that See Saw Films, makers of Slow Horses, have snapped up the rights for “a contemporary re-imagining of the Lovejoy novels that will strip away the nostalgia of the Eighties adaptation and return to the unrulier spirit of the books”, I positively giggled. And when the owner of the books’ rights told Deadline, “See-Saw’s bold, assured vision demonstrated exactly how to preserve the books’ wit and grit while re-imagining them for today’s audience”, I fell off my chair guffawing.

TV and film have been awash with remakes, reboots – and, most dread of all, re-imaginings – for a long time now. Remake fever has passed the stage of being merely ridiculous, and progressed to absurdity. A gritty and modern take on Lovejoy – the story of a loveable rogue antiques dealer – is surely a sign that the condition is reaching its crisis point.

If, that is, the new Lovejoy is ever actually made. Buried in the Deadline story is the fact that the rights were snapped up by another production company seven years ago, and they couldn’t interest any streamer or broadcaster in it. There is as yet no channel attached to this iteration either.

It used to be considered bad luck in the TV industry to chat to the press about projects in development – the vast shadow schedule of shows in the running, most of which will never be made. With revivals, such caution goes out the window; it seems the idea today is that telling the papers you’re bringing a show back will create a buzz, and demonstrate to commissioning executives the strength of the public’s affection for it and how they clamour for its return.

And it works – old brands now crowd the slates and schedules of television. In Britain, we’ve recently seen the returns – with varying degrees of success – of All Creatures Great and Small, Bergerac, and The Darling Buds of May (aka The Larkins). Looming in development or pre-production are (deep breath) Dalziel and Pascoe, Byker Grove, Threads, The Secret Life of Us, Blake’s 7, and The Night Manager. Over the water, American properties returning include Scrubs, Daredevil, The Comeback, Malcolm in the Middle, Baywatch, Buffy the Vampire Slayer, and Prison Break.

Compared to the States, we Britons don’t really have the requisite franchise portfolio of big or scaleable old brands. We’ve mined out the few we had, which makes the hoohah about Lovejoy particularly amusing. The Americans have Star Wars, Star Trek, and the Marvel Universe. We’ve got Juliet Bravo, Pipkins and Peak Practice.

It’s particularly amusing when execs make a big song and dance about how these comebacks are not about nostalgia. What are they for, then? And is there really nobody out there with a brand-new idea that’s more spectacular than a reboot of Lovejoy? Even funnier is when they take enormous pains to rejig the new versions with grit and diversity. The “fresh and contemporary re-imagining” of Dalziel and Pascoe, for example, has gender-swapped the leads, while Bergerac was retooled as a rather dour one-story mini-series that made Jersey look like High Wycombe.

The reason for all the re-imagining? I suspect this spiral is the result of too many channels and not enough viewers. With a familiar brand – even if it’s only a recognisable title – you can pretty much always bank on a high rating for your first episode. Name recognition will ensure that curious viewers will look in, and then hopefully get hooked. In the diminished world of 2020s TV, “The Return Of X” is so much more reliably bankable than launching a new show with a new idea.

This doesn’t apply only to brands that were always TV shows. Literary characters such as Dracula or Sherlock Holmes will always guarantee bums on seats. Dickens, Jane Austen and Agatha Christie are eternally strong draws, however retrodden, shop-worn and played out. Now even Homer is returning with Christopher Nolan’s film of The Odyssey.

But this mythic, Jungian quality doesn’t really apply to most TV shows. How ludicrous can the re-imagining mania get? In the hope of accelerating the process, I offer up the three following suggestions.

Rentaghost

In this dark and challenging re-imagining of the Rentaghost franchise, gone is the cosy slapstick of yesteryear. This modern, female-led version stars Olivia Colman as Hazel McWitch and Gugu Mbatha Raw as Nadia Popov, with the non-binary pantomime horse Dobbin (he/him in front, she/her at the back) bringing up the rear. Playing a darker take on Mr Claypole, a ghost who haunts himself, Tom Hiddleston brings out the devastating hidden horrors of the afterlife.

The Family Ness

A gripping political thriller. When her precious eggs are cracked by vibrations caused by fracking, Scotland’s most iconic monster rampages devastatingly through Aberdeen, causing at least 40 quid’s worth of damage. Stars Kit Harrington as Angus and Myha’la as Elspeth, both locked in combat with savage, six-hundred-ton CGI monster. Can they save Nessie from rabble-rousing populist politician Nigel Lowe-Mogg (Russell Tovey in a wax jacket)? A very serious warning about environmental despoliation?

Terry and June

Reduced to penury in the gig economy, Terry and June Medford (Stephen Graham and Sarah Lancashire) turn to crack cocaine as an escape from mid-life suburban monotony. When June stabs Terry’s toxically masculine boss Sir Derek (James Norton) to death, the couple flee from the law in their caravan. A devastating portrait of broken Britain.

Or, alternatively, here’s another idea: get the TV industry to sign a pact that no previously used ideas are to be revived for the next 20 years. Let the field of reboots lay fallow to replenish itself, and cultivate instead the fresh and fertile soil of innovation. Let’s have a revival of new ideas.

data.title: Who could replace Scott Mills on Radio 2?
data.description:
Following the shock news that Radio 2 DJ Scott Mills had been fired from his job as host of the Breakfast Show, the BBC must now begin the task of finding someone else who has the credentials to fill the UK’s most prestigious radio slot. Here are five who might have what it takes – and be considered safe enough.

Gary Davies

Woo, Gary Davies? Well, if not now, when? The veteran DJ – a staple of Radio 1 in the 1980s and early Nineties – has been a consummate stand-in man for Radio 2 over the past few years, jumping in at often late notice to cover for Zoe Ball, Vernon Kay, Rylan Clark, Dermot O’Leary, Steve Wright and, of course, Mills. His style is laid-back and somewhat old-fashioned (indeed, the style that Mills has always imitated), but ideal for the station. Does he lack the “personality” to put his stamp on the breakfast show, à la Messrs Wogan and Evans? Perhaps, but the BBC may well be drawn to someone who is a model professional and doesn’t see themselves as bigger than the station. Thirty-three years since being given the elbow by Radio 1, it would quite the redemption arc.

Rylan Clark

The motormouth from Essex has managed to pack in quite the media career in 14 years. Starting as the reality TV show grotesque (The X Factor, Celebrity Big Brother), before transitioning, via This Morning, into a beloved and surprisingly cerebral TV presence (the refrain “Can I shock you? I like Rylan?” became popular around 2020). Not so long ago, he shifted into almost Romesh Ranganathan levels of overexposure when he seemed to host every show on television, but he has since found his groove – appropriately enough as a DJ on Radio 2 (where he currently hosts a Saturday afternoon show) and as co-host of the BBC Eurovision semi-finals, alongside Mills.

Clark is witty, quick-thinking, acceptably edgy and able to appeal in buckets to Radio 2’s supposed core demographic (yes, those famous “Mood Mums”). He has a BBC podcast, has appeared in Doctor Who and hosts The One Show. A no-brainer for the Beeb?

Liza Tarbuck

Whether the honey-voiced actress could be in the running is unknown, given that this month it was announced she had left – not is leaving, had left – the Saturday evening Radio 2 show she had hosted for 14 years. Given that she did not even say goodbye on air, it’s little wonder that her loyal listeners sounded bereft on this week’s Feedback on Radio 4 (“withdrawal symptoms” and a “real sense of loss” were mentioned by the mourners, while one gentlemen described her as being “part of the family”). However, were she available, she would be a superb and obvious replacement for Mills. She is warm without being cloying, irreverent without being annoying, and comes across as the sort of no-nonsense good egg to which Radio 2 listeners respond well. For obvious reasons, the BBC may also prefer to give middle-aged men a wide berth for the foreseeable future.

Chris Evans

Stranger things have happened. While he would not be able to command the sort of salary he used to enjoy from the BBC (they have pared back since the days Evans was on a reported £2.25m) or from his current paymasters at Virgin Radio, could Evans be tempted by an emotional return to the show he made such an enormous success in the 2010s? His breakfast slot on Virgin struggles to hit 750,000 listeners at the moment (Chris Moyles’s XFM show at the same time gets 1.1m) and has little cultural cut-through. In an instance, Evans would increase Radio 2’s breakfast listenership, which currently stands at around 6.5m, and find himself back on the lips of the nation’s watercooler-users. If the BBC decides it needs a big name to make a big splash (let’s face it, they tried “a safe pair of hands” with Mills), then the ginger rabble-rouser would fit the bill.

Sara Cox

Another of those who has served their apprenticeship. Cox, like Ball and Evans before her, hosted the Radio 1 Breakfast Show, and has been a Radio 2 regular since 2011. She currently sits comfortably in the drive-time seat (indeed, during her stint, the show has been extended from two hours to three), so it’s unclear if she would fancy those early mornings (though she has been a recent stand-in on the breakfast show), but she’s a natural fit. She hosts live radio like falling off a log; she’s funny and personable, and is a general, genial presence on BBC television, having hosted The Great Pottery Throw Down, Back in Time for… and book review show Between the Covers. Now 51, Cox retains some of her cool, “wild child” credentials from her early career as host of The Girlie Show and The Big Breakfast, yet remains a down-to-earth farmer’s lass at heart.

The Radio 2 Breakfast Show airs every weekday from 6.30am

data.title: Oracle cuts thousands of jobs as AI pressures mount
data.description:
Tech giant Oracle is poised to cut thousands of jobs amid mounting scrutiny over its multibillion-dollar bets on AI.

The software group told affected employees in an email on Tuesday that their jobs had been cut “as part of a broader organisational change” expected to impact thousands at the Silicon Valley giant.

The cuts will include a range of positions, such as software engineers and salespeople, according to posts on LinkedIn from workers who say they were affected by the lay-offs.

According to the email, reported by Business Insider, Oracle’s management said it had decided to “eliminate” roles immediately. It informed staff they would soon be unable to log on to their computers and that their email, voicemail and files would be deactivated.

Oracle was founded by billionaire Larry Ellison and is one of the world’s largest tech groups with a $420bn (£316bn) price tag.

The company recently saw a 25pc slide in its share price as investors were increasingly concerned about its debt-fuelled investments in data centres.

Oracle has spent heavily on building the facilities as it seeks to capitalise on rising demand for cloud computing capacity from AI companies including OpenAI and Meta.

Most of Oracle’s investments have been funded through debt and the company plans to raise up to $50bn in debt in 2026 to finance its data centre-building plans this year.

It has fuelled fears that Oracle’s bets on AI may fail to pay off amid wider concerns about overspending by the world’s top technology companies.

Analysts from TD Cowen predicted in January that Oracle might cut between 20,000 and 30,000 jobs to free up cash for its data centre investments after its backers started showing reluctance to keep funding the plan.

Shares in Oracle fell sharply in December after one of its biggest backers, Blue Owl Capital, pulled out of financing a $10bn data centre project in Michigan.

Oracle was started by Mr Ellison and his co-founders, Bob Miner and Ed Oates, in Santa Clara, California in 1977.

Mr Ellison, who owns a roughly 40pc stake in Oracle, is currently the sixth richest person in the world, with a net worth of $195bn, according to Bloomberg’s Billionaires Index.

The 81-year-old billionaire is also a close ally and long-time supporter of Donald Trump, the US president.

Oracle declined to comment.

data.title: How Sadiq Khan made London into a taxi driver’s worst nightmare
data.description:
It should be a straightforward journey that needn’t take too long – Liverpool Street to London Bridge station, two areas heavily populated with white-collar workers heading from the commuter belts into the capital for their careers.

However, those opting to drive the short one-and-a-half miles find themselves forced to go a more roundabout way – through Whitechapel, in order to avoid the bus gate at Bishopsgate, which prevents motorists (yet allows buses and bikes) from accessing the road.

So, instead of taking around five minutes in the car, it takes between 20 and 30 minutes. Realistically, it would be quicker for drivers to swap four wheels for two legs.

Bishopsgate is just one of the many roads in the capital that have become increasingly unwelcoming to motorists, with a recent survey conducted by TomTom, the satnav company, finding that London is the slowest-driving capital city in the world.

It takes an average of three minutes and 38 seconds to travel one measly kilometre (0.6 miles) in the centre of the city. The analysis also found drivers lost an average of 141 hours stuck in rush hour traffic in 2025.

This glacial pace is annoying when you are trying to get somewhere in a hurry, to attend an important meeting, perhaps, or to make it to the theatre before curtain call. However, for the black cab drivers of London, increasingly congested, slow and difficult-to-navigate roads are leading to a significant loss of earnings, and even putting their livelihoods – and the sight of the London icon – at risk of extinction.

As of late 2025, the number of licensed drivers had fallen to around 14,800, down from more than 22,500 a decade earlier.

For Steve Cox, who became a black cab driver in 2022, navigating the streets of London has become increasingly difficult. He cites low-traffic neighbourhoods (LTNs) – areas where roads are closed to traffic to allow cyclists and pedestrians to take priority – as one of the main contributors to the capital’s slow roads.

“What used to be a 15-minute journey can now take you up to an hour,” the 54-year-old explains. “Tottenham Court Road is severely restricted for motorists between 8am and 7pm, which are some of your peak hours as a black cab.

“If you want to go from one side of Tottenham Court Road to the other to drop a passenger, we have to do two different squares and go round several back roads. It’s just ridiculous.

“It’s another 15 to 20 minutes on the journey time, and probably another £15-£20 for the customer. I’m embarrassed to charge them. It’s unfair and leads to complaints that aren’t down to me – and results in fewer people trying to flag us down.”

While LTNs were initially implemented in London in the 1970s (although under a different name) to filter traffic in the Hackney area, they were implemented en masse in March 2020 under Mayor of London Sir Sadiq Khan’s plans to improve road safety, decrease air and noise pollution, and encourage “active travel” such as walking and cycling.

Now, it is thought four per cent of London’s nine-million-strong population lives in an LTN.

These have made navigating around the capital increasingly problematic; approximately 95 per cent of black cab drivers report that LTNs and poor road conditions make picking up and dropping off passengers difficult.

Cabbies also cite London’s new speed restrictions as a reason why the capital often remains at a standstill. According to Transport for London (TfL), more than half of London’s roads now have a 20mph limit. This includes all roads within London’s Congestion Charge zone, as well as major roads, such as the A107 corridor and the White Hart Roundabout.

While this was implemented to try and cut deaths and serious injuries, City Hall was previously told this change was “devastating to the taxi industry” by Steve McNamara, general secretary of the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association in 2024.

“I understand the need for 20mph zones around schools,” says Frank O’Beirne, who has been a black cab driver for 14 years. “However, places like Park Lane, which have been reduced from three lanes of traffic to just one in order for there to be a bus and cycling lane, is just madness – especially now we’re stuck only being able to go 20mph there.”

The 50-year-old adds: “While our meters do go up in traffic, it’s only incremental – we need to be moving to actually have decent fares. Customers get so fed up in traffic they just jump out.”

Sadiq Khan’s green crusade for London has reached a nadir with the highly criticised and controversial ultra-low emission zone (Ulez). Since 2018, all newly-licenced black cabs need to be “zero-emission capable”, while the maximum operating age for older, more polluting diesel taxis has been reduced from 15 years to 12.

Cox says some older cabbies have been forced into an early retirement. “I know men in their 60s who have had their cab for 10 years and just didn’t see the point of carrying on investing so much money in a new car, and have gone and got jobs in Tesco instead.

“That and the stress of trying to earn enough money to keep you afloat is enough to push someone into another job. I don’t blame people for saying: ‘I don’t need this anymore.’”

It’s of little surprise, then, that a quarter of all black cabs on the roads before the pandemic are no longer in operation. Renting a traditional black cab, or Hackney carriage, is not cheap. Cox pays £360 a week to rent his vehicle, while others pay £400 and beyond. That doesn’t include the additional outgoings for cabbies, such as the mandatory two MOTs a year; while cab drivers don’t pay this, it does result in a loss of earnings as you can’t work while your vehicle is being serviced.

Cox says he needs to earn more than £3,000 a month just to break even on the investments he’s made to become a black cab driver. “It’s a stressful trade,” he says. “It’s made even more stressful by sudden roadworks, or ongoing protests by groups such as Palestine Action blocking roads. I can lose up to £300 a day in earnings when protests take place.”

Those still working as black cab drivers are now finding alternative methods to keep themselves afloat. O’Beirne works as a tour guide during slow periods. “I prefer doing out-of-town stuff now, because London just drives me mad,” he says. “Places like Tottenham Court Road, Mayfair, the City are just no-go areas. It’s a nightmare to navigate.

“Corporate gigs tend to pay better – I get a lot of American customers, who tend to be big tippers. But doing these gigs means you give up some freedom of setting your own hours, which is partly the beauty of doing this job.”

So, what could help London’s black cab drivers stay on the roads? For Cox, cutting, or at least reducing, some of the restrictions of LTNs on Hackney carriages would help with picking up and dropping off customers.

“If we really want to ease congestion, we could put a cap on private-hire drivers,” he adds. “They can operate without any restriction on the size of their fleet. I’ve heard of private-hire drivers getting licensed in Wolverhampton and then driving down here to get more work.”

Currently, private-hire vehicles vastly outstrip black cabs; TfL estimates more than 106,000 of these cabs are on the road.

Meanwhile, Steve Kenton, who has been a cabbie for 35 years, believes City Hall should listen more to black cab drivers. “We have some wonderful and intelligent people who choose to become black cab drivers,” he says. “We spend three to four years perfecting the Knowledge of London so we know the proper routes around the city. We attract individuals from every demographic. Our views and concerns deserve to be treated with more respect.”

“Fundamentally, if our wheels aren’t moving, we aren’t making money,” O’Beirne agrees. “We’re the only fully accessible transport network in London. Not everyone can ride a bike.”

data.title: Town centres face losing their car parks after operator’s collapse
data.description:
Scores of car parks across Britain face closure following the sudden collapse of National Car Parks (NCP), in a development that threatens to deal a hammer blow to town centres.

Administrators for NCP, the country’s biggest operator, are threatening to abandon sites if landlords refuse to accept drastically lower rents.

Up to 20 car parks have already been shut and councils are now braced for dozens more closures across NCP’s remaining 318 sites.

Closures threaten not only to leave landlords out of pocket but also deal a blow to councils, which risk missing out on millions of pounds in business rates. An unoccupied building owned by a company in administration is typically exempt from business rates.

The closure of NCP car parks would also be a blow for town centres. Many are the main sites for people visiting shopping centres, meaning visitor numbers could suffer.

Insolvency experts at PwC and CBRE are currently scrambling to safeguard NCP’s future after it collapsed into administration this month.

This first involved stripping out loss-making sites across the company’s estate before then demanding lower rents from its landlords. Administrators are now seeking a buyer for the business.

However, the process has already been frustrated by tensions with landlords who are furious at administrators’ threats to close sites if terms cannot be agreed. In some cases, landlords are being asked to reduce rents by more than 50pc.

As well as seeking significantly cheaper rates, administrators have also demanded a four-week rent waiver to help put the company on a stable footing.

Landlords have been given just days to agree to new terms on sites occupied by NCP, increasing the likelihood of widespread closures.

One landlord said they were given 24 hours’ notice of the new offer and told to accept or face immediate closure.

“We view these last-minute bullying tactics as the lowest form of negotiation and are not prepared to do business with people who behave in this manner,” they said.

City lawyers are already in contact with landlords in the event of potential legal action, with some considering whether to take sites back from NCP.

It is understood that the sites already closed have effectively been abandoned, including those in Birmingham, Leicester, London and Luton.

One landlord said they were not even told the site had been closed.

It has sparked alarm among city councils over fears that empty car parks will become a magnet for anti-social behaviour.

Some local officials are already emailing landlords to ask what security measures will be put in place to keep sites safe.

The importance of car parks to local economies has also prompted speculation that some councils may have to foot the bill for reopening sites to support businesses. Such a move would increase pressure on already cash-strapped local authorities.

The developments will trigger scrutiny of NCP’s former Japanese owner, Park24. The private equity firm blamed a combination of Covid, high rents and elevated energy costs for NCP’s downfall, while PwC said the company came unstuck as “significant rent payments” fell due.

However, others have pointed to spiralling borrowings across the business, which rose to £350m by the end of 2025. Some City sources said these debts were largely to blame for the company’s collapse.

NCP charged high prices and had limited overheads, suggesting it should have been financially stable without debt. In central London, NCP charges motorists as much as £60 a day for parking.

Following their appointment, PwC said the business had “deteriorated over a number of years post Covid-19 as demand for parking has not recovered to historic levels, particularly across city-centre and commuter locations”.

Administrators said last night they were still working hard to sell some or all of the business, which was founded in 1931 by Colonel Frederick Lucas before merging with rival Central Car Parks in 1959.

A spokesman for the administrators said: “A small number of commercially unviable sites have closed this week and while no other closures are currently planned, the administrators are engaging with key stakeholders, including landlords of some sites, to reach agreements that will allow for their continued operation.”

data.title: Water boss accused of potential conflict of interest over lobbying ties
data.description:
The chairman of a water quango that sticks up for consumers has been accused of a potential conflict of interest because of his stake in a lobbying firm that works with utilities.

Rob Wilson, who runs the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), is still listed as a senior adviser at Political Insight UK, The Telegraph can reveal.

Mr Wilson, a former Conservative minister under David Cameron, co-founded the firm and is a minority shareholder.

Founded in 2020, Political Insight UK claims on its website to help clients with “government relations” by advising them on “policy development and regulatory issues”.

This includes clients across a range of sectors, such as transport, housing, retail and public utilities. The website makes no mention of individual water companies, though the term utilities covers the water industry.

His links to the lobbying firm have raised concern owing to his role at CCW, a government-approved quango funded by households and businesses.

The CCW brands itself as the voice of billpayers by holding companies, government and regulators to account.

Alistair Carmichael, chairman of the environment, food and rural affairs committee, said: “Public confidence in the water industry is already at an all-time low.

“A body like the CCW has to be seen to be the consumers’ voice and nothing else. This is not a moment where anyone can be seen to be riding two horses.”

After publication, Mr Wilson claimed that he had not undertaken any client advisory work for the firm for more than two years, and none at all since his appointment as CCW chairman. He said the website was outdated.

He also stressed that before that, he did not advise any clients in the public utilities sector. He denies that his stake in Political Insight gives rise to any conflict of interest regarding his role at the CCW.

The row will heighten pressure on Mr Wilson after a cache of receipts revealed how he twice claimed discounts on train tickets last year by using a railcard for students.

On one occasion, he was caught by a conductor and asked to pay a £16.40 fare correction, which he later expensed.

The CCW said Mr Wilson had clicked the 16-25 railcard by accident and made the mistake in “good faith”.

Mr Wilson previously drew scrutiny during his time as a Tory MP for claiming 9p for a 320-metre car journey.

As well as owning a stake in Political Insight UK, the former minister is also a director for Transport Focus, a consumer action group for rail passengers.

In recent years, Transport Focus has worked in tandem with government officials to combat fare evasion.

The CCW says on its website that Mr Wilson, who was appointed chairman by the Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (Defra) last year, has a “number of shareholdings declared”.

However, the group said that “the list does not include any water companies”.

‘Radical transparency’

A CCW spokesman said the conflict of interest claims were a matter for Defra.

Defra asks public appointees to disclose any potential conflicts of interest to the department and puts mitigation measures in place if necessary.

Speaking last year, Mr Wilson called for “radical transparency” and greater accountability to rebuild trust in the water sector.

He also spoke to water companies about how to address whether their actions are acceptable at a conference.

“If this were explained on the front page of tomorrow’s newspaper, would the public see it as fair, responsible and in their interest?” he said, addressing attendees in his capacity as CCW chairman.

“If the answer is yes, you are rebuilding confidence. If the answer is no, you are eroding it.”

Scrutiny of Mr Wilson’s lobbying ties comes at an awkward time for the CCW.

The group has so far failed to publish financial accounts as it battles with the National Audit Office (NAO) amid a dispute over its expenditure.

Analysis of the CCW’s board minutes from last year also highlights how the group is in a separate spat with HMRC.

A CCW spokesman said this matter concerned a decision not to include VAT on its sales invoices.

“After extensive communication with HMRC, we have now become VAT registered to allow us to correct the issue, which we are in the process of doing,” the spokesman said.

“Having revisited the calculations HMRC provides, we understand any resulting interest and surcharges will be minimal.”

Defra was contacted for comment.

data.title: OpenAI funding fears hit memory chip prices
data.description:
Spending cuts at OpenAI have hit memory chip prices as fears grow about the tech company’s ability to fund its expansion.

OpenAI’s boom helped spark a surge in the price of random-access memory (RAM), a key component in both consumer electronics and data centres, as suppliers struggled to keep pace with huge demand.

Prices have risen by around 700pc over the last year, according to market research firm TrendForce.

But there are signs the price surge is beginning to ease as OpenAI puts the brakes on its vast investment in data centres.

Prices for DDR5 memory kits have dropped by as much as $100 (£75) in some cases, with one 32GB kit on sale for $370 on Amazon.

That is down from a recent peak of around $490, according to industry publication Wccftech.

The surge in prices was in part driven by an agreement struck by OpenAI in October to buy 900,000 DRAM wafers a month from Samsung and SK Hynix. The deal equated to around 40pc of global supply.

But plans by OpenAI, the tech giant behind ChatGPT, to rein in several big-spending projects have sparked fears the company will no longer need as many chips.

Last week it shuttered Sora, its AI video app that was backed by Disney, in an effort to control its ballooning costs.

OpenAI this month also scrapped a multi-billion-dollar deal with Oracle to expand the Stargate data centre in Texas.

Sam Altman, OpenAI’s chief executive, previously set out plans to spend $1.4 trillion on the data centres required to power its technology.

But the company, which has said it will burn through $115bn by 2029, is now reining in investment under pressure from investors and as rivals, such as Claude owner Anthropic, gain ground.

The scaling back of these plans has raised hopes that RAM prices will fall back from their record highs.

Any decline in prices would be a boon for consumers as the supply crisis is expected to take its toll on devices such as smartphones, computers and games consoles that also rely on memory chips.

IDC has forecast that mobile phone prices will rise up to 8pc this year, leading to a sharp fall in sales. Manufacturers are also considering putting less memory in new devices, which would make them slower and less reliable, in an effort to keep costs down.

Francisco Jeronimo, an analyst at tech researcher IDC, warned it was too early to say whether there would be a sustained fall in memory chip prices.

But he added: “If those contracts are not fulfilled, if they scale back in terms of the investments they are doing, definitely the price of memory will alleviate and that will be good for the consumer electronics industry.”

data.title: Reeves urged not to tax compensation for NS&I victims
data.description:
Victims of the NS&I savings scandal must not be forced to pay tax on their compensation, Rachel Reeves has been told.

Up to 37,500 beneficiaries of deceased savers who had accounts with National Savings and Investments (NS&I) have been denied £476m because of operational failures.

The bank is accused of having short-changed bereaved families by losing track of dead customers’ investments and withholding premium bonds, which are entered in a monthly draw with prizes up to £1m.

Dax Harkins, the former chief executive of NS&I, was forced to resign over the scandal after his position became untenable after The Telegraph reported a series of failings at the bank.

Torsten Bell, the pensions minister, announced that victims of the scandal would be compensated.

But he suggested families could be hit with tax bills after they were given funds they had previously been denied.

This is because the sums could alter the value of deceased savers’ estates for tax purposes, raising the prospect of additional charges.

While technically the estates would be taxed, it would affect those who are being reunited with the money.

Critics have said that bereaved relatives should not have to pay tax as a result of receiving compensation from NS&I.

Richard Tice, the deputy leader of Reform UK, said: “Obviously, victims of the NS&I scandal should not have to pay tax on compensation.

“Once again, people have suffered more than enough through incompetence by Government institutions.”

‘Failures by NS&I are extremely serious’

Sir Mel Stride, the Conservatives’ shadow chancellor, said that the failures by NS&I were “extremely serious” and were “undermining trust in the organisation”.

He told The Telegraph: “The Government should ensure that those who have been treated unfairly are properly compensated, including those facing an unexpected tax bill, as well as ensuring maximum transparency and accountability about what went wrong and how it will be fixed.”

Mr Bell told the Commons last week: “We also recognise there may be tax implications for affected estates and want to avoid bereaved families facing disproportionate disruption and administrative costs as a result of this error.

“We are exploring what support can be provided and will set this out alongside the NS&I delivery plan in May.”

Compensation payouts for the infected blood scandal and the Post Office Horizon scandal were free of tax.

Mr Harkins was appointed to the top job at NS&I in April 2023, having worked his way up the organisation from the sales department.

Sir Jim Harra, the former head of HM Revenue & Customs (HMRC), has been drafted in to replace Mr Harkins.

He will report on a delivery plan in May, with details of exactly how many people have been affected, how their savings will be returned with interest and what additional compensation will be paid.

Dame Harriet Baldwin, a Conservative MP and former chairman of the Treasury select committee, said the bank must “focus on quickly sorting out the mess they have created for bereaved families”.

She said that the lump-sum compensation and penalty interest could be taxable in some circumstances, but would “vary on a case-by-case basis”.

Addressing MPs, Mr Bell said NS&I had failed to learn from past missing savings scandals.

Citing Santander’s “serious failings” in 2018 in processing the accounts and investments of deceased customers, he told MPs: “What is now clear is that NS&I did not respond to those warning signs as fully as I, and more importantly, their customers, would expect, nor did the last government act.”

Originally known as the Post Office Savings Bank, NS&I launched Premium Bonds in 1956 and became an independent government department in 1969.

The bank, which looks after £240bn for some 24 million customers, has apologised for the failings.

data.title: Britain in danger of outsourcing its intellect, warns AI pioneer
data.description:
The artificial intelligence revolution threatens to erode Britain’s sovereignty, one of the country’s most experienced tech entrepreneurs has warned.

Ken Mulvany, co-founder and executive chairman of Benevolent AI, which has pioneered artificial intelligence in drug discovery, told The Telegraph that the United States and China are increasingly dominant in the crucial field.

He says: “If you look at this from the UK or Europe’s perspective, the risk is that these – China and America – are now the two cognitive powers. They’re the ones that are investing the most into this.

“I do worry about having the nation’s intellect effectively outsourced. It’s not just that, either. It’s the productivity gains.

“If for every hour of cognitive work that you do there’s a machine doing what would take you 50 hours, how do you as a nation compete with that?

“Electricity multiplied the power of muscles and AI can do the same with brains in certain scenarios. But it’s almost like you, as Britain and Europe are outsourcing national IQ to US and Chinese tech companies.

“So you’re left trying to regulate something you don’t own. And I do worry about that. I think of it as cognitive border control.”

Mulvany is better placed than most to sound the alarm. An eerily youthful 57 who betrays his Hawaiian roots with a surf-ready hairdo, he is an entrepreneur who has seen tech boom and bust more than once.

In a world increasingly haunted by visions of an AI apocalypse that would make the dotcom crash look like a minor market setback, Mulvany and Benevolent have already been tested to near-destruction.

The technology excited investors – including the star fund manager Neil Woodford long before his self-inflicted implosion – enough to snag a £1.5bn valuation way back in 2018.

This was long before the likes of Sam Altman of OpenAI and Dario Amodei of Anthropic emerged to seek thrones as the next generation of tech plutocrats.

Benevolent sought to help researchers to quickly design new drugs with computer assistance, using machine learning techniques to scour a treasure trove of science information for possibilities at an unprecedented clip. Expansion into defence and energy markets promised new horizons and riches.

It seemed to work. Benevolent burst onto the public markets long before the current AI frontrunners had attracted the attention of everyday investors, too. With the world still in the grip of the pandemic and having discovered an anti-inflammatory that was proving useful on pandemic wards, the start-up made a splashy debut.

“I wasn’t really part of it at that time,” Mulvany recalls ruefully. Having seemingly set up Benevolent for enormous success, he was already easing into semi-retirement.

As it secured a lucrative listing in Amsterdam, Benevolent won endorsement and investment from some of the pharmaceutical industry’s biggest names. AstraZeneca backed Benevolent as part of a transaction that raised hundreds of millions.

Alas, Mulvany’s big payday and exit did not set the scene for a sustainable company. A starry board and big executive hires steered Benevolent off a cliff.

The company lost almost all its value within two years, prompting Mulvany to abandon his early retirement and mount a boardroom coup to take back control last year. He had become frustrated that Benevolent had shifted from his concept of a technology company to a drug developer in its own right, with all the risk and costs associated with it.

“They brought in some luminaries,” Mulvany recalls. “Some really heavy hitters from pharma. And Benevolent morphed from a tech company to a pharma company. For me as an investor, and for some of my fellow investors, we didn’t have the appetite for that. We were technology investors.

“You had a technology shareholder base and a management and board doing biotech. All of its revenue was from its technology but it was spending a ton of money developing drugs.

“The technology cost was very low relative to drug development. So I became more of an activist shareholder at that point because it wasn’t going the way I wanted it to go and the markets reflected that too. It lost 95pc of its value.”

It’s a tale of hype meeting with financial and scientific reality that now seems to be playing out on an extreme scale as OpenAI and its rivals test the limits of pre-profit funding and investor patience. Altman alone has committed to spending more than $1tn (£750bn).

Having abandoned his retirement to mount his successful coup, Mulvany is now rebuilding Benevolent as an enterprise software company. Its new software and services are focused sharply on helping drug developers, rather than developing drugs. It’s a more commercial proposition, with a closer eye on returns for Benevolent and its big pharma customers.

“We help on the science side of things, reviewing the literature, but also how you make business decisions as a drug developer, how you expand into a particular market, how you design trials and all of that.

“People in the industry are making decisions with a fraction of the information they need.”

These are still the early days of Benevolent’s attempted comeback and Mulvany’s unexpected return from early retirement, which he had intended to spend skiing. His hopes of using the company’s targeted AI technology beyond drug discovery in fields such as defence and energy have been revived.

Benevolent, without revenue, now has the cash and investor support to pursue its new business into 2027, according to Mulvany.

Despite the mounting fears of an AI bust, he remains a believer in the technology. After a deep cull of staff, Benevolent’s coders are 50 times faster with the help of computer intelligence, for instance. But Mulvany, now an AI survivor, admits a shake-out lies ahead for those with grandiose visions.

“I saw this in the dotcom. I was there right at the bubble. But whether it’s railroads, or electricity or the internet there has always been over-investment and hype.

“So there’s going to be losers in this but the infrastructure is being put in place. So yeah, you’re seeing an AI bubble, but I’ve seen it before.”

data.title: Car finance scandal: how to claim compensation
data.description:
Have you been affected by the car finance scandal? Share your story by emailing money@telegraph.co.uk* 

Millions of drivers are in line for average payments of £830 after being mis-sold car finance between 2007 and 2024, the financial regulator has announced.

Around 12 million deals are thought to qualify for the redress scheme, paving the way for lenders to pay £7.5bn in compensation costs.

  • What is the car finance scandal?
  • Who is eligible for compensation?
  • How much compensation could you get?
  • What happens next?
  • Is this the end of the scandal?

What is the car finance scandal?

The seemingly never-ending saga revolves around mis-sold personal contract purchases (PCP) and hire purchase (HP) car deals made between April 6 2007, and November 1 2024.

It relates to commission arrangements between lenders and dealers and unfair contracts given to borrowers.

The Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) found widespread failures across the car finance industry, where the existence and nature of commission had not been adequately disclosed, and neither had contractual ties between lenders and brokers.

Failure to properly disclose commission arrangements was deemed “unlawful” by the Supreme Court last summer.

Today’s announcement from the FCA comes after a consultation process, with some of its original proposals tweaked in accordance with feedback.

Who is eligible for compensation?

The FCA expects 12 million car finance deals will be eligible for compensation. This is down slightly from the expected 14.2 million at consultation.

The scheme covers deals taken out between April 6 2007 and November 1 2024.

For the agreement to be considered unfair to the borrower, it must have involved one or more of the following without their knowledge:

Discretionary commission arrangements

You will keep hearing the term “DCA” in regard to the mis-selling scandal. This is in relation to discretionary commission arrangements, whereby salesmen are alleged to have sold finance at higher rates in return for extra pay from lenders.

There’s a chance the salesman made up an interest rate on the spot, regardless of your credit history. DCAs were banned in January 2021, so it only applies to agreements before that.

High commission

If the commission was 39pc or more of the total borrowing cost and 10pc or more of the loan, then you will be eligible for compensation.

Contractual ties

Tied arrangements, which gave a lender exclusivity or the first right of refusal without clearly informing the customer, are judged to be unfair by the Supreme Court.

Who will miss out?

The FCA said claimants will not be owed money if commission was £120 or less for car finance agreements made before April 2014 and £150 or less from that date onwards.

The regulator said commission amounts below those levels were “unlikely to have influenced the consumer’s decision or the broker’s behaviour”.

Claims for high-value loans – higher than 99.5pc of other loans that year – are also excluded, as they are “not suitable for a mass-market redress scheme”. For example, loans worth over £82,000 in 2024 would be exempt from the redress scheme. These consumers can still complain to their lender and the Financial Ombudsman.

Those who took out finance deals for new cars provided by captive lenders – which are carmakers with their own in-house banking arm – could also miss out. Many of the big carmakers, including Volkswagen and BMW, have their own in-house banking operations.

How much compensation could you get?

Exact amounts will depend on individual circumstances, but the average compensation is due to be £829.

This is far less than the £1,500 average payout that groups such as the Fair Banking all-party parliamentary group put forward.

The FCA suggested the payout scheme will total £7.5bn, with an added £1.6bn in administrative costs for lenders.

For most people, interest will be paid on the payout, from the date of overpayment to the date the compensation is paid, based on the annual average Bank of England Bank Rate per year, plus 1pc. This will be set at a minimum of 3pc.

The FCA said one in three payouts will be capped to “ensure no one is put in a better position than had they been treated fairly”.

What happens next?

This depends on where you are in the claims timeline of events.

If you have already lodged a claim

You do not need to do anything. Just hold tight and wait to hear the outcome of your case.

The timeline for you to potentially get compensation will be:

  1. Redress scheme begins mid-2026
  2. Lenders will confirm if you’re owed compensation and how much within three months
  3. You are paid what you’re owed within one month of accepting a compensation offer
  4. If you are unhappy with the outcome, you’ll be able to take your complaint to the Financial Ombudsman, or you can go through the courts.

If you plan to lodge a claim

Get in touch with the lender or broker which sold you a finance deal, and detail your claim. The FCA has an easy-to-use template letter on its website which you can download and fill in.

You’ll need your policy number, relevant dates and vehicle details.

If you are unsure who your lender was at the time, check historic bank statements or contact the dealership where you bought the car, and they may be able to help.

According to the FCA, if your agreement was active in the last six years, you should check your credit file, as your provider may be listed on there.

After making a complaint, your provider should send you an acknowledgement within eight weeks.

Submitting a complaint now will ensure your case is already in the system when lenders begin their assessments when the redress scheme goes live.

If you don’t lodge a claim before August 31

If you do not complain before the end of August, you have not missed the boat.

This is because lenders will have to assess historic finance deals to see if they meet the conditions of the scheme. They will then write to you inviting you to lodge a claim.

If it thinks you are due redress, your lender should contact you by the end of 2026 for finance deals made after April 1 2014. For agreements made between April 6 2007 and March 31 2014, lenders have until February 2027 to make contact.

Be aware that if you do not submit the claim after being contacted by your lender, then the case will not be automatically assessed and you will miss the opportunity for compensation.

If you fall under this category, the timeline of events will be:

  1. Redress scheme begins mid-2026.
  2. Lenders write to you on how to join the scheme within six months.
  3. You join the scheme within six months.
  4. Lenders confirm if you’re owed compensation and how much within three months.
  5. You are paid compensation within one month of accepting the offer.

If you think you should have been contacted but haven’t been, this could be because your old lender does not have contact details for you.

In this scenario, you have one year from the launch of the redress scheme to lodge a claim on your own behalf.

Is this the end of the scandal?

Despite the FCA’s announcement, the saga could rumble on even longer.

Those who have already submitted claims are expected to receive offers and payouts in 2026, though this could be delayed further if lenders or claims management firms challenge the FCA’s decision.

The FCA’s guidance explains that it has split the redress scheme in two; one covers loans between April 6 2007-March 31 2014, and the other for the period between April 1 2014 and November 1 2024.

As some consultation respondents suggested that agreements before 2014 should not be included, the FCA disagrees but has split the early period so that should earlier period claims be subject to legal challenges, redress for more recent deals shouldn’t be delayed.

Legal challenges could escalate to higher courts, potentially stalling payouts even further.

The FCA also has plans for any companies not following the rules. Its supervisory team will check firms are taking the right steps to compensate eligible customers, while a taskforce has been set up to tackle the poor handling of car finance claims by some claims management companies and law firms.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph processing your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: European food giant forces through emergency price rise
data.description:
One of Britain’s biggest food suppliers is pushing through emergency price increases as the war in Iran sends shipping and ingredient costs soaring.

Princes, which sells canned tuna, Super Noodles and Branston beans, has warned European supermarkets it will raise prices from Wednesday after the Middle East conflict unleashed “unprecedented cost pressures”.

In a letter to supermarkets on the Continent, Giuseppe Mastrolia, Princes’ commercial chief, blamed the closure of the Strait of Hormuz for an urgent 5pc price increase across all products.

According to The Grocer, which first reported the letter, Mr Mastrolia said: “The closure of the Strait of Hormuz – a critical corridor for approximately 20pc of the world’s oil and gas – is generating unprecedented cost pressures across the entire food and drink industry.”

He said the pressures were “affecting every stage of our supply chain, from raw material sourcing and manufacturing to packaging and distribution”.

Mr Mastrolia highlighted a 60pc increase in oil and gas prices since the war broke out in Iran late last month, compounded by a 120pc jump in shipping fees. It is understood that Princes has struck cheaper deals for both.

Despite this, the food supplier said it was preparing to charge customers at least 5pc more for its products from April 1.

It is understood that this will currently apply only to its European customers, including supermarkets in Germany and Italy.

Princes sent the letter on March 20, giving retailers less than two weeks’ notice of the planned price increase.

Typically, supermarkets are warned of price rises 12 weeks in advance, allowing for a longer period to negotiate lower prices.

The price threat to European supermarkets will raise fears that Princes could soon be forced to push up prices in Britain as well, with the company believed to be keeping the situation under review.

It also highlights the speed at which the fallout from the conflict is filtering through to food manufacturers, with Princes preparing to raise prices just over a month after the war started.

The Institute of Grocery Distribution has forecast that food inflation could hit 8pc by June if the conflict drags on into the summer, fuelled by soaring energy costs.

Inflation fears prompted Rachel Reeves to organise a sit-down with British supermarket bosses last week over what they were doing to protect their customers.

However, the meeting was postponed after almost all of the chief executives said they could not attend. It is now slated to take place on Wednesday instead.

It threatens to prove a tense meeting after senior leaders from Asda and Marks & Spencer criticised Labour over its pledge to crack down on “profiteering”.

Sir Keir Starmer and Rachel Reeves have warned businesses not to take advantage of the situation in Iran by raising prices too sharply.

However, Allan Leighton, the boss of Asda, told The Telegraph last week that claims of profiteering had “zero credibility”.

He said: “The Government strategy is called ‘Point your finger at somebody else’.”

Separately, Stuart Machin, the boss of Marks & Spencer, accused the Government of driving up businesses’ bills, claiming so-called “policy costs” accounted for a significant portion of his company’s energy costs.

The British Retail Consortium (BRC) has also claimed that the Government risks exacerbating price rises through its own policies, including its overhaul of workers’ rights and its obesity crackdown.

Figures from the BRC showed shop price inflation rose 1.2pc in March, up from 1.1pc in February.

Helen Dickinson, the chief executive of the BRC, said: “Higher costs resulting from the conflict in the Middle East are starting to feed into supply chains.

“While retailers will work with their suppliers to mitigate the impact on prices as far as possible, inflation will rise, although there are no indications it will reach the peaks of the last spike in April 2023.”

She urged ministers to “look at all the costs that could exacerbate these price rises, from new healthy food rules to trade changes with Europe, the Employment Rights Act and non-commodity charges which make up such a large proportion of energy bills”, adding that “ignoring businesses’ concerns risks even higher prices for shoppers”.

Looming tensions 

Meanwhile, Princes’ price threat risks reigniting tensions with supermarkets.

Retailers have previously pushed back against brands’ attempts to force through price increases during times of crisis.

Heinz was pulled from Tesco’s shelves in 2022 after the supermarket rejected the brand’s planned price rises in the wake of the Ukraine war.

At the time, Tesco claimed the increases from Heinz were “unjustifiable”.

A spokesman for Princes said: “The conflict in Iran has significantly disrupted the global oil supply and driven up fuel and shipping costs across all our markets.

“Like every food manufacturer, we are facing substantial cost increases across our supply chain. Where those costs are unavoidable and material, we act to recover them.”

The spokesman added: “We remain committed to working transparently with our customers and to keeping food affordable. We will only pass on inflation where we absolutely have to.”

data.title: Strip ex-Environment Agency chief of knighthood, say water campaigners
data.description:
Campaigners are calling for the former chief executive of the Environment Agency to be stripped of his knighthood, claiming he failed to combat sewage pollution while in post.

A petition calling for Sir James Bevan to be stripped of the honour has attracted almost 5,000 signatures since it was launched last week.

Sir James, who ran the Environment Agency from 2015 until March 2023, has been criticised for overseeing a reduction in water quality monitoring and accused of gagging staff from speaking out about the state of Britain’s rivers.

His track record came under scrutiny in the Channel 4 docuseries, Dirty Business, which aired earlier this month.

The series depicts the veteran mandarin as praising “operator self-monitoring”, meaning water companies were effectively allowed to mark their own homework on pollution rather than Environment Agency officers testing rivers.

Sir James downplayed concerns about pollution while at the helm of the Environment Agency. In 2023, he told a water industry conference that the debate around sewage pollution should be held “on the basis of facts not assertions”, adding: “There are some wild assertions, myths, and outright untruths flying around.”

The former Environment Agency chief also faced accusations of gagging staff. In an email to employees, Sir James said staff risked being sacked if they criticised or discredited the Environment Agency. Staff were even warned about commenting on the Environment Agency negatively inside work.

Southern Water was fined £90m during Sir James’s tenure after admitting to thousands of pollution incidents. It became clear that the Environment Agency had failed to identify that Southern Water was misreporting sewage discharges.

Carly Hardwidge, who set up the petition, said: “Revoking the knighthood from Sir James would send a powerful message. This action would restore the public’s faith in our honours system and reinforce the laudable aims of environmental accountability.”

Feargal Sharkey, the veteran water campaigner, backed the petition, saying: “I’d have to support the idea that he needs his knighthood removed because I can think of no reason why he should have ever earned it nor be allowed to retain it.”

‘Rewarding failure’

Since leaving the Environment Agency, Sir James has gone on to be a non-executive director at Welsh Water as well as a trustee of the Canal and Rivers Trust.

Earlier this week Welsh Water was hit with a £44.7m penalty following breaches in the supplier’s sewage network.

Henry Tuffnell, a Labour MP in Wales who sits on the Environment Committee, said: “Welsh Water dramatically needs to improve their environmental performance, investment and relationship with customers.”

Mr Tuffnell added: “It is therefore very concerning that they have rewarded failure in hiring Sir James Bevan, who has such a poor performance record from the EA.”

One of the key criticisms of the utilities industry is that weak executives move into the non-exec roles where they are ill-suited to challenging poor performance.

Alistair Carmichael, the head of the Environment Committee, said: “The problems of the water industry are ultimately problems of culture within the industry. Many of the problems come from poor corporate governance where under-performing executives are not properly challenged by regulators and their own non-exec directors.”

He added: “A revolving door where people can move easily from being poachers to gamekeepers serves only to make that problem worse. We will know if the Government is serious about changing the culture of the industry if, when they set up their new regulator, they take steps to lock that revolving door.”

A spokesman for Welsh Water said: “We appointed Sir James Bevan as a non-executive director in February 2025 following a rigorous search and appointment process.

“The Channel 4 programme is a television drama based on historical events relating to regulatory matters in England. Some dialogue, characters and scenes have been ‘recreated’ for the purposes of dramatisation. The portrayal of Sir James Bevan does not reflect the experience of the board since his appointment.”
