data.title: Sadiq Khan could wage war on ‘Chelsea tractor’ owners with new charges
data.description:
Sir Sadiq Khan is considering waging a war on “Chelsea tractor” owners with new charges, a new document suggests.

Transport for London’s (TfL) Vision Zero action plan claimed there was a “pressing need for targeted safety interventions” against larger vehicles.

It stated the size of large SUVs – nicknamed “Chelsea tractors” – made roads “more hazardous for everybody outside the vehicle” because they “significantly increase the severity of collisions”.

The plan is part of Sir Sadiq’s efforts to eliminate deaths and serious injuries on London’s roads by 2041.

There is no specific definition of an SUV, but it is a term given to vehicles that are generally taller, wider, heavier and less fuel-efficient than traditional cars. Critics have pointed out that electric vehicles (EVs) are just as heavy, but would not be affected by the plans.

Thomas Turrell, a member of the London Assembly, said: “This family car tax is a double standard. The mayor will not go after his precious heavy EV cars with this regressive, backwards tax, instead choosing to hammer families.

“His anti-car zealotry will not stop here. If he truly believes vehicles should be taxed for being heavier, he should be consistent rather than money-grabbing.”

Mike Hawes, the chief executive of the Society of Motor Manufacturers and Traders, said all cars sold in the UK “regardless of size” must meet “exacting safety and pedestrian protection standards”.

He added: “Singling out specific cars based on size restricts consumer choice and would unfairly penalise the many drivers who require a larger vehicle for essential mobility.”

No proposals about possible charges for SUVs have been put forward yet.

SUVs account for about a third of all new cars sold in the UK. They have grown in popularity in recent years, with many drivers favouring their higher seating position.

The Vision Zero document claimed “oversized” vehicles such as SUVs made it “harder for drivers to see people walking and cycling nearby”, adding that children were “more easily hidden from a driver’s view”.

It stated that in Britain, SUVs were 14 per cent more likely to kill pedestrians and cyclists than other passenger cars if there was a collision, and 77 per cent more likely to kill children.

Punishing London’s motorists

Sir Sadiq expanded the ultra-low-emission zone to cover the whole of London in August 2023, which requires drivers of vehicles that do not meet emissions standards to pay a daily fee of £12.50.

London also has an £18 congestion charge for driving in a zone in the centre of the capital at certain times.

Sir Sadiq has faced sustained criticism from ordinary Londoners that he ignores their concerns about schemes that punish motorists.

One such scheme was formally approved this week, which involves the construction of a cycle path at the Holland Park roundabout in west London, one of the capital’s busiest road interchanges.

Residents have repeatedly objected to the £6m scheme and have claimed that TfL has cited false statistics generated to justify the works.

Ruth Saunders, a member of the Save Our Streets group based near the roundabout, said: “This scheme is designed to benefit a small number of commuter cyclists travelling through the borough for a few hours each day, to the detriment of local residents and other road users.”

Regarding the potential plans to tax SUVs, a spokesman for Sir Sadiq claimed: “There is growing evidence about the safety risks of large SUVs, which are becoming more common on London’s roads.

“The mayor has asked TfL to undertake detailed analysis of the safety risks posed by large SUVs and their wider impact on London’s roads.

“This could then inform any future policy proposals in London or recommendations to Government or the sector.

“As the numbers of large SUVs increase on London’s roads, it is right that TfL consider their impact on safety and how they affect other road users.”

data.title: Millions of drivers in line for £830 each from car finance scandal
data.description:
Millions of drivers who were mis-sold car finance will receive around £830 each after the details of a new compensation scheme were published on Monday.

Some 12.1 million car loan deals involving mis-selling dating back to 2007 will be covered by the new programme overseen by City watchdog the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA).

Each will get an average £830 payout, higher than the £700 drivers had been expecting.

However, the eligibility criteria have been tightened, meaning roughly two million drivers who had been initially in line for a payout will now miss out.

It was previously thought that some 14.2 million deals would be eligible for compensation.

Bank bosses had lobbied for the scheme to be watered down. Lenders argued that some claims should be thrown out, arguing that earlier plans by the FCA set the parameters of the scheme too broadly.

Alex Neill, a co-founder of consumer rights group Consumer Voice, said: “While compensation payouts for some have increased, it comes at the expense of millions of other drivers who are now excluded altogether. The FCA has narrowed eligibility and reduced the overall bill for lenders, raising real concerns that many people will still be undercompensated.

“Millions of people were overcharged, and our research shows some were pushed into real financial difficulty. This was the regulator’s chance to put that right, but it instead appears to have let lenders off the hook.”

The FCA banned car dealers in 2021 from receiving commissions from lenders based on the level of interest rate charged to customers. These discretionary commission arrangements were often not disclosed to customers.

After a Supreme Court judgment, the regulator also said that other types of agreements were unfair, including those involving excessively high commissions. Tied arrangements, which gave a lender exclusivity or the first right of refusal without clearly informing the customer, were also judged to be unfair.

However, the FCA said on Monday that finance agreements involving minimal commission will no longer receive redress, meaning roughly two million deals will not qualify for the scheme.

High-value loans involving luxury cars will be excluded, as the FCA has said they are “not suitable for a mass-market redress scheme”.

For those who do qualify, the FCA said it expects millions of consumers to be compensated this year, and most of the rest by the end of 2027.

In a third of cases, payouts will be capped with carmakers on the hook for £7.5bn in compensation costs, down from an original estimate of £8.2bn. The total cost of delivering the scheme will be just £9.1bn, down from £11bn, after the FCA loosened rules on customer complaints.

However, the compensation plans could still face a judicial review by banks, which are reportedly preparing to mount a legal challenge.

At the same time, at least one law firm, representing a large number of victims, has pledged to sidestep the scheme amid concerns that it is too lenient. It is instead looking to pursue some of its claims directly through the courts.

Risk of judicial review

Lenders who wish to challenge the scheme will have 28 days to present their case to a tribunal, which could then go to a higher court for a decision. This risks causing further delays to the scheme.

Major banks, including Lloyds, have already set aside billions of pounds to pay claims. Close Brothers has cut hundreds of jobs owing to its exposure to the compensation scheme. Close Brothers said in a statement that it was assessing the potential implications of the redress scheme on the group.

Rachael Jones, of Autotrader, said: “We support this pragmatic and proportionate approach from the FCA that strikes the right balance between ensuring robust protection and transparency for consumers, while underpinning the stability of an automotive sector that contributes billions to the UK economy every year.

“It’s vital that this scheme doesn’t inadvertently impact a market that has adapted and is now working well for consumers.”

At the same time, at least one law firm representing a large number of victims has pledged to sidestep the scheme amid concerns that it is too lenient. It is instead looking to pursue some of its claims directly through the courts.

Darren Smith, the managing director of Courmacs Legal, a law firm based in Blackburn dealing with four million car finance claims, said: “The FCA’s final scheme is a complete failure for consumer rights. By prioritising lender balance sheets over fair compensation, the regulator has abandoned vulnerable motorists. Lenders are being let off the hook again.”

The payouts relate to finance agreements, which are used in the purchase of the vast majority of new cars.

The FCA had originally wanted the compensation scheme to be up and running by early 2026. However, pressure from lenders and extended consultation have led to delays.

Nikhil Rathi, the chief executive of the FCA, said: “We’ve listened to feedback to make sure the scheme is fair for consumers and proportionate for firms. It will put £7.5bn back into people’s pockets.

“Now we need everyone to get behind it and ensure millions get their money this year. Payouts should not be delayed any longer, especially as household bills come under greater pressure. Delivering compensation promptly also gives lenders the chance to rebuild trust, and means we can draw a line under the past and support a healthy motor finance market for the future.”

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Net zero zealots are putting Gen Z off building planes
data.description:
Britain’s aerospace companies are gearing up for their biggest boom in a generation.

Record airliner orders will require plane makers and their suppliers to lift production by a fifth in the next few years, creating well-paid jobs for thousands of workers.

Yet a UK aerospace industry that produced the jet engine, the Harrier jump jet and Concorde is being hampered by a growing recruitment crisis.

Years of demonisation by green campaigners such as Greta Thunberg for its inability to wean itself off fossil fuels are beginning to take their toll as young people turn away from careers in industry.

A lack of inspirational new aircraft and the allure of rival jobs in technology and AI are compounding the situation, leaving even the biggest firms struggling to hire.

Airbus, which employs 15,000 people in the UK, is resorting to converting one of its Beluga wing transporters into a recruitment centre in an effort to bolster its appeal to Gen Z.

The firm will fit the plane with classrooms and a mock-up of its assembly line as it seeks to recruit hundreds of apprentices a year to the Broughton wing plant in North Wales.

Bosses are hoping that Nasa’s Artemis programme to return humans to the Moon by 2028 will also help restore some of the wider industry’s lost glamour.

Richard Gardner, the honorary president of Farnborough Air Sciences Trust, said Gen Z had been turned off aviation by eco-zealots who wrongly condemn the sector for its environmental impact.

He suggested the problem starts in school, where children are increasingly taught about the impacts of climate change.

He said: “We talk about the word brainwashing, which is quite emotive for some people, but that is what it is.

“The teaching unions are very influential in terms of the way that profession is going, and they are pretty green in their tendencies.”

Gardner added: “I think they mostly buy into these green policies, science and economics teachers aside, and that aerospace and defence are therefore not high in their career recommendations.”

He said the recent rise of the Green Party as an electoral force is also a “big worry” for aviation.

Green policies include a commitment to banning domestic flights for journeys that could be made by train, halting airport expansion and imposing a tax on frequent travellers.

Kevin Craven, the chief executive of ADS, the trade group for aerospace, defence and space firms, said that while the industry might not be decarbonising fast enough for some young people, its focus on reducing emissions may be failing to motivate others.

He said: “There is a feeling that aerospace has faded into the background a bit because there are no longer any mountains being climbed.

“Concorde, the Comet, the jet engine and the Harrier were all British firsts that really excited and inspired people.”

Craven added: “All new aircraft are really a cylinder with wings and look approximately the same. There are incremental advances which are huge for sustainability, but it’s hard to see that progression.

“There is also huge competition from other high-tech arenas – particularly AI – and, more generally, the City, where vast swathes of money are available to the best and brightest.”

Mr Craven said it was galling to see tech companies recruiting the most able engineers, even though their electricity consumption is set to double by 2030, creating clear parallels with the net zero challenges facing aviation.

He said: “When you look at cloud computing, on the other side of it is a dirty big data centre requiring huge amounts of power and huge amounts of cooling.”

‘Incredibly competitive world’

Sue Partridge, the UK country manager for Airbus, said the world’s biggest plane maker had turned to initiatives such as the Beluga recruitment project as it prepares to deliver 1,000 jet planes a year as early as 2027, up from 793 in 2025.

Airbus’s Filton, Gloucestershire, wing design and development centre near Bristol faces competition for A-level students and graduate recruits from a broad spectrum of rival employers, she said.

The firm, which has trained 4,000 British recruits in the past decade, also faces a fight to hire 16-year-old apprentices for its assembly line in Broughton, Flintshire, with Merseyside’s carmakers and the cluster of green energy firms on nearby Deeside, Wales.

She said: “It’s an incredibly competitive world out there, and we need to do everything we can to attract talent and secure those skills we are going to need for the future.

“But it is difficult, particularly in newer digital skill areas, where we are competing with Google and companies like that, which was never the case in the past.”

Partridge added: “With the younger cohort, the challenge is attracting them to the industry in the first place and inspiring them so that they want to actually build aeroplanes. They get influenced in their career choice at an early age.

“A few years ago, all the stories were about flight shaming, and aerospace was the bad guy. That’s why we want to get into schools so they really connect with Airbus and aerospace manufacturing.”

The Beluga, named after the white whale whose bloated fuselage it resembles, garners rare attention for a modern plane and would be a formidable tool in encouraging take-up of Stem (science, technology, engineering and mathematics) subjects, Ms Partridge said.

“There are still inspirational stories we can tell, and I think the Beluga is one,” she said. “We know people love the plane, and we can use that as a symbol.

“It’s an iconic aircraft, and if you see one in the sky, for me, it’s just as impactful as Concorde used to be. We already have a Concorde near Filton, South Gloucestershire, and we can use this in the same way.”

Mr Craven said the development of so-called flying taxis, in which Britain is playing a leading role, is one area that should resonate with younger people.

That is largely “because they will be silent, and there won’t be obvious pollutants pouring out of the back”.

However, it is not clear how big the market for the electric vertical take-off craft will be, with some industry observers concerned that they will be limited to whisking the very rich to and from first-class flights, a role hardly likely to endear them to Gen Z.

Industry experts hope that a new era of manned space travel could achieve greater cut-through.

Elon Musk’s SpaceX has made space a “much closer frontier”, while the Artemis missions have the potential to capture hearts and minds just as the Apollo programme did in the 1960s and 1970s, said Mr Craven.

He said: “Aerospace and space are joined at the hip, and whenever we ask an astronaut to an event, there is a frisson of excitement. People want heroes, and that combination of high-tech and courage is the ultimate inspiration.

“I can remember watching the Moon landing on a black and white TV with a 10-inch screen in the middle of Africa. It’s probably why I ended up as an engineer.”

data.title: Apple fined for breaching Russian sanctions
data.description:
Apple has been fined £390,000 after it was found to have breached Russian sanctions.

The technology giant has been handed a penalty over two payments totalling £635,618 in June and July 2022 that it made to Okko, a Russian streaming app, Britain’s sanctions watchdog said.

Until May 2022, Okko was owned by Sberbank, a bank controlled by Moscow that was sanctioned after Vladimir Putin’s invasion of Ukraine. It was then sold to a new owner, JSC New Opportunities, which itself was sanctioned by the Foreign Office in June that year.

However, the Office of Financial Sanctions Implementation (OFSI) found that Apple Distribution Limited, an international branch of Apple based in Ireland, continued to make payments to Okko on June 30 and July 28 in 2022.

OFSI said there had been a “narrow window in which the [first] payment could potentially have been cancelled” before it completed, taking into account JSC’s sanctioned status.

The watchdog said Apple had also made more than £1.2m in payments to Okko in April that year. At the time, Okko was controlled by Sberbank, which was already sanctioned. However, these payments occurred before stricter sanctions liability rules were introduced.

According to a Government penalty notice, Apple had relied on “self-certification” by app developers to establish whether their ownership could pose a sanctions risk, as well as third-party due diligence.

The watchdog said that Apple’s failure to enhance its sanctions processes in early 2022 “by affirmatively requesting ownership information from Russian-paid app developers very likely contributed to the breach payments occurring”.

It added that Apple’s potential fine had been reduced from £600,000 after it voluntarily disclosed the sanctions failure to officials and entered settlement talks.

The penalty notice said Apple was not aware that Okko was sanctioned and the breach was not intentional.

An Apple spokesman said: “We follow the laws in the countries where we operate and take sanctions compliance extremely seriously. After identifying two payments to a developer that days earlier had become affiliated with a sanctioned entity, we promptly and proactively reported our findings to the UK Government.

“We are constantly working to enhance our already robust compliance protocols, which are consistent with industry standards.”

Apple has previously been fined thousands of pounds by Russian courts for failing to censor content deemed illegal by Moscow. It also paid a $13m (£9.8m) fine to Moscow in 2024 over alleged competition law violations in Russia.

The iPhone-maker halted all product sales in Russia in March 2022. However, the company’s App Store still works in the country. Apple has previously obeyed demands from Russian regulators to block apps used to evade censorship.

The fine from the OFSI comes amid a wider crackdown by the regulator on companies and entrepreneurs backed by Moscow.

More than £28.7bn of frozen Russian assets have been reported to OFSI, which is part of the Treasury.

In its annual report last year, OFSI said it had issued fines to businesses for sanctions breaches in 2025 totalling £500,000. Last year, it fined Herbert Smith Freehills, a top City law firm, £465,000 over payments to Russian banks.

The law firm said the payments were down to “human error” during the winding up of its Russian offices.

data.title: AstraZeneca to invest £11bn in China after snubbing Britain
data.description:
AstraZeneca has unveiled plans to invest $15bn (£11bn) in China just months after dropping hundreds of millions of pounds of investment in Britain.

The Cambridge-based company announced the investment, which will be used to expand its medical manufacturing and research capabilities, during Sir Keir Starmer’s visit to Beijing. The Prime Minister hailed the financing as a deal that would help to support British jobs back home.

But the announcement follows a period of frosty relations between the Government and AstraZeneca which has seen the drugmaker threaten to halt investment in Britain in a row over NHS pricing.

The pharmaceutical giant, which is Britain’s biggest listed company, has abandoned plans for a £200m expansion of its Cambridge research site and £450m overhaul of its Liverpool vaccine factory.

The deal may also raise eyebrows given that five of AstraZeneca’s staff were detained by authorities in China in 2024 amid claims of potential illegal activity. The president of its China division was subsequently placed under investigation by local police, with no further details provided.

Astra is the largest drugs company in China, which is the company’s second-biggest market after the US.

Separately, AstraZeneca also announced on Friday that it had struck a deal worth as much as $18.2bn with China’s CSPC Pharmaceuticals.

Under the tie-up, AstraZeneca will have the global rights to sell CSPC’s obesity drugs outside China.

CSPC is currently developing once-monthly weight-loss jabs. The biggest weight-loss treatments currently require people to inject themselves once a week.

In total, if all of CSPC’s eight obesity drugs make it to market, AstraZeneca said the deal could be worth as much as $18.2bn – including future sales of the weight-loss treatments.

Sir Pascal Soriot, the company’s chief executive, is one of a clutch of Western business leaders who are occasionally summoned for meetings with Xi Jinping, the Chinese premier.

Sir Pascal said on Thursday: “Today’s landmark investment of $15bn begins an exciting next chapter for AstraZeneca in China, which has become a critical contributor to scientific innovation, advanced manufacturing and global public health.”

The “landmark” move was recognition of China’s growing scientific capabilities in areas that could help unlock new treatments for cancer, haematological conditions and autoimmune diseases, among others. It will be focused on sites in Bejing, Shanghai, Wuxi, Taizhou and Qingdao.

Sir Keir said: “Unlocking opportunities for British businesses across the globe and delivering for working people back home is always the driving force behind my international engagements.

“AstraZeneca’s expansion and leadership in China will help the British manufacturer continue to grow – supporting thousands of UK jobs.”

Astra has been at loggerheads with the Government over how much the NHS pays for medicines.

The company and its rivals have argued that the way the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (Nice) evaluates “value for money” on medicines is flawed and deprives patients of potential breakthrough treatments.

For instance, Nice previously rejected Enhertu, Astra’s breast cancer drug.

Wes Streeting, the Health Secretary, walked away from talks with drugmakers last summer after failing to strike an agreement.

But in December, ministers announced a new deal with the US that saw Nice agree to pay up to 25pc more for treatments, a move aimed at protecting the UK life sciences industry.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Marie Claire publisher loses a third of its value after warning of AI hit
data.description:
The publisher behind Marie Claire and Country Life lost a third of its value after warning AI search tools were hitting reader numbers.

Shares in Future, which also owns The Week and price comparison site Go.Compare, crashed by 30pc on Tuesday after it admitted a decline in traffic from Google had been “more pronounced than anticipated”.

Publishers have been left reeling from Google’s rollout of AI overviews at the top of its search results. These are designed to answer search queries using information scraped from websites and have been blamed for sharp drops in the number of people clicking through to sites.

Future said the change had led to a fall in readership, denting its advertising and e-commerce revenues and driving up the cost of ad spots across the industry.

The company warned that revenue would decline by a low single-digit percentage in the second half of the year. This could mean a fall of anywhere between 1pc and 5pc.

The share slump wiped almost £100m off the company’s market value. Future has suffered a collapse over the past five years, with the company’s value down 90pc since 2021 as shares fell from nearly £40 each to just £2.75 today.

According to the Reuters Institute, traffic from Google is down by a third to news sites because of the AI change and news outlets expect it to plunge a further 43pc over the next three years.

The Competition and Markets Authority said in January that Google will have to let publishers decide whether they appear in AI summaries after the News Media Association – which represents titles including The Times, The Guardian and The Telegraph – accused the tech giant of “exploiting” its dominant position in search.

Google will also be banned from “manipulating” its search results by retaliating against companies that criticise it or favouring businesses that spend heavily on Google advertising. Google denies engaging in such practices.

Nevertheless, the rollout of the new technology is still hurting free-to-read websites reliant on digital advertising.

Reach, which publishes The Mirror and Daily Express as well as dozens of regional titles, suffered its biggest loss in more than a decade last year following a drop in reader numbers.

Future has launched an initiative called “Google Zero”, aimed at reducing its reliance on search referrals for revenue.

The publisher is instead seeking to make more money directly from its readers and is offering new services such as helping brands become more visible on AI services such as ChatGPT.

Future said its takeover of SheerLuxe, a social media-based beauty and fashion brand, was progressing well and that price comparison site Go.Compare had returned to growth.

The Future board said it believed the company was fundamentally undervalued and would explore options for brands that were not delivering value for shareholders.

Kevin Li Ying, the chief executive, said: “Whilst we are disappointed with the impact of the changes in the search ecosystem on our near-term trading performance, we are making good progress in executing the elements of our growth strategy that are in our control.

“This includes a laser focus on driving the platform effect to optimise monetisation across our brands through our Google Zero strategy and leveraging AI as a new source of revenue through products like Future Optic.

“The board remains determined to drive a return to growth and to unlock the substantial value from our unique portfolio of assets.”

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Morrisons to sell dozens of pharmacies in scramble to cut costs
data.description:
Morrisons has put dozens of pharmacies up for sale in a fresh effort to cut costs.

The private equity-owned supermarket has launched a sale process after concluding that many of its in-store pharmacies are not financially viable.

As a result of the review, property agents have been tasked with selling some of the pharmacies on a store-by-store basis, rather than the entire portfolio.

Those affected are likely to remain open in stores, but under the brand of any eventual buyer.

The move to sell dozens of pharmacies comes after Morrisons’ losses plunged to £381m last year, fuelled by steep borrowing costs.

Rising debt pressures have led bosses to reduce borrowings, which fell to £3.1bn from £3.5bn last summer.

The sale of pharmacies is the latest attempt to strip back the business and focus increasingly on traditional retail.

A separate restructuring last year triggered the closure of four pharmacies, alongside 52 cafés, 17 convenience stores, 35 meat counters and 13 florists.

This led to more than 300 jobs being put at risk of redundancy, as bosses identified areas “where the costs of operations are significantly out of line with usage, volumes, or the value that customers place on them”.

Rami Baitieh, chief executive of Morrisons, said the cuts were a “necessary part of our plans to renew and reinvigorate Morrisons”.

Morrisons is pursuing a turnaround strategy after Mr Baitieh said the business had been going through a “challenging period”.

The retail boss blamed weaker consumer spending among poorer shoppers, many of whom have been affected by price rises.

This has led to Morrisons struggling to boost its share of the grocery market, which is at 8.4pc.

That is up narrowly from 8.5pc this time last year, although it is well below the 9.9pc it achieved in October 2021, when it was bought by private equity firm Clayton Dubilier & Rice in a £10bn deal.

This has prompted speculation that Morrisons could lose its position as Britain’s fifth-largest supermarket to Lidl, which now boasts 7.7pc of the grocery market.

Morrisons is not alone in seeking to sell off pharmacies, as the sector has been battered by rising costs in recent years.

Around 1,400 pharmacies have shut in England since 2017, according to the National Pharmacy Association, leaving many households unable to access medicine in their areas.

This has been blamed on rising wage costs and a shortfall in how much the NHS pays pharmacies.

Boots has also closed hundreds of pharmacies in recent years, while LloydsPharmacy, which had 138 sites, collapsed in 2023.

Rachel Reeves has piled more pressure on pharmacies over the past year by increasing business rates and the minimum wage.

Supermarkets have also come under pressure from rising taxes under Labour, most notably by the Chancellor’s decision to increase employer National Insurance rates.

Speaking last month, Mr Baitieh pleaded with the Government to avoid hitting retailers with any extra costs.

He said: “I would say, please, no more. This is very important for the market. We expect inflation to go up.”

Morrisons declined to comment.

data.title: 120-year-old British wheel factory at risk of closure
data.description:
A 120-year-old British wheel factory is at risk of closure after its German owners called in the administrators.

Moveero, which makes wheels, rims and hubs for off-road vehicles used in farming and construction, said its UK arm had become insolvent on Thursday, putting around 250 jobs at risk.

Administrators from Interpath have been appointed to run the struggling business, which trades from a historic factory in Telford, Shropshire.

Its customers include JCB and Caterpillar, among others, with its products used for the wheels of everything from earthmovers to airport tugs and tree harvesters.

The business, dating back to 1908, was part of GKN’s automotive division but was sold to German private equity firm Aurelius in 2020.

Aurelius was also behind a disastrous buyout of ethical beauty chain the Body Shop, which collapsed just five months after its new owners took control.

In a statement on Thursday, Ryan Grant, Chris Pole and Sam Birchall, administrators at Interpath, said Moveero had been battling “significant operational challenges”, including weak demand for its products and tough competition.

Rising interest rates have made buying heavy equipment more difficult for farmers and construction companies, along with trade tariffs imposed on imports by the likes of the US and China.

Moveero Ltd reported a loss of £4.4m in 2024, the latest year for which there are accounts, after sales fell 26pc to £32.6m.

The wider Moveero group, which includes businesses in the US and Denmark, continues to operate profitably and is not insolvent.

In the UK, administrators have been appointed to “protect the interests of creditors”.

A statement said this would “allow the UK businesses to continue trading while buyers for the businesses and their assets are pursued, with all staff retained during this period”.

David Geraghty, Moveero’s chief executive, said the company’s customers had also provided emergency funding that had “given us the additional time we need to continue to explore the options available”.

Mr Grant, the managing director at Interpath, added: “We have been pleased with the positive conversations and financial support provided by customers in recent days, which has ultimately provided the businesses with a crucial lifeline, enabling us to trade while we explore options for the business.”

Interpath said its “immediate priority is to seek buyers for the UK businesses and assets” and urged potential buyers to come forward as soon as possible.

Aurelius has been approached for comment.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Denmark’s fat jab champion is slimming down the whole economy
data.description:
Novo Nordisk, Denmark’s fat jab pharmaceutical giant, reached an unwelcome milestone last week.

After a dizzying 76pc plunge in its share price over the past 18 months, a torrid week of trading meant it finally gave up the last of its market gains recorded since June 2021 when it released pioneering weight-loss drug Wegovy.

It follows years in which Novo Nordisk has been arguably Denmark’s greatest global success story. Its first-mover success with Wegovy and Ozempic turned it into Europe’s largest company, with the $100bn (£74bn) weight-loss drugs market at its feet.

The tide has since turned. This week, Novo admitted that its newest drugs were showing less promise in trials than those of its American rival Eli Lilly. It has also yielded to Donald Trump’s demand for hefty price cuts in the hitherto lucrative US market.

Novo is now in a metaphorical weight-loss programme of its own. It has shed its long-serving chief executive, cut 11pc of its workforce and lost hordes of foreign shareholders.

This is not a typical tale of corporate tumult. Novo is such a colossus in Denmark that its ructions can shake the country’s whole economy.

Yet Mette Frederiksen, the country’s Left-wing prime minister, chose this moment to take the country to an early election, set for March 24.

Since taking office in 2019, Frederiksen has steered Denmark through the turbulence of the pandemic, the Ukraine war and Trump’s threats to seize Greenland.

She will be hoping that Novo’s travails do not derail her bid for a third straight election victory.

It is something of a gamble. Novo’s fate is inextricably linked to that of the economy and thus to her own political prospects.

The company sold 309bn krone (£36bn) of drugs last year, which is more than 10pc of Denmark’s annual $430bn GDP. It employs one in every 100 Danes.

As Novo’s crisis deepened, Denmark’s economy – which was one of the fastest-growing in Europe – slowed dramatically at the end of last year. The country recorded 2.2pc growth in the third quarter, falling to just 0.2pc in the fourth.

Per Hansen, an economist at bank Nordnet, says: “One of the engines that has been driving the Danish economy over the past two to three years has been Novo Nordisk and the pharma industry.”

Las Olsen, the chief economist at Danske Bank, says that if you remove the pharmaceuticals sector, Denmark’s growth rate would be more like 1pc or 1.5pc.

“Which is fine, of course – but not the stellar growth rate that we have seen in the headline numbers,” he says. “I think we will grow more in line with European economies, perhaps a little less.”

These aren’t views that will cheer up the gloomy Danes.

Measures of Danish consumer confidence have languished in deeply negative territory ever since Russia invaded Ukraine.

“It’s a conundrum,” says Helge Pedersen, the chief economist at Nordea Bank. “House prices are at a record high, there’s almost no unemployment – everything is good but consumers are depressed.”

He says ordinary Danes are still preoccupied with inflation, even though the official rate is just 0.8pc.

“People are still worried about inflation, in particular food prices. We all discuss meat prices, they’re so high, coffee prices, chocolate prices. But that’s perceived inflation, which is higher than reality.”

Frederiksen and her centre-Left coalition partners have already started delving into the public coffers to soothe voters’ disgruntlement.

The Danish government recently cut the tax on electricity bills to almost zero. It also increased spending on childcare and welfare support and removed taxes on coffee, chocolate and sugar.

It has plenty of fiscal firepower. It was running a budget surplus estimated at more than 2pc of GDP in 2025 and its debt-to-GDP ratio was 30pc last year, one third that of Britain.

That allowed Denmark to ramp up defence spending last year by more than $4bn, which – along with higher healthcare expenditure – may prop the economy through any pharma slowdown.

The defence spending is a response to the Russia threat and also to pressure from Trump. He has called for Europe to fund its own defence and he is looking to wrestle at least de facto control over the Danish territory of Greenland.

Hansen says the Greenland issue may also have cast a pall over consumer confidence.

“Danish people are afraid that the new world order that is initiated by Mr Trump is something we have not dealt with since World War Two. That is really what is scaring the Danes,” he says.

Paradoxically, though, it may be helping the tough-talking and experienced Frederiksen. Since Trump’s Greenland manoeuvring became more explicit and tangible, her Social Democratic Party’s previously fading popularity has rebounded.

Geopolitical uncertainty and cost of living pressures are common across Europe. But in Denmark, there could be another more idiosyncratic source of economic anxiety.

Around 10pc of Danes hold shares in Novo. Many more will be exposed to its precipitous share-price decline via their private pensions.

Hansen, though, says Danish investors have been strangely patriotic: as foreigners have fled, Danes have been buying up the stock.

“The number of Danish private clients with us that hold one or more Novo Nordisk shares keeps climbing,” he says. “People still think there is a difference between what Novo Nordisk will achieve longer term and its current valuation in the stock market.”

Denmark’s economists, like its investors, also seem relatively sanguine about their country’s economic champion. Olsen says he was more worried during Novo’s breakneck growth phase than he is now.

“The fear was that it might erode the competitiveness of the rest of the economy. The large amount of hiring at Novo Nordisk could have led to very high base growth, that would then leave the rest of the economy uncompetitive if something was to go wrong,” he says.

“But what we have seen so far is that manufacturing wages growth in Denmark has, if anything, been lower over the last four or five years than we see in competitor countries. There is a competitive economy outside of Novo Nordisk.”

Pedersen agrees that the Danish economy is diversified enough to weather the Novo storm: “But of course you should keep an eye on it because the numbers are still so big on the macro level.

“Politicians do need to be aware that we have two Denmarks – one with and one without the pharmaceutical industry.”

Right now, the politician with the most riding on the Danish pharmaceuticals industry is Frederiksen.

As she called the election, she talked mostly about Greenland and wealth taxes.

Novo Nordisk’s decline might have garnered global attention with the company no longer deemed Denmark’s greatest success story.

Yet for Frederiksen, the hope is that this will simply be background noise as she bids for a third straight election victory – instead of a shot in the arm for her political rivals.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Miliband urged to back Shetland gas fields that could fuel UK for five years
data.description:
Britain should move to tap five years’ worth of gas reserves lying near the Shetland Islands, a North Sea energy company has said.

Serica Energy says an estimated five trillion cubic feet of gas lies under the seabed west of Shetland, where there is already infrastructure for piping it back to the mainland.

The claim will pile fresh pressure on Ed Miliband to allow drilling in the North Sea. The Energy Secretary has faced mounting calls to rethink his position on oil and gas amid growing warnings of looming gas shortages linked to the Iran war.

Chris Cox, Serica’s chief executive, said: “Of course [five trillion cubic feet of gas], that sounds like a big number, and it is.

“In fact, it’s equivalent to supplying every household in the UK for five years. And yet, some people continue to say that the amount of gas we can produce in the UK is not significant.”

Serica, which published its 2025 results on Thursday, said its data showed there were many other potential new gas fields in the area.

It follows Wednesday’s Commons clash between Kemi Badenoch and Sir Keir Starmer over Labour’s blocking of two new North Sea oil and gas fields.

Mr Miliband has so far resisted granting approval for plans to exploit the Rosebank field west of Shetland, and Jackdaw in the North Sea. Both are in the final stages of development and could be pouring gas into UK pipes within months.

The fields cited by Serica lie in the same area west of Shetland as Rosebank, some with licences and some without. The latter would fall foul of Mr Miliband’s ban on new exploration and licencing.

Mr Cox said the gas deposits west of Shetland were potentially huge.

“We are very excited by the overall potential,” he said.

Serica has acquired some of the rights while others are held by rival companies such as Adura and Ithaca, whom Mr Cox described as equally “bullish” about the drilling prospects in the area.

Their key advantage is that they lie close enough to existing platforms and pipelines to be developed quickly, meaning they could be in operation within a couple of years. Fuel shortages and price rises caused by the Iran conflict are now predicted to last at least that long.

David Latin, Serica’s chairman, said the Iran conflict showed the need for a rethink by Labour. He called on Mr Miliband to accelerate approvals for licenced fields, lift the ban on new licences and scrap the windfall tax.

He said: “There is significant untapped oil and gas potential on the UK continental shelf … maximising the benefits available to the UK from domestic oil and gas, and achieving net zero by 2050 are not mutually exclusive objectives.

“Indeed, they complement each other, not least when oil and gas imported over thousands of miles typically comes with significantly higher emissions than the equivalent domestic production.”

A government spokesperson argued that the best way for the UK to protect itself was to reduce fossil fuel use, adding: “Issuing new licences to explore new fields cannot give us energy security and will not take a penny off bills.”

Mr Cox warned that the Labour government’s decision to extend the windfall tax by two years to 2030 had hit it hard – leaving the company with an effective overall tax rate of 165pc.

The extension of the windfall tax forced Serica to set aside $130m (£97m), and turned a $80m pre-tax profit into a post tax loss of $52m.

Serica was founded in 2004 and aimed at buying up older fields from larger companies. It is now one of the biggest oil and gas producers in the UK.

Last year its 11 fields produced the equivalent of 118m barrels of oil – half of which was gas that fed into UK pipes.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Labour risks ‘banning’ Saturday jobs, warns M&S
data.description:
The boss of Marks & Spencer has warned Labour it risked destroying Saturday jobs by overhauling workers’ rights.

Stuart Machin suggested that the introduction of new rules to guarantee weekly staff hours risked backfiring by preventing employers from hiring part-time weekend workers.

This would prove particularly problematic for those trying to take their “first step into work”, Mr Machin said.

Writing in a blog post for Alan Milburn, Sir Keir Starmer’s employment tsar, Mr Machin said: “New regulations dissuade employers from offering a shift or two a week, and the red tape around work experience often makes it prohibitive to offer.

“We’re at risk of not only disincentivising the Saturday job, but banning it.”

The warning comes as companies brace for a new wave of red tape under Labour’s sweeping workers’ rights shake-up.

Although the Employment Rights Bill received royal assent late last year, various issues are still under negotiation, and are expected to be finalised in secondary legislation.

This includes rules around guaranteed work for those on “low” and “zero-hours” contracts.

Union bosses have been urging Sir Keir to hand all workers the right to guaranteed hours.

However, retailers have raised concerns that this will make it harder to give part-time workers extra shifts.

Lord Wolfson of Aspley Guise, the boss of Next, which employs 20,000 part-time staff, has previously said this change would leave shops “chronically overstaffed”.

He said it would force retailers to employ temporary staff to cover seasonal demand, such as the busy Christmas period, rather than offer more shifts to part-time workers who typically pick up work on Saturdays or Sundays.

This would “deprive loyal and skilled employees of income at times when they need it”, Lord Wolfson said.

Speaking on Thursday, the Next boss also raised concerns about Labour’s plans to speed up minimum wage increases for younger staff.

In its manifesto before the election, Labour pledged to pay young people the same minimum wage as older workers.

Lord Wolfson said: “It doesn’t feel like the right time. With everything that we’ve been saying about entry-level jobs being difficult, it doesn’t feel like the right time – even if it’s the right thing to do long term.”

Youth unemployment recently jumped to an 11-year high, with at least 16.1pc of 16 to 24-year-olds seeking work not yet in a job, according to the Office for National Statistics.

Increases in the minimum wage have led to almost a third of businesses reducing entry-level roles, with half looking to hire more older workers instead, according to an study from accounting firm RSM on Friday.

Mr Machin called for the Government to focus on the importance of retail to boost employment.

He said: “Retail and hospitality have been left out of most visions for economic growth. Politicians momentarily become interested in the ‘everyday economy’, but they quickly become distracted by flashier industries.

“That lack of interest is reflected in the range of cost pressures facing retail: business rates, packaging taxes, energy tariffs, national insurance rises. If we valued retail as the first rung on the jobs ladder we would surely take a different approach.”

It marks Mr Machin’s second political intervention in as many days as the retail boss also accused the Government on Thursday of driving up energy bills through a series of green levies.

Posting on LinkedIn, he said: “Over the last few years the ‘policy costs’ on our energy bill have skyrocketed.

“These are the tariffs that [the Government] place on our bills to fund their policies, and have nothing to do with the price of oil or gas. They now make up over half our bill. It’s just not sustainable for UK businesses.”
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: Energy bills bailout ‘could pay for itself’, says City
data.description:
An energy bailout for millions of households would pay for itself, economists have said.

Deutsche Bank said a support package worth £4bn would ease inflation by holding down gas and electricity bills.

Economists said this would result in lower interest rates, which in turn would save the Government billions of pounds in reduced borrowing costs.

The intervention is likely to raise pressure on Rachel Reeves, the Chancellor, after she has so far resisted calls to roll out a wide-ranging energy-bills support package after criticising the cost of the Tories’ bailout in the wake of Russia’s invasion of Ukraine in 2022.

However, Deutsche Bank’s Sanjay Raja said any potential bailout could “pay for itself”.

“Fiscal support that would actively reduce CPI inflation could end up paying for itself – even adding to the fiscal headroom,” he said.

It comes as EDF and E.On Next, two of Britain’s biggest energy suppliers, warned that household gas and electricity bills could top £2,000 by autumn because of the war in Iran.

EDF suggests annualised bills will rise to £2,047 by October, up from its forecasts of £1,937 for July. E.On Next has similar figures, predicting £1,955 from July and £2,064 from October.

EDF also warned that prices could rise even further by October.

It said: “Our forecasts from July 2026 onwards have continued to rise week-on-week, driven by increases in wholesale prices arising from ongoing strikes in Iran and the Middle East.”

EDF and Eon collectively supply around seven million households in the UK.

The expected increase in bills is a direct consequence of the conflict in the Middle East, which has sent oil and gas prices soaring.

As well as higher energy bills, households have also been hit by soaring petrol and diesel prices.

Petrol now costs more than £1.50 per litre, up from below £1.33 at the end of February, according to the RAC. Diesel is up more than 35p at nearly £1.78 on average.

This has prompted calls for Ms Reeves to postpone the rise in fuel duty scheduled to take effect in September.

If the Chancellor freezes fuel duty and cuts £150 from household bills, this would ultimately cost the Government £4bn, said Mr Raja.

But it would also trim 0.4 percentage points off inflation. His calculations indicate this could be enough to take around 0.3 percentage points off interest rates in financial markets.

Combined with the boost to growth that would come from avoiding the blow from higher rates, Mr Raja suggests that the Government would have to borrow £5bn less than expected.

“In effect, paying for the entirety of fiscal support levied to reduce consumer price inflation,” he said.

The idea of government support lowering borrowing costs has also been raised by Zack Polanski.

The Green Party leader said this month that “the Government should guarantee right now that it will not allow energy bills to rise beyond the April-June price cap”, proposing an £8.4bn scheme.

He said: “If the price cap rises, we will see interest rates rise, mortgage rates up, bond yields up and inflation up.

“And we will be back into the doom loop that has done untold damage to our economy and caused misery for households across the UK for years now.”

Britain is particularly exposed to inflation from global markets because it is a net importer of energy.

Economists expect Britain to face a jump in inflation, which is potentially twice as big as that facing the eurozone.

A survey by the National Institute of Economic and Social Research found the average economist expected an increase in inflation of 0.8 to 1.1 percentage points in the UK, compared with 0.4 to 0.7 in the eurozone.
data.author: The telegraph

data.title: British factories brace for an energy crisis worse than the 1970s
data.description:
Europe’s fuel supplies could be squeezed within days, and governments need to act.

That is the ominous warning from Wael Sawan, the boss of Shell boss, who this week said “a ripple effect” could see the continent’s energy security hit “as we get into April”.

A growing number of governments across the world are already introducing exceptional measures to limit the amount of fuel people use as the world reels from the Middle East energy supply shock triggered by war in Iran.

South Korea’s residents have been told to drive less, take shorter showers and use washing machines and vacuum cleaners only on the weekend.

In Sri Lanka, the government has declared that public institutions should take Wednesday off. The island nation has also implemented fuel rationing, with drivers now limited to buying 15 litres of petrol or diesel a day for private cars.

Thailand, with its tropical climate, has called on workers to swap suits for t-shirts in an effort to reduce the use of air conditioning. Swimming pools are no longer being heated in Mauritius and fountains have been turned off at the government’s direction.

All that may seem like a distant concern – something affecting far-off lands with vastly different economies and climates to ours.

Yet restrictions are already beginning to hit Europe. Slovenia became the first EU country this week to introduce fuel rationing, limiting drivers to 50 litres a day.

Here in the UK, ministers insist fuel supplies are secure and are urging people to go about their days as normal. Yet if this crisis drags on, that may not last.

The Telegraph has reported that strict speed limits could be imposed under the National Emergency Plan for Fuel, should ministers choose to activate it.

Adam Bell, a former government energy official at consultancy Stonehaven, said home-working guidance may also be likely if the Government decides to bring in measures to curb energy consumption in the coming months.

“If you are going to do anything in the short term, it will be that,” he says.

“If you do not have enough fuel, governments have to think very carefully about how they use the fuel they have, what is still coming in, and what they hold in inventories,” says Callum MacPherson, the head of commodities at Investec.

Ashley Kelty, of investment bank Panmure Liberum, said limits on the amount of gas factories can use may also be considered.

Even if the Government doesn’t step in, manufacturers may adopt self-imposed limits as costs skyrocket.

The average annual electricity bill for small industrial and commercial businesses has risen by 20pc, or £96,000, to £578,000 since the war in Iran began, figures from Cornwall Insight show.

For those businesses that use gas – including chemical producers, glass and ceramics factories and metalworks – the pain is even worse. Average annual gas bills have risen by nearly 60pc to just more than £1m.

“Many of these companies are already battling slimmer margins, so this rise in energy costs is not something they can simply absorb,” Jacob Briggs, from Cornwall Insight, says.

“For some firms, this could mean the difference between investing in growth this year or shelving their plans entirely, and for others, high bills could force some very difficult economic decisions.”

Arjan Geveke, the director of the Energy Intensive Users Group, told The Telegraph last week there was a “high risk” that factories would start voluntarily cutting production in the coming weeks because of high energy costs.

Kelty fears that the impact of the crisis on the UK economy could be “horrendous”, with the automotive and steel manufacturing sectors the worst affected.

He said: “Even if the rockets stop tomorrow, the problem is that it’s going to take a month to get back to any form of normality in terms of restarting oil production and getting all the tankers out of the Gulf and to their original destinations.”

The UK will face a “very challenging” winter, as even if the conflict comes to an end, it will struggle to refill its gas storage, Kelty added.

All this is set to weigh on the economy. If the energy crisis continues and Brent crude oil remains at around $100 a barrel, Capital Economics has forecast that the UK will enter a mild recession by the end of the year.

Jennifer McKeown, chief global economist from Capital Economics, said: “The energy-intensive sectors of industry, hotels and retail are likely to be hit the hardest.

“The sharp increase in the cost of energy is going to mean that it is quite likely that industrial production will need to be cut back.”

In the summer of 2022, as energy prices soared following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, many European nations imposed policies to curb oil and gas consumption.

Measures at the time included restrictions on air conditioning and reduced lighting for national monuments and advertising boards.

By late 2022, Britons were encouraged to cut energy use during peak times, with some electricity providers offering reduced costs in an effort to encourage households to shift consumption to off-peak hours.

Restrictions were far more draconian during the Arab oil crisis of the 1970s. In 1973, drivers were using fuel ration books and were advised to reduce their maximum speed to 50mph.

By early 1974, fuel shortages and rationing meant that many industrial businesses were operating with a three-day working week and TV channels were shut off after 10.30pm.

Fatih Birol, executive director of the International Energy Agency (IEA), has said that the current energy supply shock is worse than the 1970s. “No country will be immune to the effects of this crisis if it continues to go in this direction,” he has warned. “So there is a need for global efforts.”

The IEA has called for car-free Sundays globally and urged people to work from home three days a week in order to conserve fuel.

Ministers in Britain are understood to have found this intervention unhelpful. With the Government convinced the UK has enough fuel for the moment, their biggest concern is to avoid panic buying or unnecessary changes in business patterns that could create fresh problems.

However, disruption to energy supplies looks likely to continue. QatarEnergy, which operates the world’s largest liquefied natural gas facility, said on Tuesday that it was preparing to declare “force ‌majeure” on some fuel contracts.

The crisis measure would allow it to stop shipments agreed under contract without facing any legal liability. Italy, Belgium, South Korea ⁠and China will be affected.

MacPherson said: “If this went on and on, and we had to live in a world where there is much less oil than we have been used to in the past, people would have to travel less, and when they do travel, it would be more expensive.”

While Britain may have escaped the worst of it so far, the threat of an energy-triggered economic crunch looms.

data.title: The Observer’s high-stakes takeover leaves it flirting with disaster
data.description:
When The Observer unveiled its new digital subscription model in November, it promised readers a “bold new online presence”. The 234-year-old newspaper was finally being dragged into the internet age under the stewardship of Tortoise, a media start-up co-founded by former BBC News chief James Harding.

But this new “digital daily” era has got off to a challenging start. The company had just 3,500 online subscribers as of January, according to figures provided to investors.

The Observer also had a print circulation of around 114,000 at the time of the sale, but the figures suggest Harding has significant work to do to reach his target of 173,000 paying subscribers by 2029.

Now, just under a year after its controversial sale, The Observer is bracing for sweeping redundancies and facing searching questions about its prospects in the digital age.

Inside The Observer, scepticism is growing. One insider branded the digital strategy a “disaster”, while another journalist claimed Harding “doesn’t seem to have a plan”.

Tortoise, which launched in 2018, is part of the “slow” journalism movement, positioning itself as an antidote to fast-paced breaking news. It instead places greater emphasis on more considered opinion and analysis, with a particular focus on culture and the arts.

The loss-making startup has imported this philosophy to The Observer, which it bought from The Guardian last April after a long and tortuous process.

Journalists staged a four-day strike over the deal, fearing for the financial sustainability of the newspaper under its new owners and raising concerns that it would effectively be transformed into a weekly magazine.

The slow news strategy is popular among those fatigued by the modern news cycle and The Observer’s print sales are understood to have held up well.

Online, however, the model is coming under pressure. With war in Iran raging, fast-paced news is back in vogue. As referrals from Google’s search engine decline, all publishers have been forced to rethink their digital strategies.

Observer executives hope that the title can win subscribers through its brand of long-form investigative journalism, including a growing stable of podcasts.

But as AI overviews and shifting social media algorithms trigger a precipitous fall in traffic, outlets across the industry are having to work harder to reach audiences, raising questions over how effective The Observer’s news-avoidant strategy will be.

It is perhaps telling that the newspaper’s biggest success to date under its new ownership was an investigation into alleged fabrications in Raynor Winn’s hit memoir The Salt Path – an old-fashioned scoop.

But precious little in that mould has followed since. An apparent lack of direction is now taking its toll, especially among the rank-and-file staff on the ground floor of the newspaper’s Fitzrovia offices.

In a major shake-up of the fledgling operation, all permanent staff were last week offered voluntary redundancy. For at least one employee, this quickly became compulsory. Overall, around 25 people are poised to leave the business, or 17pc of the company’s workforce.

Multiple sources said the redundancies had mostly fallen to former Tortoise staff members and were predominantly being taken by young women in their 20s and 30s. “It’s going to leave the paper paler, staler and maler,” says one insider.

Observer executives argue that they are attempting to implement difficult changes in a compassionate way. Voluntary redundancy is understood to have been offered on generous terms. They insist that the shake-up is needed to prepare the newspaper for its new iteration in the digital age.

But this has failed to mollify staff, who were told there would be no job losses as a result of the sale.

Bosses sparked anger this week after using the word “respectfully” in an all-hands meeting about the cuts. “I’m not sure giving people less than a week to make potentially life-changing decisions is respectful,” says one journalist.

Another adds: “I think now they’ve got their list of people who would like to go, they’re going to go to each department and try and juggle people around and see who else they can lose.”

A third source says Tortoise is “not good at redundancies”, adding that the process would be “quite brutal”. Journalists were summoned to a further meeting at 5pm on Friday to be offered reassurances about the shake-up.

Stepping down

Making matters worse are rumours that Harding and other senior figures spent “six figures” at the World Economic Forum in Davos in January, where they were courting luxury advertisers. Insiders dispute this figure.

This is not the first bout of upheaval at The Observer since it divorced from The Guardian. Lucy Rock, who was named as the newspaper’s first female editor following the takeover, is understood to have stepped back after just weeks in the role before officially departing after a year.

Despite being given the ostensible title of editor, Rock’s role was restricted to the print newspaper and she worked alongside Basia Cummings, the digital editor. Both reported to Harding, the editor-in-chief.

Guy Edmunds, the newspaper’s director of advertising, also stepped down after only a few days in the job.

Harding, who was editor of The Times before joining the BBC, is now further tightening his grip, telling staff he will take a more hands-on role as editor. Meanwhile, Rich Furness, co-chief executive, has been shifted into a modified role.

After taking control of The Observer, Harding pledged to invest £25m over five years and break even in 2027. He is now having to demonstrate to his backers, who include Gary Lubner, a South African businessman and Labour donor, and the billionaire dynasty behind the Nando’s chicken chain, that his strategy is working.

One investor starting to question The Observer’s prospects is The Guardian, which, in addition to pushing through the sale in the face of staff revolt, also invested £5m in Tortoise through its owner, the Scott Trust.

The Observer’s travails will put particular pressure on Anna Bateson, The Guardian’s chief executive, who came under scrutiny for her ties to Harding during the sale process. The pair holidayed together on a £15m superyacht owned by TalkTalk tycoon Sir Charles Dunstone, as well as in the French ski resort of Val d’Isère.

At the time, The Guardian said Bateson had declared her friendship with Harding. Nevertheless, the links drew accusations of a “sweetheart” deal, particularly as The Observer was not put out to competitive bids.

Now, the apparent lack of progress in Harding’s strategy is raising awkward questions for Bateson about the wisdom of the deal.

Meanwhile, there is confusion about whether The Guardian will be forced to fund the redundancies under the terms of the sale agreement. A Guardian spokesman denied this.

It is an unwanted headache for The Guardian, which has its own problems. The paper is battling fresh allegations of anti-Semitism following a recent opinion piece that downplayed repeated acts of vandalism of a branch of Gail’s in north London.

Gail’s, which was founded by an Israeli baker in the 1990s, has been targeted by pro-Palestinian activists because its largest shareholder is Bain Capital, a private equity firm that also invests in Israeli defence and cybersecurity firms. A column by Jonathan Liew described vandalism of the Archway cafe as “small acts of petty symbolism”

The saga rekindled scrutiny of Guardian editor Katharine Viner, who has been accused of failing to stamp out anti-Semitism in the newspaper’s ranks.

The Guardian is also seeking to adapt to sweeping changes in online journalism. The newspaper, which itself launched a voluntary redundancy programme in 2024, has sought to reduce its reliance on advertising by generating more money directly from readers through donations, as well as increasing revenues from outside the UK.

The media group lost around £25m in the year to March 2025, though this was down from £37m the previous year.

The Guardian has created new global editor roles in the UK, US and Australia aimed at bridging the gap between its regional outposts and expanding its heft as an international media organisation.

Like many publishers, it is also betting on personality-led video journalism, which is considered an antidote to the decline of online search. Last month, it appointed Leah Green, a former Channel 4 commissioning executive, as the head of its newly-formed video division, Guardian Studios.

But insiders say the pace of change under this new strategy, dubbed Project Berger, is glacial, with video teams struggling to keep up with the fast-moving news agenda. “They have big plans for Berger but made it clear they don’t have the resources,” says one journalist.

A year after parting ways, both The Guardian and The Observer are under pressure to carve out their places in an uncertain digital future – and prove the many critics of the sale wrong.

Richard Furness, co-chief executive of The Observer, said: “Our paying customers across print, digital and audio have grown over 25pc since we took over in April 2025.

“In that time we’ve transformed The Observer’s audience from a declining UK weekly print readership to over four million people a week in print and digital, and growing.

“We have invested in both the production and the journalism of our print edition, and have constantly increased our share of the Sunday market, and on multiple weeks have overtaken The Sunday Telegraph in terms of sales.

“In just under a year we are incredibly proud of what we have all achieved at The Observer.”
data.author: The telegraph
